cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to the Recut Edition of the Four Houses Commented Sayings on the Book of Serenity
cb:div.head: Preface to the Recut Edition of the Four Houses Recorded Sayings on the Book of Serenity
cb:div.p:
Ever since the Buddha held up a flower and Kāśyapa faintly smiled, though it is said the family style was revealed in silence, one still feels
that too much was let out in a muddled flood. Then later, meddlesome descendants came along, not knowing to cherish the family
treasure; each one staked body and life, raising dust on level ground and stirring waves on clear waters, chaotically
scattering flowers in empty space, deceiving and bewildering eyes, passing errors from mouth to mouth. Public cases multiplied in many forms. Tiantong and
Xuedou's leftover spittle was already bitter enough and not gathered back in; Yuanwu and Wansong's dream-talk was again too much of it
was not awake. Fortunately, the old man Qingliang long understood the calamity of not settling the ancestors' unfinished business. Today
his disciples, with ancient countenances, have brought out all the old curios, divided them into four houses, and newly recut
the blocks. Alas, stolen goods and private hoards are now in plain sight; the true thieves cannot escape. I dare ask for the vajra jewel-sword
to cut it all off at one stroke. Then take these fragmentary commentarial words and, together with the people of the four seas,
let them make a song of peace.
Inscribed by Luo Rufang of Jinqi Creek in the Southern City
Written by Shen Xian of Changzhou in mid-autumn of the dingwei year

cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to the Recut Edition of the Four Houses Commentaries and Verse-Chanting
cb:div.head: Preface to the Recut Edition of the Four Houses Commentaries and Verse-Chanting
cb:div.p:
Since the Buddha and patriarchs held up the flower and Kāśyapa faintly smiled, the sharp workings of the teaching have shifted like clouds, and the lineage meaning is deep and
still. This is a separate transmission outside the teachings; the true meaning within it is hardly something human language can
describe even one ten-thousandth part of. Yet for opening later students, expounding Dharma, and benefiting beings, this is especially apt
for meeting capacities and leading them into awakening. The old carved edition of the Four Houses sayings was not very skillfully engraved.
Its format was cramped and crude, and the comments and notes were constricted, to the distress of readers. Yet it was already like ascending a great
a jewel mountain and entering the Huazang sea, groping with all one’s strength and never begrudging the effort. In the imperial capital, Chan elders
often waved their fly-whisks and discoursed on the school, yet recently things have grown blurred and hard to distinguish. The Dharma lecturer
Juexu, arousing great vast compassion, wished to commission the block-carvers anew, but his strength did not suffice, so he consulted
me. I truly had long cherished this wish, so how could I not give my full effort? Thus workers were gathered and three parts were carved.
Like-minded monks and laymen carved the remaining one. Then the old devices of pointed thrusts, staff-shouts, raised brows,
and lifted fingers became all the more clear and penetrating in their leaks and openings. Then Master Jue’s intention was fulfilled, and
the merit too is boundless. I only fear that I am unable to be of service in this task; what further deliberation is there
to be had?

On an auspicious dawn in the chrysanthemum month of the dingwei year of Wanli, by Hua-
ting Xu Lin, Prefect of Chuxiong Prefecture in Yunnan.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to Elder Wansong’s Commentarial Chanting on Tiantong Jue’s Verses on Ancient Cases, the Congrong Hermitage Record
cb:div.head:
Elder Wansong’s Commentarial Chanting on Tiantong Jue’s Verses on Ancient Cases
Preface to the Congrong Hermitage Record
cb:div.p:
In former days when I was in the capital, there were many elder Chan masters. Only the Reverend Chenggong of Sheng'an
had a grave and lucid bearing, and his words were open and forthright. I alone esteemed him deeply, and so I often visited him concerning the ancestral
Way. Again and again I questioned him with what I had gained from the sayings and encounter-cases of revered elders of old. Master Chenggong
would sometimes approve of them, and I too thought I had attained something. But since meeting with sorrow and hardship,
I put away the mind that sought rank and fame on a high shelf, and my search for the ancestral Way grew ever more urgent. So again I took up the former
matters and asked Sheng'an about them. Sheng'an reversed the old judgments and no longer saw them that way. I was greatly perplexed.
Then Sheng'an calmly said to me: In the past, you held an important post, and moreover Confucian scholars
often did not truly trust Buddhist books, but only picked through sayings and cases to furnish topics for talk. Therefore I
did not dare apply the hammer and drill too harshly. But now, judging your mind, you truly ask for the sake of the fundamental matter,
in questioning me. How could I still repeat my former fault and not speak bitter medicine plainly? I am old
now. I have never been versed in Confucian learning and cannot teach you. There is an elder called Wansong, who in both Confucian and Buddhist learning
is fully equipped, thoroughly versed in both the school and its teachings, and unhindered in eloquence. You may go see him. I
after I had paid respects to Wansong, cut off all visitors and set aside household affairs. Though in bitter cold or great
heat, there was not a day without practice. Burning the lamp to continue past daylight, neglecting sleep and forgetting meals, for nearly three
years. By mistake I received the Dharma's grace and unworthily bore the seal of a disciple, and as the layman Zhanran Congyuan
he regarded me. In the course of training under him, his devices and sharp points were unfathomable, his transformations inexhaustible. Lofty,
towering like a ten-thousand-fathom peak none could climb or gaze up to; surging and vast like ten-thousand-acre waves
with no one able to reach their shore. Looking at him, he was before me, then suddenly behind me. Turning back to what I had formerly
studied, it was all but clods and gravel. Ah, to climb East Mountain and find Lu small, to climb Mount Tai and find all under heaven small,
are these empty words? Those who have not yet entered the inner precinct, on hearing such words, will surely
say that I have forgotten my roots and delight in the strange. Only Pingshan Xianxian will perhaps understand one another with me.
After that I was ordered to go to the imperial camp and accompanied the western campaign. Separated from my teacher, I knew not
how many thousands of li lay between us. The teacher's past Dharma sayings and verses were all collected by our Dharma elder brother, Master Long.
He had gathered them, but now I could no longer obtain them. In our lineage there is Tiantong, who composed one hundred verses on ancient cases,󱡐
acclaimed as peerless songs. I earnestly begged Wansong to comment on and sing through these verses to open up later
students. In all, through nine letters back and forth, after hardships over seven years, I finally received the favor of having it sent to me. I in the western regions, lonely and
adrift for several years, suddenly received this book, and it was like sobering from drunkenness, like reviving from death. Leaping with joy,
I cried out in delight, bowed toward the east, read and reread it again and again, stroked the scroll and sighed: Wansong
has come to the western regions. Every phrase and every single character has its point and destination, its knot and opening, its eye.
It stands above present and past alike, sufficient to serve as a model for ten thousand ages. If not one who can guide humans and gods and wield the
balance of creation and transformation, who could have part in this? I and several friends in the traveling palace, morning
and evening, swam and wandered in this book, as if climbing a great treasure mountain and entering the Flower Treasury Sea, where rare
and wondrous things, vast and complete, were all present. To the left we encountered them, to the right we met them; the eyes were enriched and the heart filled. How
could worldly language describe even one ten-thousandth of it? I dared not keep its beauty
for myself alone, and wished to share it with all under heaven. In the capital there is only my Dharma younger brother Congxiang, who with
me has a friendship beyond age. I respectfully sent him a letter, asking that it be carved and published in the world, to bequeath it to those who come later.
Thus I wrote this preface, saying: The Buddhas, patriarchs, and all the masters buried their roots a thousand fathoms deep; in one hundred encounter-cases there appeared
seedlings born into the world. Tiantong should not have drawn out branches, and how could Wansong bear to pull out vines? Zhanran
upon those branches and vines further added thorny cords, piercing through the nostrils of those who chase fragrance and follow scents.
He trips the heels of those who walk mystery and embody subtlety. If you want your heels to touch the ground
while your nostrils stir the heavens, then you must pass through this tangle of vines before it can be done. In the jiashen year,
on the Zhongyuan day, Yila Chucai Jinqing of Qishui wrote this preface in the western regions, at Ali
ma City.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Letter Sending the Commentarial Chanting on Tiantong’s Congrong Hermitage Record to Layman Zhanran
cb:div.head: A Letter Sending the Commentarial Chanting on Tiantong’s Congrong Hermitage Record to Layman Zhanran
cb:div.p:
In our tradition there are Xuedou and Tiantong, just as in Confucius' school there were Ziyou and Zixia. Of these two masters'
verse comments on old cases, they are like Li Bai and Du Fu in the poetry world. The world says Xuedou had the talent of a Hanlin scholar.
Yet this picks my blossoms and does not gather my fruit. It is also said that if one does not travel ten thousand li, and does not
read ten thousand scrolls, one should not read the Minister of Works' poetry, speaking of its breadth and richness. As for comparing this to Tian-
tong Laoshi's verse comments on old cases, every phrase and every single word flows forth from the deep source of the Buddhas and patriarchs.
Students cannot fathom them. The Bai-shan Dayin Collection sets forth his deeds, but among them there are some things sparse,
broad, and unlike the truth. As for the bringing up and presenting of cases, it is careless and brief, relying only on headings. Wansong
once tried to comment and sing them; since the time of war and arms, this ancestral work was abandoned. Lately, having retired to Yan-
jing at Bao'en, I then built a little snail-shell hut and named it the Congrong Hermitage. Planning to complete the old undertaking, just then
I met the layman Zhanran, who urged and requested that it be finished. My old eyes are dim and blurred, so much was dictated by mouth.
Disciples took it down in writing. In it are abundantly recorded causes, conditions, and biographical incidents: first, to honor Tiantong's
vast learning and surging waves, and his skillful aptness in fitting things together; second, to spare students the labor of investigation and review.
Third, to show that Wansong transmits and does not invent, and that this is not arbitrary judgment. I privately compare it to Foguo's
Blue Cliff Record, in that every section has an address to the assembly in full. I privately compare it to Yuantong Jue's
Record of the Sea, in that sentence by sentence it has never been rambling or disjointed, but complete. As for added sayings and critical eye-
points, and the moments of writing and trimming, I likewise did not yield when facing the occasion. At the end of the renwu year, Layman Zhanran
sent a letter, firmly insisting it be brought forth. So I could not avoid airing family ugliness abroad, burdening me and burdening you.
On the Shangsi Day of the guiwei year, the rustic old man Wansong sends this along by the wind and so dispatches it, without further elaboration.

cb:juan.cb:jhead:
Elder Wansong’s Commentarial Chanting on Tiantong Jue’s Verses on Ancient Cases
Congrong Hermitage Record One

body.byline: Collated by the Later Student Xingyi

cb:div.cb:mulu: 1. The World-Honored One Ascends the Seat
cb:div.head: Case One: The World-Honored One Ascends the Seat
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Shutting the door and sleeping receives those of the highest capacity. Again and again I hold up the mirror, bending myself for
the middling and lower. How could I bear to carve a crooked piece of wood into a ghost-eyed face? If there is one at the side who does not consent,
let him come forth. He cannot be blamed for it either.

Raising the case: One day the World-Honored One ascended the seat. Mañjuśrī struck the gavel and said, Clearly contemplateIf you do not hit it today, then
the Dharma of the Dharma King; the Dharma of the Dharma King is thus. The World-Honored One then got down from the seat.how will you know what his mind is doingdiscuss it again another day
Measure

The Master said: Perfectly encompassing the ten epithets, alone honored in the world, shaking out his eyebrows, lifting high his nose—
in the lecture halls this is called ascending the seat; in Chan monasteries it is called ascending the hall. Before you all had reached
the Dharma hall, before Wansong had come out of the abbot’s quarters, if you recognized it at that time, you had already fallen into three
and four. Did you not see Xuedou say: If among the crowd there were a true adept,
why would Mañjuśrī have needed to strike the gavel? Examining it closely, Xuedou should not have asked for salt, and Wan-
song still less should have offered a horse. Even if the seven Buddhas and patriarchs were to say, Clearly contemplate the Dharma of the Dharma King,
the Dharma of the Dharma King is thus, still you must pull the nail from the eye and draw out the wedge from behind the skull before it will do.
To this day, at the end of opening the hall, the gavel-strike says, Clearly contemplate the Dharma of the Dharma King, the Dharma of the Dharma King
is thus, following this precedent. The World-Honored One then stepped down, and thus saved half of it.
The other half he entrusted to Tiantong, who versified it, saying:

lg.l:
A single stretch of true wind can you see it? unbroken, the transforming mother tends the loom-Do not let it blow into the eye; once in, it is especially hard to get out
shuttleUneven and missed, the strands get tangled

weaving ancient brocade, holding the image of spring yet helpless before the East Lord’s leaking outGreat skill seems clumsy
what can be done?Yin and yang do not bend to favoritism; the seasons spare no one

cb:div.p:
The Master said: Tiantong says, A single stretch of true wind, can you see it? Is the World-Honored One’s ascending
the seat a single stretch of true wind, or is Tiantong’s raising it in verse a single stretch of true wind?
Or is Wansong’s asking for instruction a single stretch of true wind? If so, then it has become three stretches
already. What is the single stretch of true wind? Moreover, each of you has a share in it, so you may well
investigate it carefully. He also says, Unbroken, the transforming mother tends the loom-shuttle. The transforming mother is another name for the transforming artisan who makes
things. The Confucian and Daoist teachings take one qi as their source. The Buddhist stream takes as its basis
the one mind. Guifeng said: Original qi too is made by mind and all is included within the objective aspect of ālaya-
consciousness. Wansong says: This is the true lineage of Caodong, the life-pulse of patriarchs and Buddhas.
The pivotal knot bites at the hub-mouth; where it turns is subtle and hidden. Fine thread is spit from the shuttle’s belly.
In use it is close-woven and exact. How could it be spoken of in the same day as false causes and causelessness? Then, turning to
the later lines, he versifies the World-Honored One’s rich reserve, saying: Woven into ancient brocade, it contains the image of spring. Although
it is like insects boring wood and by chance making patterns, still what can one do? Build the cart behind closed doors, and when it goes out the gate
it fits the ruts. At the end Mañjuśrī overturned it for him, yet says: Helpless before the East Lord’s leaking out—
what can be done? Mañjuśrī strikes the gavel and the World-Honored One immediately descends the seat. But when Kāśyapa strikes the gavel, then
hundreds of thousands of Mañjuśrīs appear. Since it is equally such a moment, why is it that
the gathering in and letting go differ? Tell me: where is the East Lord’s point of leakage? Earnestly, for
untie the clove-knot and let the carefree spring at the branch-tips come forth.

cb:div.head: Case Two: Bodhidharma Vast and Empty
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Bian He offered the jade three times and still could not avoid punishment. If one tosses the night-shining jewel to others, few do not
reach for the sword. A foot-soldier guest has no foot-soldier host; the false fits, the true does not. Even if it is a rare and precious jewel, it is of no use.
I bring out a dead cat’s head. Look.

Raising the case: Emperor Wu of Liang asked Great Master Bodhidharma, What is the highest meaning of the holy truth?At dawn on rising, there was no profitable opening
Bodhidharma said, Vast emptiness, no holiness. The emperor said,For now, ask the second oneSplit the belly and gouge out the heart
Who stands before me? Bodhidharma said, I do not know. The emperor did not accord, so Bodhidharma crossed the river to Shaolin and faced the wall for nine years.Recognize the teeth inside the nostrilsSee the cheeks from behind the headA square peg will not go in
a round holeA household with no stagnant goods does not grow rich

The master said: Prajnatara once instructed Great Master Bodhidharma, saying, After my passing, in sixty-
seven years you should go to China and set forth the great Dharma medicine, directly pointing to those of highest capacity. Be careful not to
go too quickly, lest it decline before the sun sets. And when you arrive, do not stay in the South. There they only delight in
conditioned merit and do not see the Buddha-principle. Even if you do reach there, you must not remain long. Indeed,
there was the matter of roaming Liang and crossing into Wei, lingering dully for nine years. In recent times, in Cizhou, the robe and Dharma were entrusted to
Renshan. Shan said, I am not such a man. Zhou said, If you are not such a
man, then it will not bring harm upon you. Because Shan's feeling for the milk of the Dharma was deep, he bowed and accepted it. Zhou
then said again, Since you are thus, first of all you must not too easily come forth into the world. If you hasten ahead rashly
and act lightly, there will surely be obstacles in between. This and Prajnatara's repeated charge to Bodhidharma about the nine
years are one and the same in spirit. Zhexi's verse says: Do not begrudge passing through autumn frost, for the sake of making
the flavor last long. Even if picked while still raw, in the end it will not be fragrant. This can serve as a warning for those who come
later. But if one is a genuine person of the Way, whether to emerge or withdraw, one knows the right time oneself. Although Emperor Wu
did not accord, he set forth a line of questioning, which was not without sharpness. To this day, in monasteries everywhere, when opening the hall
and striking the gavel, it is still said: O dragons and elephants in this Dharma assembly, you should contemplate the First Principle. But as for the First
Principle truth, is it really something that permits contemplation? Does it permit the question and answer of Emperor Wu and Bodhidharma? Wan-
song says: Leave the First Principle aside for the moment; what do you want with holy truth? Tianhuang said: Simply
exhaust ordinary feelings, and there is no other holy understanding. The Shurangama says: If you make a holy understanding of it, you at once fall prey to a host of
false views. Bodhidharma's saying, Vast emptiness, nothing holy, is exactly this. In the flash of flint and lightning, one may well
handle it directly and settle it before one's own eyes. Emperor Wu's stubborn drool would not recede, and he asked further, Who is facing me?
On the Liang king's side, this too was well-intentioned. But he did not know that from Bodhidharma's side, it was as if he had been spat on full in the face.
So Bodhidharma could not avoid offering another answer: I do not know. Already delicate flowers fade easily;
how much more so when frost is added atop snow. When Bodhidharma saw his eyes fixed and stirring, he immediately
turned around and took another road. The people of old, whether coming forth or remaining hidden, whether silent or speaking, all did so
for the Buddha's work. Later, Emperor Wu truly did come to long for the noble one after the fact, and he personally composed a stele inscription
which says: Seeing him, I did not see him. Meeting him, I did not meet him. Now and in the past, I regret it and resent it,
I do. Though I am but an ordinary man, I dare to take him as my teacher hereafter. Since Emperor Wu suffered disgrace and flight,
and since Bodhidharma returned west, no one has raised up the First Principle truth. Fortunately,
there is Tiantong, who brings it forth for the assembly. The verse says:

lg.l:
Vast emptiness, no holiness the incoming chance goes by a far road is it not like strikingOne time drinking water, one time chokingA red face is not as good as straight speech
at the nose while swinging the axe?In a master’s hands, a master is praised

Missing, he does not turn his head after the pot falls vast and still,Let bygones be bygones
coldly he sits at Shaolin.Old yet the mind does not rest

Silently, silently, he fully upholds the true command autumn clear,still talking of military strategy
the moon turns like a wheel of frost.Look with high-set eyes

The river runs pale, the Dipper hangs low, the night-handle steady and exact robe and bowlWho dares take it on
are entrusted to the descendants.Do not indulge delusion
From here on, among humans and gods, medicine and sickness are made.Heaven’s course has already passed; the envoy must know

cb:div.p:
The master said: Vast and empty, nothing holy, the incoming faculties are far apart. This phrase originally comes from Zhuangzi. Greatly
divergent, it does not accord with ordinary human feeling. At that time the First Patriarch also used but very little expedient means.
He did not realize that if the medicine does not daze and confound, the illness will not be cured. From the start he brought down a thunderbolt
hand. But now people have long since indulged in private leniency and forbearance. Thus they fail to be like one who swings the hatchet by the nose
and wields the axe. Zhuangzi, escorting a funeral, passed by Huizi's tomb, turned and said to his attendants: A man of Ying
once had a dab of white plaster spread on the tip of his nose, as thin as a fly's wing, and had Carpenter Shi chop it off. Carpenter Shi swung his hatchet
like the wind. Listening and chopping, he closed his eyes and let his hand move freely, removing all the plaster without injuring the nose. The man of Ying
stood there without changing expression. Since you, Master, have died, I no longer have anyone to serve as my target.
Then there is the one who, when the pot fell, did not turn his head. In the Later Han, Meng Min was living away from home in Taiyuan and once carried a
earthen steamer, which fell to the ground. He did not look back and simply left. Guo Linzong saw this and asked his meaning. He replied:
The steamer is already broken. What benefit is there in looking at it? Linzong found him extraordinary for this, and so urged him to
travel and study. The meaning is that if Emperor Wu had assented on his own, Bodhidharma would never have bent himself to follow another.
If the king of Liang did not accord, he would flick his sleeves and leave at once without regret. In the golden palace he let loose
a faceless utterance, and only half was spoken. Nine years at Shaolin with his mouth hanging on the wall, only then was it
eighty percent complete. Like the clear autumn moon turning into a frosty wheel, he secretly used the Dharma-eye, until at last in the frosty night
the moon falls of itself into the stream before us, naturally and freely, illuminating the Way whose ultimate principle admits no comparison. The Milky Way grows pale, and the Dipper
handle hangs down in the night. Tiantong, ascending the hall, said: One point within the circle shines on the utmost subtlety. Where wisdom has no
function, there knowledge yet remains. When dependent thought is fully purified and nothing remains to be done, at midnight the river of stars and the Dipper's
handle hang down. These two lines are like a mute acting as an interpreter, trying to point it out to people yet unable to speak it forth.
If even that cannot be expressed, how much less can it bear teacher and disciple transmission, or medicine and illness treating one another? It turns into having no connection at all.
How can one fully uphold the true command and carry it through? Empty flowers waste who knows how many tortoise-hair threads, and the stone woman
vainly holds up a needle.Hey

cb:div.head: Case Three: East India Invites the Patriarch
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: The opportunity before signs appear in a kalpa before time—like a turtle facing fire. The separate transmission outside the teachings,
in a single phrase, makes flowers bloom on a pestle-mouth. Tell me, is there still any share here of receiving, upholding, and reciting scripture, or not?

Raising the case: The king of East India invited the Twenty-seventh Patriarch Prajñātāra to a royal feast.
The king asked, Why do you not read sutras? The Patriarch said, This poor monkAgain and again one goes paying off mouth-debtsReceiving pay without merit, one cannot sleep or eat at ease
on inhaling does not abide in the skandha-realms; on exhaling does not enter into the myriad conditions. I constantly turn such a
sutra, hundreds of thousands of myriads of millions of scrolls.Up to this point, the lecture and praise bring limitless excellent causes

The Master said: The Twenty-seventh Patriarch was first named Jeweled Garland Youth. Because the Twenty-sixth Patriarch
Punyamitra rode in the same carriage with King Jiangu of East India, he asked the boy:
Can you remember former events? He replied: I remember that in past kalpas I dwelt with the Master. The Master expounded
Mahāprajñā, and I upheld the profound sutras. Because we alternated in carrying on the true teaching,
I awaited the Master here. The Patriarch told the king: This is no minor sage; he is an emanation-body of Mahāsthāmaprāpta.
The king ordered him into the carriage, brought him to the palace, made offerings to him, and eventually had his hair shaved. The Patriarch took the Prajñā-
sutra affair as the basis and named him Prajñātāra. In Liang times they took Bodhidharma to be Guanyin;
in the western lands they take this Patriarch to be Mahāsthāmaprāpta. Only Amitābha Buddha, to this day,
has no trace to be found. After a long pause, he said: Fenggan was too loose-tongued. Later, because of an imperial assembly, the Venerable One
took the chief seat. This old fellow displayed marvels and confused the crowd; at the time it would have been best to overturn him and
cut off the vines. But once they asked the Venerable One why he did not read sutras, they truly could not let him off.
This old fellow too had no great-man bearing; he just flipped up a gourd and horse-ladle trick,
and the king immediately bowed. What does he know of itch or pain? Wansong says: The king greedily wanted one grain of rice from him,
and the Venerable One lost the food of ten thousand years. He only knew to prop up heaven with an iron spine, not noticing his skull-gate
had struck the ground. If one wants to lift him back up, only Tiantong can do it. He versified it, saying:

lg.l:
A cloud-rhino sporting with the moon, bright and holding its radiance; a wooden horse roaming in spring, swift and unbridled.Secretly linked by a single thread, the pattern is already manifest
Passing through a thicket of a hundred flowers, not one leaf clings to the body

Beneath the brows, a pair of cold blue-green eyes; reading sutras, how could one everNever joined the swarm chasing snakes and mayflies
pierce oxhide?Gone too far

A clear bright mind rises; with heroic strength from vast kalpas it breaks through layer upon layer ofOne arrow before King Awesome-Sound
encirclement.Shooting through two barriers

At the wondrous round pivot-mouth, numinous functioning turns; Hanshan forgets the road by which he came.When was it ever stirred
The way he came.For a moment not abiding, like a dead man
Shide joins him, and hand in hand they return.It must be a local man

cb:div.p:
The Master said: The first two lines, opening the topic, have already versified not abiding in the skandha-realms and not entering the myriad conditions.
Now, in the doctrinal teaching’s categories, the five skandhas, twelve sense-fields, and eighteen realms are called the three divisions.
The Venerable One briefly raised the beginning and end, thereby including the middle. The Sanskrit ānāpāna is translated as
exhalation and inhalation. Its method has six parts: first counting, second following, third stopping, fourth contemplation,
fifth returning, sixth purification, all fully set out in Tiantai calming and contemplation. One preparing against what is unforeseen cannot
fail to know this. Weishan’s Admonitions says: If one has never set one’s mind on doctrine and principle, the mysterious Way has no basis for
matching realization. The Baozang Treatise laments: The priceless jewel lies hidden in the pit of skandhas and sense-fields.
When will you attain the numinous light shining alone, far beyond roots and dust? Tiantong’s line, Cloud-rhino sporting
with the moon, bright and holding radiance—there is an old poem: The rhinoceros, because it sports with the moon, patterns are born in its horn. Fine words,
but sadly they get reduced to literary talent and sentimental cleverness. Wooden horse roaming in spring, swift and unbridled:
this versifies exhaling without entering myriad conditions. It may be called good movement without wheel-tracks. Beneath the brows,
a pair of cold blue-green eyes. Luopu said: If one alone is clear about oneself, the Dharma-eye is not yet clear.
Such a person has only one eye. If you want both eyes perfectly bright, then only by not abiding in
the skandha-realms and not entering myriad conditions. In the forest without shadows, suspend sun and moon on high. On the branchless branch,
secretly discern spring and autumn—only then will it do. Reading sutras, how could one ever pierce oxhide? Changqing
said: What fault has the eye? The Surangama Sutra says: Now carefully observe the holy assembly in this gathering.
Use your eyes to look all around; though the eye surveys completely, it is only like reflections in a mirror, with no separate discrimination. Here
is where people miss it. Yaoshan said: Even that oxhide must be pierced through. Wansong says: Then one indeed has vajra-eyes.
A clear bright mind transcends vast kalpas. The Third Patriarch said: Only do not hate and love,
and all becomes openly clear. In one thought, ten thousand years of receiving and upholding are inexhaustible. Lumen said: The whole great
earth is the student’s one scroll of scripture; all heaven and earth are the student’s one eye. With
this eye, read such a scripture, and through thousands of myriads of millions of kalpas there is never interruption. Wansong
says: Reading in this way is not easy. Heroic strength breaks through heavy encirclement. In the Later Han, Wang Mang sent his kinsmen
Wang Xun and Wang Yi to Kunyang, where they encircled Guangwu in dozens of layers. Guangwu’s troops were weak,
and he wished to surrender to Xun and Yi, but they would not accept it. So Guangwu further rallied his generals and sent out troops
to counterattack, and Xun and Yi were greatly defeated. The Venerable One was complete in both civil and martial ways—going out a general, entering a minister. Skandha-
realms and myriad conditions are not merely heavy encirclement. At the wondrous round pivot-mouth, numinous functioning turns. The Erya says:
A pivot is called a hinge-post. Guo Pu comments: It is the door-leaf’s pivot. Flowing water does not rot; a moving
pivot does not decay—this speaks of its living motion. The Venerable One moved before being touched, turned without being pushed.
Here or there, there was no can and cannot. Tiantong sifted sand and selected gold, split stars and cleaved
both sides, making the distinctions clear. In the last two lines he shows still more surplus skill, saying: Hanshan forgets
the road by which he came; Shide joins him, and hand in hand they return. This versifies the court feast and the ocean-like assembly,
boring through paper and piercing windows. The Venerable One, old-womanishly kind, gave them a little. Hooking the curtain, the nursing swallow returns; through the hole in the paper
the foolish fly comes out. He uses Hanshan’s poem and matches it like tally and token. The poem says: If you would gain a place to settle your body,
Hanshan can keep you long. A faint breeze blows through hidden pines; listening close, the sound grows finer. Below
there is a grizzled man, muttering as he reads Huang-Lao. For ten years he cannot return, having forgotten the road
by which he came. After Lüqiu Yin visited him, he and Shide went hand in hand out the pine gate and no longer
returned to the monastery. Some editions read softly murmuring as he reads Huang-Lao. This verse praises losing the way home while wandering weak,
and also pointing out the road to the lost. Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang invited the Huayan Chan Master Xiujing
into the palace for a vegetarian feast. All the great monks and elders read sutras; only the master’s whole group sat silent.
The emperor asked, Why do you not read sutras? Jing said: When the Way is at peace, one does not transmit the Son of Heaven’s command; when the age is clear,
one need not sing songs of great peace. The emperor said: It may do for you alone not to read, but why do your disciples
also not read? Jing said: In a lion’s den there are no other beasts; where the elephant king walks, fox tracks are cut off.
The emperor said: Then why do all the other great monks and elders read? Jing said: The jellyfish
originally has no eyes; to seek food it must depend on shrimp. The emperor was greatly pleased. Besides, the patriarch-venerable came from
distant kalpas, bearing the name Mahāsthāmaprāpta, reciting profound sutras. Therefore, from his teacher
he got the name Prajñātāra. So in the end even old habits were not removed. Still, Huayan has him beaten in that there is
at least a patchrobed monk’s clue to it. When Wansong gets here, he cannot help but laugh. Tell me, what is he laughing at?
At the point where the Luohan of Yunju opens his robe, at the moment when the tea-bottle of Gong County meets the lips.

cb:div.cb:mulu: 4. The World-Honored One Points to the Ground
cb:div.head: Case Four: The World-Honored One Points to the Ground
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: As soon as a single dust-mote is raised, the whole earth is gathered in. With one horse and one spear, one opens frontiers
and unfolds the land. One who can be master wherever he is and make the teaching his own on meeting conditions what kind of person is that?

Raising the case: As the World-Honored One was walking with the assembly, he pointed to the ground with his hand and said, This placeTurn with his heels
is fit for building a Brahma monastery. Indra took a blade of grass and stuck it into the groundOne should not break ground over Tai Sui’s head
and said, The Brahma monastery is already built. The World-Honored One smiled faintly.Building and repairing is not easyRewards and punishments are clear

The Master said: Because the World-Honored One once spread out his hair to cover mud and offered flowers to Dipankara Buddha, that Buddha pointed
to the place where the hair was spread and said, In this patch of ground a monastery should be built. At that time there was an elder named Xianshou
who planted a marker at the indicated spot and said, The monastery is already built. The gods scattered flowers and praised him,
saying, This noble son has great wisdom indeed. Tiantong raises this story with broad sameness and slight difference. Wansong said:
The World-Honored One transferred the ancestral estate to Dipankara, and then there was an elder to take it up at the front and gather it in at the back.
Now that it is handed over to Tiantong, there must be a written deed and contract. The verse says:

lg.l:
On the tips of the hundred grasses, boundless spring casually picked up, it is used with intimacy.Jiashan is still hereEntering the wild field, do not
pick and choose
The sixteen-foot golden body, a heap of merit and virtue at ease, hand in hand, enters the red dust.I respectfully inquireMaking a play of the occasion

In the dust one can be the master; beyond transformation, guests come of themselves. Wherever one touches,Once authority is in handwatch when the command is carried out
livelihood is sufficient according to one’s share.Not obtained from another
One need not mind that one’s tricks are no match for others.No shame in the face

cb:div.p:
The Master said: Tiantong first finishes versifying the koan in four lines, then spreads out the broad outline
and unfolds the transforming style. Zhaozhou picked up a single blade of grass and used it as the sixteen-foot golden body.
The World-Honored One pointed it out right in the wind; Indra casually picked it up. Tiantong intertwines person and circumstance
and brings it out in verse. Not only the ancient sages if you right now can be master in the dust, then beyond transformation
guests too will come. Tell me then, the elegant imperial son-in-law Liu who built this Baoen Monastery is it the same as or different from Indra
sticking in the grass? The Master raised his whisk and said: A thousand-year monastery, and in one morning
a monk.

cb:div.cb:mulu: 5. Qingyuan on the Price of Rice
cb:div.head: Case Five: Qingyuan on the Price of Rice
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Jati cut flesh to feed his parents, yet he is not entered in the biographies of filial sons. Devadatta pushed
a mountain to crush the Buddha how could he fear a sudden thunderclap? If you pass through the thorn forest and chop down the sandalwood
tree, then when the year is spent and the old year ends, it is still cold in early spring. The Buddha-Dharma body where is it
located?

Raising the case: A monk asked Qingyuan, What is the great meaning of the Buddha-Dharma? Qingyuan
Minor officials recite the statutes a lot
said, What is the price of rice in Luling?Old generals do not discuss war

The Master said: Chan Master Xingsi of Qingyuan Mountain in Jizhou, when he first visited the Sixth Patriarch, immediately asked,
What should one practice to avoid falling into stages? The Patriarch said, What have you done before?
Qingyuan said, I do not even engage in holy truths. The Patriarch said, Into what stages could you fall? Qingyuan said,
If I do not even engage in holy truths, what stages could there be? The Patriarch esteemed him deeply. Though there were many students in the assembly,
this master stood foremost among them. It was like the Second Patriarch saying nothing, and Shaolin called it
getting the marrow. Judging by this monk’s question about the great meaning of the Buddha-Dharma, he too was a genuine one just entering
the monastery groves, wanting to travel with Manjushri through the Iron Encircling Mountains. Qingyuan was a man who did not even engage in holy truths,
and yet he only made it into an ordinary greeting and inquiry, saying, Luling
rice what price? Some say, The price of Luling rice does not allow deliberation. They do not realize
they have already entered the grain-measure shops and stalls. If you want not to enter this guild,
ask Tiantong. The verse says:

lg.l:
Peaceful governance leaves no outward sign; the old rustic’s household style is utmost simplicity.Has the comet star appeared yet or notHow can it compare to my planting here
farming fields to earn food to eat

They simply go on with village songs and communal drinking how could they know of Shun’s virtue and Yao’s benevolence?A poor devil’s happiness never quite reaches through
Only then does loyalty and filiality become complete

cb:div.p:
The Master said: In the sixth year of Taihe under Emperor Wenzong of Tang, Niu Sengru was prime minister. The emperor asked:
When is the realm at peace? Niu replied: Peace has no outward sign. Now the four border peoples do not
encroach upon one another, and the common people are not scattered. Though this is not perfect rule, it may still be called modest prosperity.
If Your Majesty seeks some other peace, it is beyond your servant. He withdrew and repeatedly submitted memorials asking to resign.
He was sent out as military governor of Huainan. Wansong says: This is already setting up models and drawing patterns, and so
the old rustic’s household style, pounding the earth and singing, makes rites, music, and literary refinements turn into something forced.
The price of Luling rice can expose deep mystery to the sun. Shun’s virtue and Yao’s benevolence the simple wind transforms of itself. Village
songs and communal drink each gets what is fitting. Moon bright, wind clear, each rests in his own place. Do you understand?
If so, return to the hall as convenient.

cb:div.head: Case Six: Mazu White and Black
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: When one cannot open the mouth, a tongueless man can speak. Where one cannot lift a foot,
a footless man can walk. If you fall into another’s trap and die beneath words, how could there be
any share of freedom? When the four mountains press in, how do you break free?

Raising the case: A monk asked Great Master Ma, Apart from the four propositions and beyond the hundred negations, please directly point out
the Patriarch’s coming-from-the-West meaning for me. The Great Master said, Today I am tiredIf one recognized the point of this monk’s question, it would save people much effort
and cannot explain it for you. Go ask Zhizang. The monkThere is already moonlight in the boatand more, wind added to the sail
asked Zang. Zang said, Why not ask monk Shan? The monk said, monk ShanYet one is subject to another’s dispositionGood roots are often alike
told me to come ask you. Zang said, Today I have a headache and cannot explain it for you.Quite sharp and clever
Go ask Brother Hai. The monk asked Hai. Hai said,I cannot but become Master Ma’s discipleA bitter gourd is bitter to the root
When I get to this point, I do not understand. The monk reported this to the Great Master.A sweet melon is sweet clear to the stemDemanding sandal money
The Great Master said, Zang’s head is white; Hai’s head is black.Practice for another thirty years

The Master said: The Sixth Patriarch told Master Rang, The prophecy of the twenty-seven patriarchs of India says that beneath your
feet a colt will appear and trample everyone under heaven to death. The illness is in your mind; you need not
speak too soon. Later, after polishing tile and beating the ox, the divine colt entered the stable and came to be called Mazu. Ox-
gait, tiger-gaze, tongue stretched past the nose, wheel-marks under his feet he had one hundred and thirty-nine Dharma heirs,
each a master in one region. Zhizang and Brother Hai were Xitang and Baizhang.
Looking at it, this monk too was one studying the Buddha-Dharma, taking the four propositions and hundred
negations to test the aim of the separate transmission outside the teachings. The Mahayana Compendium says: affirming being is the slander of addition,
affirming nonbeing is the slander of subtraction, affirming both being and nonbeing is the slander of contradiction, affirming neither being nor
nonbeing is the slander of idle talk. If the four propositions are left behind, the hundred negations cease of themselves. Huangbo said: If you want
a direct and swift understanding, everything altogether is not it. Wansong says: If you want a precise and detailed understanding, everything
is not not it. Turning it over and back, one does not leave the four propositions, nor cut off the hundred negations. The Western
Patriarch’s meaning in what is it unclear? Nagarjuna said: Prajna is like a great mass of fire,
impossible to enter from any side. Yet he also said: Prajna is like a cool clear pool, enterable from every side.
This monk said, Apart from the four propositions and beyond the hundred negations, please directly point out the coming-from-the-West
meaning. In all quarters this is called a lock-mouth question. Mazu, unhurried, simply said, Today I am
tired and cannot explain it for you. Go ask Zhizang. He spared his own eyebrows
and pierced the monk’s nostrils. The monk could not avoid being driven about by him and truly
went to ask. Zhizang too, without consulting, spoke in accord: Why not ask the master? This monk,
with no opening of brows or eyes, said, master told me to come ask. Zang said, Today my head aches.
I cannot explain it for you. Go ask Brother Hai. It may be said, no father, no such son.
The monk asked Hai. Hai said, When I get here, I do not understand. One might have thought after Marquis White
there would also be Marquis Black. Though this monk lacked spirit, he at least had beginning and end, and came back
to report it to Mazu. The Patriarch said, Zang’s head white, Hai’s head black. This one phrase has baffled everyone under
heaven. Donglin Zhaojue versified it: The hundred negations and four propositions cut off no words. Black and white
are clearly divided, fixing the upright and the partial. Wansong says: Four at dusk, three at dawn vainly giving rise to joy and anger. One
day the three men were viewing the moon with Nanquan. Then Mazu said, At just such a time, what
is it? Baizhang said, Just right for practice. Zang said, Just right for making offerings. Nanquan brushed
his sleeves and left. Mazu said, Sutras enter Zang, Chan returns to Hai; only Puyuan alone
transcends beyond things. Here it is indeed fitting that black and white be clearly distinguished. Wansong says: Zang’s head white,
Hai’s head black. A duck’s head green, a crane’s head red. The divine colt of ten shadows stands south of the sea; the five-
colored auspicious qilin paces north of heaven. All quarters, do not borrow a fox’s borrowed might. Tiantong has his own
true news. The verse says:

lg.l:
Medicine becoming illness take warning from the sages of old. Illness becoming medicine who indeedA barbarian drinks milk and instead blames the good physicianMaster, your pulse is much disordered
Use medicine to apply medicine, use poison to remove poison

could it be? White head, black head sons fit to carry on the house,Could it be Tiantong
heirs to the house.Fired in one kiln

With words, without words the mechanism that cuts off the stream. Majestic and open,And it makes Weishan’s smile turn fresh again
they sit and sever the road of the tongue.Once dead, one does not live again
They should laugh at old gimlet Vimalakirti.Only one stake is obtained

cb:div.p:
The Master said: The four propositions are four slanders, like a great mass of fire that cannot be entered from any side. The four
propositions are also four gates, like a cool clear pool enterable from every side. In former years Wansong
served as secretary at Daming. At that time Master Heng of Tanzhe passed through Daming, and in the dark of night
knocked at the gate and told the attendant he wished to burn incense and form a connection. Tanzhe at once allowed an interview. Wansong
asked for instruction: What is a living phrase? What is a dead phrase? Zhe said: If the secretary
understands a dead phrase, it too is a living phrase. If he does not understand a living phrase, it too is a dead phrase. At
that time I thought to myself: the methods of an old adept are indeed different. Today, seeing this monk’s question, he was dead set
on wanting a separate pointing out of the Patriarch’s meaning beyond the four propositions and hundred negations. The minds of these three old
fellows were alike. If one just took it as understanding by leaving the four propositions and cutting off the hundred negations, then
one would bury this monk in one pit. Later Tiantong, in versing Yangshan’s gavel-strike in a dream,
said: Apart from the four propositions and beyond the hundred negations, Master Ma and his sons’ illness ceases to need medicine. Wansong says:
What kind of mind-practice is that? White head, black head sons fit to carry on the house. In the Zhou Yi, in the Meng hexagram,
the line Nine in the second place says, The son can carry on the household he can bear the family enterprise. With words, without words cutting off the stream
is the mechanism. Wansong says: There are only ripples on clear water, not yet waves that tower to the sky.
Majestic and open, they sit and sever the road of the tongue. They should laugh at old gimlet Vimalakirti. The Sanskrit Vi-
maliya is translated Broad Adornment, the name of the city where Vimalakirti lived. When Manjushri asked about the gate of nonduality,
Vimalakirti was silent. This monk asked Master Ma and his sons, and vines spread all over the ground.
Tell me, where is the point where one should laugh? If only one can avoid touching the taboo of the present age, that is still better than
the tongue-cutting talent of the former dynasty.

cb:div.head: Case Seven: Yaoshan Ascends the Seat
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Eyes, ears, nose, and tongue each has its own function. Eyebrows stay above. Scholars, farmers,
artisans, and merchants each return to their own task. The clumsy one is always at leisure. How does a true lineage master
set things forth?

Raising the case: Yaoshan had long not ascended the seat. The prior said, The assembly has longed forMovement is not as good as stillness.
instruction for a long time. Please, master, teach the Dharma for the assembly. Yaoshan ordered the bell struck. The assemblyBetter weighty than light.
gathered. Yaoshan ascended the seat, sat silently for a long while, then descended and returned to his quarters. The prior followed after and asked, master, you just now agreed to teach the Dharma for the assembly. WhyHuddling together to make appearances—what a long, drawn-out affair.One round—
of talk.
did you not let fall a single word? Yaoshan said, Sutras have sutra teachers, treatises haveIf the great sea knew contentment, the hundred rivers would flow back upstream.
treatise teachers. Why blame this old monk?A pity—dragon head, snake tail.

The Master said: For the hungry it is easy to make food; for the thirsty it is easy to make drink. Therefore three visits and five
invitations, a bodhisattva ascends the hall; half a verse and the whole body; a yaksha ascends the seat was this out of craving for Dharma?
Chan Master Nan of Huanglong said: Nowadays many people take the Dharma lightly. One wants
to treat them like a farmer who lets the field dry from time to time, making it parched and thirsty, and only then irrigates it so that
it may become luxuriant and bear fruit. But Yaoshan’s long not ascending the seat was not quite like that. Juefan said: In one
hut is deeply hidden a thunderbolt tongue, letting the ten thousand forms each speak for themselves. Yongjia said: In silence he speaks;
in speaking he is silent. The great gate of giving stands open without obstruction. The prior missed it at every point,
saying, The assembly has longed for instruction; please, master, teach the Dharma for them. In the way of human feeling
and proper conduct, and in terms of host and guest, this was not beyond the bounds. Yaoshan ordered the bell struck. One sees only
thunder issuing commands. As the assembly gathered, who knew the stars and Dipper were already shining in patterns? Yaoshan
ascended the seat, stayed long, descended, and returned to his quarters. This one display of spiritual power was no small thing.
The prior followed after and asked, master, you just agreed to teach the Dharma for the assembly. Why did you not
let fall a single word? Cuiyan Zhi said: After Yaoshan descended the seat, the prior from the start blamed him for not
teaching the Dharma to the assembly. It may be said he misled the whole army. Wansong says: It was exactly because the general’s head
was not fierce enough. Yaoshan said, Sutras have sutra teachers, treatises have treatise teachers. Why blame this old
monk? Langya Jue said: It is fine to be doubtful when Yaoshan descends the seat, but when the prior
presses in, one eye is lost. Wansong says: How many can make it whole again?
Yet he did not know that two eyes were gained in exchange. Xuedou said: What a pity for old Yaoshan.
He stumbled on level ground, and all the people on earth could not help him up. Wansong says: master too
must put out a hand. Wuyu versified it: Before leaving the abbot’s room he had already stumbled; quietly
returning, he became all the more forlorn. Sutra teachers and treatise teachers still tell each other; one clause made clear and
one summons it oneself. Wansong says: Clerks are easy to examine; the koan is not yet rounded off. Hand it over to Tian-
tong how will he judge it? The verse says:

lg.l: A foolish child fixates on a hush-money coin; a fine steed outruns the wind and moves at the shadow of the whip.What use is it fit forKick it up and go at once

Clouds sweep the long sky; a crane nested in the moon. Cold clarity enters the bones and sleep cannot come.A whole muddle under the tree
Dreaming with open eyes

cb:div.p:
The master said: The Nirvana Sutra says that when a baby cries, the mother takes a yellow leaf and says, I will give you
gold, and the child immediately stops crying. This verse has long been pondered. The instruction and the question, Why not let fall a single
word? An outsider asked the Buddha, I do not ask about speech, and I do not ask about no speech. The World-Honored One remained silent for a long
while. The outsider then bowed and said, World-Honored One, great compassion has parted my clouds of delusion and enabled me
to enter. After the outsider left, Ananda asked the Buddha, What principle did the outsider see, that
he said he had entered? The Buddha said, It is like a fine horse in the world that moves when it sees the shadow of the whip. Yaoshan and
the World-Honored One alike raised the whip. The monastery superintendent led the monks; there was a share in bowing and praising, yet they instead blamed him for
not uttering a single word. It may be said that patch-robed monks in the Eastern Land are not equal to the outsiders of the Western Heaven. Tian-
tong versifies in this way, and Wansong explains in this way, all of it being yellow leaves to stop crying, only because
all of you have not yet awakened from your fevered dream. Those who sleep lightly wake at a single shout; those who sleep heavily must be shaken
before they are startled. There is yet another sort: even when beaten and roused, they still
compare themselves with Yaoshan's moon-nesting crane of clear eyes, too pure to sleep. Cloud and mud are far apart. Even so,
for all that, sleep-talk is not a little.

cb:div.head: Case Eight: Baizhang’s Wild Fox
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Keep the word not in your mind, and entering hell is like an arrow shot. One
drop of wild-fox saliva, swallowed down for thirty years and still it cannot be spat out. It is not that India’s rule is severe,
it is only that the dull fellow’s karma is heavy. Has anyone here ever made this mistake?

Raising the case: When Baizhang ascended the hall, there was always an old man who listened to the Dharma and left with the crowd.
One day he did not leave. Baizhang then asked, Who is this standing here? The old man said, In the age of Kashyapa Buddha in the past, I once lived on thisFind stillness amid the clamor.All along I have had doubts about this fellow.He cannot manage affairs, yet visitors keep coming.
One must wait until—
mountain. A student asked me, Does a person of great practice still fall into cause and effectit turns out he is the one in charge.
or not? I answered him, He does not fall into cause and effect, and so I fell intoJust do good deeds; do not ask about the future.One fitting phrase can become the hitching post for a donkey through ten thousand kalpas.
a wild fox body for five hundred births. Now I ask master to give a turning word in my place. Baizhang said, Not blind to cause and effect. At these words the old man had a great awakening.You say it does not fall into cause and effect.What does it matter?
The origin.Bury it in a single pit.The fox’s slobber is still there.

The master said: Whenever Chan Master Dazhi of Baizhang Mountain in Hongzhou ascended the seat, there was always
an old man present. In the time of Kasyapa Buddha, while dwelling on this mountain and hearing the Dharma, he once gave a mistaken reply to a student,
a single turning phrase, and to this day had fallen into the body of a wild fox. It was truly because he himself leaned against walls and clung to them,
sending others tumbling into pits and ditches. Seeing that Dazhi had the skill to pull nails and extract wedges, he then
set himself aside and followed him, asking Dazhi to speak a turning phrase in his place. Dazhi bestowed fearless eloquence,
and lightly turned it, saying: Not blind to cause and effect. At these words the old man had a great awakening. Speaking according to the facts,
not falling into cause and effect is the nihilistic view that denies them. Not being blind to cause and effect is going along with the flow and finding the subtle.
Anyone with even a slight understanding of the teachings will see it as soon as it is raised. Even so, though he has shed the fur
coat, he still wears scales and armor. Have you not seen Chan Master Yuan, while in Master Nan's assembly,
hear two monks bring up this story? One monk said: Even with not being blind to cause and effect, one still has not
escaped the fox body. Another monk immediately answered: Then even not falling into cause and effect
would also never have fallen into a fox body, would it? The master was startled and found these words extraordinary, so he hurried up to Huangbo's
Jicui Hermitage, and on the way suddenly had a great awakening. When he saw Master Nan and told the story, before he had finished
tears streamed down his cheeks. Master Nan told him to go warm himself by the attendant's couch. He suddenly rose and made a verse, saying:
Not falling, not obscured, monk and lay have originally no taboo. A true man's bearing is like a king's, how could he
submit to being bagged and covered over? One chestnut staff, free to go vertical and horizontal; the wild fox leaps into the golden-
furred ranks. Master Nan laughed loudly. Seen this way, when at first he said, I now ask the master to speak
a turning phrase in my place, it was only good to say not falling into cause and effect, so as to keep beginners from falling
into the pit of conceptual understanding. Later, Baizhang ascended the hall and raised this earlier cause and condition. Huangbo then asked:
The man of old gave one mistaken turning phrase and fell for five hundred lives into a wild fox body. If each turning phrase is not mistaken,
what should happen then? Zhang said: Come closer and I will tell you. Bo came forward and gave
Zhang a slap. Zhang clapped his hands and laughed, saying: I thought the fox's beard was red, but here is an even redder-bearded
fox. Yangshan said: Baizhang attained the great pivot, Huangbo attained great function. Their fame was not gotten in vain.
Guishan raised this and asked Yangshan: Huangbo often used this pivot. Was it innate,
or did he get it from another? Yang said: It was both received through teacher and lineage, and also a direct penetration of the self-nature's source.
Gui said: Just so, just so. Look at those father-and-son, Baizhang and Huangbo, roaming without
fear, like lion kings. How could they make their livelihood in a wild fox den? Wansong has already
let his tailbone show all the more; now he lets Tiantong display his claws and fangs. Look. The verse says:

lg.l:
One foot of water, one fathom of waves before five hundred births, there was nothing to be done.Fortunately, the rivers are clear and the seas are calmHad I known it would be like this today,
I would regret not being careful at the start.

Not falling, not blind even discussing it, one still crashes as before into the vine-tangleStubborn drool does not stop.
nest.Wrapping the waist and binding the feet.

Ah haha do you understand? If you are open andFit for laughter, fit for grief.Pressing the ox's head to make it eat grass.
free and clear,Like an insect boring wood.

then no harm in my da-da he-he, spirit songs and village dancesBy chance it forms a pattern.
making a tune of themselves.Every clap is the command.
Clapping hands in their midst, singing li-luo.Bringing up the petty details.

cb:div.p:
The Master said: To set up cultivation and realization and divide cause and effect one foot of water becomes one fathom of waves, and one falls into the five-
hundred-birth wild fox spirit. Though the two monks beneath Jicui Hermitage had eloquence beyond the common run,
when examined closely they still could not avoid crashing into the vine-tangle nest. In Tiantong’s line here,
two characters are not quite steady. Why not say, still crashed into the wild fox den? Ah haha
this verse clarifies where Baizhang awakened, and Tiantong bares his own chest and says, Do you understand?
I only ask whether Tiantong understood or not. If you are open and free and clear, then no harm in my
da-da he-he. Since there is fortunately a patch of shade, why not make use of it for people? Da-da he-
he this is infant speech, not true in form. Also, in the Notes on the Lotus it says: duo-da is the appearance of learning
and practice; po-he is the sound of practicing speech. The Nirvana Sutra speaks of diseased practice and infant-
practice. Some editions read po-po he-he. Chan Master Shandao of Stone Chamber said: Of the sixteen practices in the Nirvana,
the infant practice is supreme. Da-da he-he describes people studying the Way
when they have left behind the mind of discrimination and choosing and rejecting. It is the same meaning as the spirit songs and village dances below.
But tell me, what tune is it? The ten thousand sounds, though intent, cannot hear it; the solitary crag, earless,
still knows the tune.

cb:div.head: Case Nine: Nanquan Cuts the Cat
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Kick over the blue sea and dust flies across the earth. Shout apart the white clouds and empty space
shatters to pieces. Even strict enforcement of the true command is only half the matter. When great function is fully manifest, how is it
applied?

Raising the case: One day at Nanquan’s place, the monks of the east and west halls were fighting over a cat. Nan-
When people are level they do not speak; when water is level it does not flow.
quan saw it, picked up the cat, and said, If you can speak, I will not cut it. The assembly had no reply. Nanquan then cut the cat into two. Later Nanquan again raised the earlier storyWho dares face the edge?Just wait until
rain pours on your head.Drawing the blade and not returning it to the sheath.
and asked Zhaozhou. Zhaozhou immediately took off his straw sandals, placed them on his head, and walked out. Nanquan said, If you had been there, you would indeed have saved the cat.Bring it again and it is not worth half a coin.Best to give one slash.
Into two pieces.With a crooked mind, one does not notice the mouth shouting.

The teacher said: Chan Master Xiou of Fayun Yuantong saw two monks standing together talking, and took
his staff, went over, struck several times, and said, One patch of karma-ground, all the more with the assembly heads of two halls
who, because of a cat, caused contention. Nanquan neither reconciled them nor offered admonition, nor did he punish them.
A true man of the Way, using the proper matter for the sake of others, then lifted up the cat and said,
If you can speak, then it will not be cut. At just such a moment, throughout the ten directions, sentient and insentient alike,
all together begged for life from Nanquan’s hand. If at that time there had been one who came forth and spread open
both hands, or else blocked him at the chest and embraced him, saying, No need to trouble master with miraculous function—even if Nan
quan had carried out another true command, I dare guarantee the cat would have been saved. But this whole hole of dead rats—
since there was not the slightest breath in them, Nanquan had already extended and did not withdraw, fully carrying out the command. In the Liao
dynasty monk Jue wrote the Record of Mirror-Mind, reproaching people like Nanquan for killing living beings and creating sin. Chief Seat Wen
wrote the Endless Lamp Correcting Errors to defend him, saying: In old versions, he only made an empty cutting gesture with his hand;
how could it really have been one slash into two pieces, with fresh blood spurting everywhere? These two, in judging
the ancients—Master Wen’s fault is heavy, Master Jue’s fault is lighter. Nanquan remains as ever, in the herd of water buffaloes,
shaking his head and swishing his tail. Do you not see? Chan Master Fori, while seated with the assembly at tea,
saw a cat come, and from his sleeve tossed a pigeon to it. The cat caught it and went off. Fori said,
Fine indeed, but it still cannot be allowed—it is a contrived empty display. Nanquan himself thought: When the tune is lofty, those who can join are few.
So he raised the earlier story and asked Zhaozhou. Zhaozhou then took off his straw sandals, put them on his head, and walked out.
Truly, striking and singing went together, and the rhythm was complete. Quan said, If you had been there, you would indeed have saved
the cat. This little bit of functioning, though hard to understand, is easy to see. You need only, at the point of
picking up the spoon and lifting the chopsticks, look through it and break through; then you will see cutting the cat and wearing straw sandals are no
different. Otherwise, look further and see what other device Tiantong makes. The verse says:

lg.l:
Monks of the two halls all fall into wrangling; Master Wang is able to test right and wrong.Being in the right does not depend on a loud voice.A bright mirror.
Set on the stand, whatever comes is reflected.

With a sharp blade he cuts it off—both forms perish; through a thousand ages it makes peopleHow much wind from the Dragon King can it withstand?
cherish the true master-worker.There is one person who will not consent.

This Way is not yet lost; one who knows the tune is worthy of praise.What use is a dead cat's head?Not that there is none,
only that there is little.

Piercing mountains and opening the sea—only Great Yu is honored. Smelting stones to mend the sky—alone
Merit is not bestowed in vain.
the worthy Nüwa excels.Lacking even one will not do.

Old Zhaozhou has his own livelihood: straw sandals on his head,Whatever is picked up at hand is right.
worn just a little off.For now, believe half.

Coming from within difference, still a clear mirror shines: only this truePatch-robed monks are hard to fool.
gold is not mixed with sand.What is real is hard to extinguish.

cb:div.p:
The teacher said: The monks of the two halls all fell into wrangling; to this day they have never settled it. If not for
Tiantong understanding Nanquan and testing the pattern to reveal the clues, often right and wrong would not be distinguished. When right and
wrong are clearly distinguished, how do you judge? Then it is best to cut with a sharp sword and bury the whole pit.
This not only wipes out one unresolved public case, it also lets the world beneath a thousand ages
be clear and fresh. At that time Nanquan was superior as teacher, strong in resources; seeing the assembly speechless, he then
brought it up to Zhaozhou, showing that among the assembly there was someone. Zhaozhou took off his straw sandals and placed them on his head
and went out. Truly this Way was not lost; a knower of the tune is praiseworthy. Confucius said, If Heaven is not about to
let this culture perish... Look at that teacher and disciple: their Ways matched, chant and beat followed each other; there is no
fitting comparison. In posthumous naming, one whose springs and streams flow freely is called Yu; also one who receives the throne and succeeds is called Yu.
In the Yu Gong of the Classic of Documents, he guided the river from Jishi to Longmen. In the Huainanzi, Gonggong
was militarily strong and violent, contending with Emperor Yao for merit. When his strength was exhausted, he rammed Mount Buzhou
and died; the pillar of heaven broke because of it. Nüwa smelted five-colored stones to mend the sky. In the Liezi,
when yin and yang lose their measure, it is called a breach; refining the essences of the five constants is called repair. Yungai originally
cited Dongshan’s story of snatching away Chief Seat Tai’s fruit tray, saying: Though Dongshan had a drill-hammer
that could smash open empty space, still he lacked the needle and thread for mending. Nanquan was like
Great Yu piercing mountains and opening the sea, revealing miraculous function. Zhaozhou was like Nüwa smelting stones
to mend the sky, rounding off the topic. Wansong said: From eighteen on, Zhaozhou knew how to break up homes
and scatter households—who knows how many livelihoods he had! Straw sandals on his head, worn a little off. Tut,
tut—there is nowhere to go, yet he makes this move of going and coming. Baofu Zhan said: Even so,
it is only torn straw sandals. Nanquan, leveling the high and meeting the low, said, If you had been there, you truly would have
saved the cat. Cuiyan Zhi said: Big or small, Zhaozhou could only save himself. Letting go
that one move. Tiantong says: Coming from within difference, still it is a clear mirror. Only this true gold
is not mixed with sand. They only know to push the boat with the current, not to steer against the wind. And now
you lot come up here—there is no cat either, so why quarrel over dogs? He drove them down with his staff.

cb:div.cb:mulu: 10. The Old Woman of Mount Tai
cb:div.head: Case Ten: The Old Woman of Mount Tai
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: There is gathering and releasing; dry wood is at hand. Able to kill, able to give life, the balancing steelyard
is in hand. Dusty toils and demonic outsiders are all subject to one pointing command. The great earth and mountains and rivers all become playthings.
But tell me: what realm is this?

Raised: On the road to Mount Tai there was an old woman. Whenever a monk asked,Village folk by the city, roadside rabbits.
Which way does the road to Mount Tai go? the old woman said, Go straight on.Traveling all your life and still not knowing where you have gone.
As soon as the monk walked off, the old woman said, A fine teacher-monk—off he goes just like that again.Not yet a good intention.Caught by thieves and not even knowing it.
The monk told Zhaozhou. Zhaozhou said, Wait, I will examine her for you. Zhaozhou also asked just as before. The next day, in the hall, he said, For you I haveYou have long been waiting for white.When people are level, they do not speak.When water is level,
it does not flow.The device for trapping a tiger.
examined through that old woman.I will wait for black instead.

The teacher said: That old woman on the Mount Tai road was used to following Wuzhuo in and out of monasteries, well seasoned in
consulting Mañjuśrī, before three and after three. Whenever she saw a monk ask where the road to Mount Tai
went, she immediately pointed straight out the great road to Chang’an and said, Go straight on. If the monk
did not raise doubt or hesitate and just went, the old woman said, A fine teacher-monk—off he goes just like that again.
This old woman too had hook and awl in hand; from of old she has tripped up many worthies. This
monk, since he could do nothing with her, picked it up and told Zhaozhou. Zhaozhou said, Wait, I will examine
her. He leaves all under heaven deeply puzzled. This old fellow, old as he is, still does not rest his mind—what is he after?
He also wanted to settle a sect-eye. Zhaozhou asked as before; the old woman answered as before.
Some people then split the story into two chunks: in the first part, they say this monk supports the old woman; in the later
part, they say the old woman supports Zhaozhou. Only Xuanzhe said: The earlier monk also asked in just this way
and was answered in just this way; later Zhaozhou also asked and was answered in just this way. Tell me, where is the point of examining through?
Wansong said: He examined through it already. And he also said: Not only was she examined through by Zhaozhou—
she was also examined through by this monk. Wansong said: It not only implicates Xuanzhe,
it also implicates Wansong. Langya said: Big or small, Zhaozhou in this old woman’s hands
lost body and life. Even so, there are many who misunderstand. Wansong said:
Absolutely avoid using yourself to measure others. Weishan Zhe said: Monks under heaven only know to ask
the old woman for the road, but do not know how deep the mud is under their own feet. If not for old Zhaozhou,
how would the great merit of a hard-ridden horse be made manifest? Even so, it still required Tiantong’s praise in song. The verse
says:

lg.l:
Old with years, become a spirit—no false transmission: Zhaozhou, an ancient Buddha, heir to Nanquan.Above all, do not bewitch other people's men and women.
Zhenzhou truly produces big radishes.

The withered turtle loses its life because of chart and image; the fine steed chasing the wind
Clever ghosts and spirits turn around and fall into the net.
gets entangled and dragged.Sudden wind and sudden rain still do not escape the reins.

He examined through the old woman’s Chan and spoke it outHow many men are true men?
before others—it is not worth a coin.Knowing the root is not holiness.

cb:div.p:
The teacher said: Ghosts and demons become spirits through uncanny powers. Spells and medicines become potent through dependent powers. Heavenly
dragons become spirits through recompense-powers. Worthies and sages become spirits through miraculous powers. Buddhas and patriarchs through the Way
become spirits. Nanquan and Zhaozhou were people above Buddhas and patriarchs—how much more when aged.
Therefore it is said: old with years, become a spirit. Zhaozhou, an ancient Buddha, heir to Nanquan. Mazu
said: Sutras return to the treasury, Chan returns to the sea; only Nanquan alone transcends things. Zhaozhou
took Changsha as friend and Nanquan as teacher. Therefore in his examinations and discernments, it is not a matter of gain and loss,
victory and defeat, that can be graded. People everywhere call it Zhaozhou’s Barrier, and indeed it is no obstacle to being difficult to pass.
Even so, Confucius said: The divine turtle could appear in a dream to Lord Yuan, yet it could not
escape Yu Qie’s net. Its wisdom answered seventy-two drillings without a missed stalk, yet
it could not avoid the calamity of having its belly cut open. Thus wisdom has where it is trapped, and spirit has where it
does not reach. Zhuangzi says: Lord Yuan of Song dreamed of a man with loose hair who said, I come from the abyss of Zai Road.
I was sent by the Clear River at the place of the River Earl. The fisherman Yu Qie caught
me. On waking he divined it—it was a divine turtle. And indeed there was a fisherman Yu Qie who netted a white
turtle, five feet around. The lord wanted to keep it alive. They divined and it said: Kill the turtle and divine by it,
auspicious. So they cut open the turtle. Through seventy-two drillings there was not a missed stalk. That is the story. Luopu
said: If you wish to know a person of the highest stream, do not paste the words and teachings of Buddhas and patriarchs onto your forehead.
Like a turtle bearing the chart, it takes upon itself the omen of losing its life; like a phoenix caught in a golden net while racing to the heavens.
men. What did he mean by that? Among King Mu of Zhou’s eight steeds, there was one that rode the clouds and sped along, surpassing flying
birds. Hence it is said, a fine team of horses chases the wind. This verse praises the old woman for being able to test the monk, yet
she still could not avoid being seen through by Zhaozhou. Though Zhaozhou could test the old woman, he still could not avoid Langya’s
inspection. In Chan study this is called the gold-and-shit teaching. If one does not understand, it is like gold; when it is exposed, it is like shit.
Therefore it is said, speaking of it before people is not worth a coin. You need only leave behind notions of gain and loss, victory and
defeat, and calculating mind. Then naturally you will stand level with the old woman and look down on Zhaozhou. But if you come to Wansong’s
gate, you will not be allowed to beat your chest and strut.

cb:div.head: The Eleventh Case: Yunmen’s Two Illnesses
cb:div.p:
He addressed the assembly, saying: A man without a body falls ill; a man without hands compounds medicine; a man without a mouth takes
food. A man without sensation is at ease. Tell me, how is the disease in the vital region to be treated?

Citation: Great Master Yunmen said, When the light does not break through, there are two kinds of illness. One is that nothing is clear anywhere, and before one there seems to be some thing.Do you still feel your mouth dry?
Has your tongue shrunk back?Seeing ghosts in broad daylight, is it failing eyesight?
The other is that one penetrates the emptiness of all dharmas, yet dimly there still seems to be something there; this too
is a failure of the light to break through. Again, the dharma body also has two kinds of illness: having attained the dharma body, one does not forget attachment to dharma; one’s view still remains, and one falls to the side of theAlready a knotted chest, how could one bear a blocked throat?Disaster does not
come alone.
dharma body. This is one. Even if one has penetrated it, to let it go unchecked is still notNot only evil influences, but even one's own family.
permissible. If one examines it carefully and asks what breath or trace remains, that too is an illness.Nursing illness and losing the body.
。Before the skilled doctor leaves the door, the illness flares again.

The master said: Chan Master Qianfeng of Yuezhou was a dharma heir of Dongshan Wuben. Yunmen traveled widely
in his training and had met this master as well as Caoshan and Shushan. This koan therefore has an earlier source.
Qianfeng addressed the assembly and said: The dharma body has three kinds of illness and two kinds of light. One must pass through them one by
one, and further one must know there is an opening beyond. Yunmen stepped forth and said: Just
like a person inside a hut, why does he not know what is outside the hut? Qianfeng gave a hearty laugh. Yunmen
said: This is still the student’s point of doubt. Qianfeng said: What sort of mind activity is this of yours? Yunmen said:
