for trapping tigers, burying troops and stirring battle. Everywhere they all say Yantou's one raise and one press, and Dongshan's
mistaken naming and phrasing. They do not realize that Dongshan's one raise and one press are even clearer.
If one is not a long-practiced adept, one will certainly not grasp it. Nowadays a certain sort of freshly shaven novice
sees Tiantong's verse say one raise and one press reveal mind-activity, and then says, No wonder
Chan people refuse to explain it plainly to others. So after all it is all just mind-activity, and they only make devices
and tricks to deceive students. Wansong says: One measure of mind-activity is one measure of compassion.
Without taking a fall, one does not learn a lesson. It may be said the fruit comes from the flower, and sweetness comes
out of bitterness. Yantou raised and pressed, Dongshan lured it out. Dongshan raised and pressed, and Wansong
explained it through. If suddenly someone came out and bowed, Wansong would let him pass and not strike. Why?
Because under his skin there is no blood.

cb:div.head: Case Twenty-Three: Luzu Faces the Wall
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: Bodhidharma for nine years is called wall-gazing. Shenguang's three bows leaked out
heaven's secret. How can one sweep away the tracks and erase the traces?

Raised: Whenever Luzu saw a monk come, he would immediately face the wall. Nanquan heard of this and said, IThey have met
ordinarily tell him: Before the empty kalpa, bear it up; before the Buddha appeared in the world,Without interrogation he confesses
even to understand at that time, one cannot get even one or half a one. Yet he like thisMonk, do you understand or notOnly because the peg and rope were let slip
will go on till donkey-year.The busy one does not understand

The master said: Chan Master Baoyun of Luzu Mountain in Chizhou, whenever he saw a monk come, would immediately turn
to face the wall. Since Bodhidharma's nine years, no one had carried out this command again. It did at least make those in the various quarters
Then he asks about the upward and downward matter of asking Buddha, asking the Patriarchs, so everyone can feel a bit of the ache and itch. Nanquan
was his fellow practitioner. Seeing the man would not stir even to a needle or awl, he at once gave a side stroke, opening a side passage
and breaking through. I usually tell him: Take it up from before the empty kalpa, before the Buddha appeared in the world,
and realize it then. Not one or even half a one can get it. The meaning seems like he is letting out a single thread, but in truth
he has already conveyed the Dharma intent for that attendant. He also says: If he goes on like this till donkey-year,
the meaning seems to scold him for being too solitary and severe, but actually he praises him for a face-to-face entrustment.
Do you not see it said: Even if one speaks it ten parts complete, how can it compare with one direct personal arrival? Therefore Vulture Peak
is like painting the moon, and Caoxi is like pointing to the moon. How could it compare to Luzu in the crystal palace,
in the Palace of Vast Cold, opening his robe and meeting directly. Baofu saw Nanquan and Luzu laid open and worn through,
and asked Changqing: As for Luzu, where are the joints and pattern lines? Since Nanquan
spoke like that, Wansong said: How did Baofu come to use the two words joints and pattern?
If he were not able to distinguish spring and autumn on a branch where no buds sprout, it would be hard to pose this question. Changqing replied,
saying: To withdraw oneself and yield to another, among ten thousand there is not one. Wansong said: The ancients had such
clear eyes. Xuanzue said: Was Nanquan speaking in responsive accord, or was he refusing to speak? Wan-
song said: Half covered, half revealed, he cannot be deceived in the least. Cuiyan Zhi said: Why
labor like this? Wansong said: That is already setting up a mold and drawing a pattern. He also said: If
a monk comes, what would he see? Wansong said: I still find it too little. He further said: Knowing
the time is best. Wansong said: If it were Tao Yuanming, he would knit his brows and go home. He also
said: I am not like that. If one has not yet formed the embryo, one cannot understand; if one does understand, I will break
your back. Wansong said: Reverend, who is your staff meant for? Luoshan said: If Teacher Wang
had seen it at that time, I would have given him five fiery strokes across the back, because he knows how to let go but not how to gather in.
Wansong said: Rising before dawn at the fifth watch, there are already night travelers. Xuansha said: If I
had seen it then, I too would have given five fiery strokes. Wansong said: Stirring up the spring wind, in the end
it does not cease. Yunju Xi said: Luoshan and Xuansha both speak this way. Is it one
and the same, or is there another principle? Wansong said: Counted together, ten fiery strokes. He also said: If
you can sort it out, I will allow that your Buddhism has somewhere to go. Wansong said: Good, give him five
fiery strokes. Worthies, look here: Nanquan thus belittles and bends down Luzu, while Changqing
instead says this is precisely withdrawing oneself and yielding to another. Luzu blocks the passes and ferries, yet Luoshan
says he can release but not gather. All of it is casting nets before the fish, drawing the bow after the thief has passed. Still
look at what different move Tiantong makes to meet it. The verse says:

lg.l:
Within blandness there is flavor, wondrously surpassing feeling and speech; unbroken, as ifWho told you to add salt and vinegarAnother day you may discuss it
abiding, before the images and before the First.It has already fallen into second place

Plain and stolid, like a fool, the Way is precious; when jade is carved with patterns,
No one puts a price on it
its native simplicity is lost.The monk's hand is skillful

A pearl in the deep naturally shines of itself; a full, clear freshness,Show off less
cool and bright, grinds away the summer into autumn.The substance is exposed in the autumn wind
One sheet of idle cloud afar divides sky and water.Good affairs meet many obstacles

cb:div.p:
The Master said: Formerly the second imperial son-in-law of the Tudan clan was going to take office in Nanjing. Passing by Daming in Cizhou,
he entered the hall to pay respects. He saw a monk sitting facing the wall. The son-in-law said: A fine
bland fellow. Master Quan said: There is flavor within blandness. The nature of water is originally bland; add
tea or honey, and sweetness and bitterness arise. Nature too is calm and plain; divide it by delusion and awakening, and then ordinary and
sage are established. Though one says flavor in blandness, this is the flavor of no flavor. Its flavor
is constant, wondrously surpassing feeling and speech. The character for feeling comes from heart, the character for speech from words. Here,
the road of language is cut off and the workings of mind are extinguished. Fayan said: Principle at the utmost forgets feeling and speech. How
could any metaphor match it? In the Daodejing, in the chapter The valley spirit never dies, it says: The gate of the mysterious female is
the root of heaven and earth, continuous as if abiding. It also says: I do not know whose child it is, before the image of the Emperor
it comes. For patch-robed monks this means: continuous as if abiding, it must not be cut off in one direction and utterly severed.
Before the image of the Emperor means before the empty kalpa, before the Buddha appeared in the world. As for
like a fool, the Way is precious, this secretly uses Xuedou's line The Way is precious like a fool. His verse says: After the rain, clouds congeal,
dawn half opens, several peaks stand like a painting, blue and towering. Empty-born did not understand sitting in the cliff cave,
and so he stirred heaven flowers to flutter down. This verse praises Empty-born seated in meditation while the heavenly emperor rained flowers.
Now Luzu could not let the matter rest, and stirred up a whole band of old fellows like Nanquan and Xuansha
to inspect and pick at it. This is exactly jade carved with patterns and thus losing simplicity. Better the pearl in the deep,
shining of itself. In the Jin History, Lu Ji's Chongwen Fu says: Stone stores jade and the mountain shines.
Water holds a pearl and the river is made lovely. The jade hidden in stone was chiseled open by the likes of Nanquan;
the pearl hidden in water was dredged out by the likes of Xuansha. Fortunately it was of itself fully fresh and clear,
cool enough to grind summer into autumn, and yet it was forcibly turned into one patch of idle cloud dividing sky and water afar. Wansong
never faced a wall. You lot here, what joints and patterns are you looking for? In my own place I answer: Listen
to Tiantong's verse on the ancient case.

cb:div.head: Case Twenty-Four: Xuefeng Looks at the Snake
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: The carp of the Eastern Sea, the turtle-nosed snake of South Mountain, Puhua's donkey bray, Zihu's
dog bark. Not falling into the common track, not going by way of the strange kind, tell me: what sort of person's
way of walking is this?

Citing the case: Xuefeng instructed the assembly, saying: On South Mountain there is a turtle-nosed snake. All of you
people must look carefully. Changqing said: Today in the hallLifting the sitting cloth he said, This was not borrowed
there will surely be many who lose body and life. A monk brought this up to Xuansha. XuanshaHearing the wind, it immediately fliesNo more than three piled up
said: It must be my elder brother Leng to do that. Even so, I would notFox friends and dog companions
be like that. The monk said: Then how would you do it, Reverend? XuanshaIf there is another longer one, please bring it outScratching an itch on a poisonous insect's head
said: What use is South Mountain? Yunmen thrust his staff toward Xuefeng'sEven just this turtle nose is still extra
face and made a threatening gesture.Why harm your own life

The Master said: Though the turtle-nosed snake of South Mountain is a dead snake, in front of Xianggu Cliff one knew how to handle it and make it live.
Xuefeng brought it up before the assembly, originally intending to use poison to remove poison. Changqing only knew
how to push the boat with the current, saying there would be many in the hall today who lose body and life. If he had
known how to take the helm against the wind, Xuefeng would have had to enter the nirvana hall. Xuansha, hearing
of it, said: It must be my elder brother Leng. Here there is a bit of entanglement. If one says
Xuansha was approving Changqing, then why did he also say, I would not be like that? He not only
knew how to push with the current, he also knew how to steer against the wind. That monk, heedless of danger,
immediately asked, How would you do it, Reverend? Xuansha only said: What use is South Mountain?
Right here one sees the hand that can make a live snake dance. Yunmen then thrust out his staff in a threatening pose.
He used it most intimately. In the verse on the Yunyan sweeping-the-ground story, he once cited the line about before Xianggu Cliff
the hand that handles snakes. What one did in youth, one is ashamed of in old age. As for Yunmen saying the servant seeing the maid
is over-attentive, he then rides the thief's horse to chase the thief. Even so, it does not hinder rough and capable hands and feet. Today again
he verses this snake-handling story, boasting of dragon-grabbing hands. Look at him: after hacking with a great axe, he rubs the hand.
The verse says:

lg.l:
Xuansha greatly hard, Changqing a little bold; the turtle-nosed snake of South Mountain is dead,At the critical moment he yields not even to his fatherSeeing what is right and not doing it
of no use.The carrying pole snaps the binding rope

When wind and cloud meet at the right time, horns and head arise; then indeed one seesWhen the time comes, even an earthworm becomes a flood dragon
Shaoyang put his hand to it and play with it.Unable to suppress a laugh

Putting hand to it and playing, he flashes lightning light;If you cannot bring it out, stop after two or three tries
amid it, watch the transformations.In a blink, body lost and life gone

On my side, I too can send and can summon; on that sideShow off less
there is also seizing and releasing.The seven-inch vital spot is in hand

This matter now, to whom is it entrusted? A cold mouthOld man Wansong
wounds people, and they do not know the pain.Ah yes, ah yes

cb:div.p:
The Master said: Xuansha specially sent a messenger racing with a letter up to Xuefeng. When Xuefeng opened it, he saw three
sheets of blank paper. He showed them to the monk and said: Do you understand? The monk said: I do not. Xuefeng said: Do you not
see it said, A gentleman a thousand miles apart shares the same wind? The monk returned and reported this to Xuansha. Xuansha said: The old monk on the mountain
blundered past it and does not even know. Xuansha inherited from Xuefeng. He was always ready to attest for his
father and steal the sheep, not yielding when faced with what is right. Saying What use is South Mountain? was again resolute
and unmatched, too hard indeed. Changqing, following along crookedly, then said there would be many who lose body
and life. He did not understand the lion's backward pounce. This is truly seeing what is right and not doing it, that is lack of courage.
When wind and cloud meet at the right time, horns and head arise, and indeed one sees Shaoyang lay hands to it and play. This verse
praises Yunmen for producing a live snake right before the face, unlike the other masters, whose bow and cup only cast shadows.
As for being able to send and summon, a common saying has it: To call a snake is easy; to send a snake away is
hard. As for seizing and releasing, this verse speaks of first thrusting it before the face and then making a threatening gesture.
If one can already seize and release, one must also understand sending and summoning. At the end Tiantong says: This matter now
to whom is it entrusted? A cold mouth wounds people and they do not know the pain. Whenever one claps out a verse, it returns to oneself.
Only when one brings it forth before the face is one truly a master. Xuedou also said: Now it is hidden in
front of Rufeng. Those who come should each look at the expedient means. The Master shouted loudly: Watch your feet
below. Wansong said: Xuedou greedily watches below his feet, not knowing he has already pierced through the skull. Tian-
tong's cold mouth wounds people. A clear-eyed man does nothing in the dark. If I had been Yun-
men at the time, I would have thrust the staff into Xuefeng's breast. If deliberation did not come,
then afterward I would have had him bear it himself and personally take a bite. Why is it like this? This morning is the second
day of the second month, so for now we let the dragon raise its head.

cb:div.head: Case Twenty-Five: Yanguan's Rhinoceros Fan
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: The sea of worlds has no shore, yet it does not depart from this very place. Matters from before dust-kalpas are all present
right now. Try making it present itself face to face, and if you still cannot bring it forth in the wind, then
say, where does the fault lie?

Citing the case: One day Yanguan called to his attendant, Bring me the rhinoceros fan.
The attendant said: The fan is broken. Yanguan said: Since the fan is broken,In any case, it cannot lack himBefore it is raised, it is entirely complete
then give me back the rhinoceros. The attendant had no reply. ZifuDid you not see it plainly stated, why not take the hintThe fan is still here, though having it is like not having it
drew a circle, and within it wrote the character ox.Producing clever novelties, able to make and to sell

The Master said: In Yanguan County of Hangzhou, at Zhenguo Haichang Monastery, Chan Master Qi'an
was of the Tang imperial line. When Xuanzong was still hidden as a monk and was about to visit the Master, the Master foresaw it and told
the steward: Strictly forbid idle talk, speak only of ordinary matters. The emperor stayed a long time, then suddenly took leave. The Master
secretly said to him: The time has come. Do not remain stuck, coiled in the mud. He also entrusted him with the future affairs of the Buddha-Dharma.
After Wuzong's suppression of the teaching for six years, Xuanzong restored it, and the Master had great influence in this. The emperor was about to summon
the Master back to the palace for offerings, but the Master had long since passed away. The emperor, in grief, posthumously titled him Wukong Chan
Master. One day the Master called to the attendant: Bring me the rhinoceros fan. The attendant
said: The fan is broken. This too is plain and straightforward talk between father and son. The Master said: The fan
being broken, give me back the rhinoceros. This is the whole body entering the weeds for the sake of raising the child,
the occasion for it. The attendant had no reply, yet still by way of the crooked he struck the straight, only he himself did not know it.
Touzi answered in his place, saying: I do not refuse to bring it forth, but I fear the head and horns are not complete. Wansong said: Good
at patching and mending. Xuedou commented: I want precisely the incomplete head and horns. Wansong said:
That is an old thing from our own house. Shishuang said: If it is returned to the Reverend, then there will be none. Wan-
song said: He avoids it right in front of him. Xuedou commented: The rhinoceros is still there. Wansong
said: Clear eyes are hard to deceive. Baofu said: Reverend, you are advanced in years, better ask someone else. Wan-
song said: Much favor, deep resentment. Xuedou commented: A pity, toil without merit. Wan-
song said: A good heart gets no good return. Judging by this whole pack of old fellows, they only speak principles in vain.
As for the fan and the rhinoceros, in the end they still could not bring them out. Only Zifu drew a circle
and wrote the character ox in the middle. Fan and rhinoceros stood there fresh and whole, unmoving. Xuedou
commented: Why did he not bring it out just now? In truth, when he said the fan is broken, how
had even a hairbreadth been touched? And when he did not bring it out just now, what was ever lacking? Now bringing it
out adds nothing at all. Tiantong praised Zifu for bringing it out and using it intimately. Marking the
name and making the invitation, he specially prepares a serving of tea. The verse says:

lg.l:
The fan is broken, he asks for the rhinoceros; curled within the written character there is a source and reason.If one does it, do not stop at twoBetter than talking about
principle

Who knows the cassia wheel's thousand-year soul, marvelously making one bright point of autumn.Roots buried a thousand fathoms deep
In this very life the shoots appear

cb:div.p:
The Master said: Various places say the fan was painted with a rhinoceros gazing at the moon; others say the rhinoceros horn was used for
fan. Or some say it had a rhinoceros-horn handle; in either case it is called a rhinoceros fan. Yanguan
at that time opened a whole stall of them, each person producing one fan. Only Zifu, with his own hand,
made a new style, wholly distinct from the rest. The master once instructed the assembly, saying, When one sees Zifu’s temple-banner across the river,
one should turn back at once; under his heels he well deserves thirty blows. How much more if he crosses the river and comes here.
At that time, as soon as a monk stepped out, the master said, Not fit to talk with. In general, lecture halls value speech reaching its point;
the school gate values function reaching its point. The character crooked and curled has the deepest source. Xuansha
instructed the assembly, saying, I have the treasury of the true Dharma eye and entrust it to Mahākāśyapa; it is like
painting the moon. Caoxi raising the whisk is like pointing to the moon. The cassia wheel is the moon itself. In the Nirvāṇa
Sūtra, the World-Honored One emitted the moon of loving light; King Ajātaśatru’s feverish affliction was cooled. Therefore
it is said, Who knows that the cassia wheel’s thousand-year soul wondrously becomes one bright point of autumn. It may
be called: if the great handle is in hand, the clear wind constantly reaches the body. Of the rhinoceros fan case, the raised verses
are the most numerous, yet in truth they have never met Yanguan face to face. If Wansong were
the attendant, and heard him say, Bring me the rhinoceros fan, what feather, cattail, paper,
bamboo, silk gauze, plain silk, or palm fan—whatever came to hand, I would pick one up and hand it over. Why? Even if there are
a thousand clever kinds, in the end there are not two kinds of wind.

cb:div.head: Case Twenty-Six: Yangshan Points to Snow
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: Ice and frost are one color; snow and moon interweave their light. It freezes the Dharma-body to death and chills away
the fisherman. Is it still fit to be admired, or not?

Raised: Yangshan pointed to a snow lion and said, Is there anyone who can get past this color?
Yunmen said, At that moment I would have pushed down the snowYangshan, without noticing, takes a fall on level groundUnable to do anything to the boat, he smashes the bailer
lion. Xuedou said, He only knows how to push it down, not how to set it back up.Seeing injustice on the road, he draws his sword to help

The master said: The ancients, meeting the occasion and encountering things, revealed a great matter from before the empty kalpa.
The Lotus Sūtra says: Purely one, without mixture, fully endowed with the marks of pure white brahma-conduct.
Commentators say white is the root of all colors, and the One Vehicle is the source of all vehicles.
But they do not say that above white there is yet another matter. Therefore Yangshan pointed to the snow lion
and instructed the assembly, saying, Is there anyone who can pass beyond this color? Now white is the root of all colors;
the color of snow is utmost white. How can there still be one who gets past this color? Wansong says:
Since it is called a color, it must stand opposed to the eye. The color beyond white is only colorless—one that does not
stand opposed to the eye. That is why Yunmen said, At that moment I would have pushed it down. If you then head straight toward
the place of utmost white where there is no white and take hold there, you have fallen precisely into the formless realm. Xuedou
therefore separately pointed out a living road: at the very place of pushing down, he instead teaches setting it up.
Foyan said, If one supports and raises it here, what an orderly sequence of living activity. Wan-
song says: For other schools and different lineages, this must indeed be said. But you must further know the Dongshan
house style: at the very time of falling, it rises; at the very time of rising, it falls to the bottom. Only then
are rising and falling simultaneous; rising and falling do not stand. Then buy more straw sandals and wander thirty
years and still not see it. Fojue’s verse says: One color with nothing beyond, pointed out to people; in a white silver world
he repeatedly stretches it forth. Transcendently push it down and then set it up again—how can that compare with east wind and warming sun
bringing freshness? Wansong says: After sunrise, one whole muddle. A certain kind of student, seeing Yun-
men push down and Xuedou set up, immediately makes it into turning sharp exchanges and boundless great function.
Or seeing it, they understand toward the side of one color, or toward the side beyond color, taking that as the lifeblood of the school.
Fojue already stands as witness. If you still do not believe it, ask Tiantong again. He verses:

lg.l:
One falls, one rises—the snow-court lion; cautious in transgressing, yet cherishing benevolence.Just like a living one
One who knows the Dharma is wary

Bold in action and seeing what is right; clear light shines in the eyes, seeming to bewilder the home.Seeing injustice on the roadeast and west
are not distinguished

Turning the body in bright clarity, one still falls into position; in the patch-robed monk’s house there is nowhere to abide.Go up one more storyLet it pass for now
A whole life

Dying together, living together—what this, what that? Warm tidings break the plum, and springKnives and axes cannot split it open
reaches the cold branches.Obtained soul-returning incense
Cool gusts strip the leaves, and autumn clears the pooled waters.Come beat the poison-smeared drum

cb:div.p:
The master said: Yunmen’s one push-down and Xuedou’s one lift-up, with Yangshan pointing to the lion, all aim to pass beyond
this color. The three are the tripod’s legs; one missing will not do. The Three Mysteries and Three Essentials are all contained in
this. Yangshan feared people would sit in mere clarity—is this not cautious transgression with benevolence?
Pointing it out to people to make them pass this color—is this not bold action in seeing what is right? The Analects says:
To see what is right and not act is lack of courage. Yunmen again feared people would sit on the side of one color—
is this not cautious transgression with benevolence? So he immediately pushed it down, also boldly acting on what is right.
Xuedou feared people would only know how to push down—that too is cautious transgression with benevolence. Then he could further set it
back up: bold action in seeing what is right. Have you not seen Zhaozhou say, This old monk is not in bright
clarity. Truly, clear light shining in the eyes still bewilders the home. Turning within bright clarity still does not
avoid falling into position. If one only knows pushing down and not setting up, what use can he be? A true-color
patch-robed monk is like a pearl rolling on a tray: though sharing death and life, he does not abide in death and life.
Though there is no this and no that, provisionally he sets up this and that. In the final two lines, at times it is at the gate of the sun;
at times before the hall of the bright moon. The eternal vast sky, one morning’s wind
and moon; morning mushrooms and summer cicadas. Tell me: what season is it now? For now, follow
the old trees in the same cold leanness, then chase the spring wind into the burned scars.

cb:div.head: Case Twenty-Seven: Fayan Points to the Blind
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: With many teachers, lineages grow chaotic; when Dharma proliferates, craftiness arises. To treat illness where there is no illness, though it
injures compassion, if there is a branch, climb the branch—why not raise the story?

Raised: When Fayan pointed at the blind with his hand, two monks together wentDo not say you do not know, do not say you did not see
to roll it up. Fayan said, One gained, one lost.Traveling together but not in stepDividing the body beneath the sword

The master said: During morning attendance before the meal, Fayan pointed at the blind with his hand. Two monks together went to roll up
the blind. Fayan said, One gained, one lost. Dongchan Qi said, Venerable, how do you
understand it? Some say: because they did not understand the intent, they just went to roll the blind. Others say: the one pointed to
understood, and the one who went without being pointed to lost. Is understanding it this way acceptable or not?
Since this understanding is not allowed, then tell me: which one gained, and which one
lost? Wansong says: washing a clod of earth in mud. This is not only Fayan. One day Nanquan
said to a monk, Last night there was a good wind. The monk also said, Last night there was a good wind. Quan
said, It blew down a pine tree before the gate. The monk also said, It blew down a pine tree before the gate.
Next Nanquan said to another monk, Last night there was a good wind. The monk said, What kind of
wind? Quan said, It blew down a pine tree before the gate. The monk said, What pine tree?
Quan said, One gained, one lost. The pointing-to-the-blind story is an extremely skillful device for people. The two monks
rolling the blind—on each man’s own side, there are naturally two roads. Fayan first gave them
one seal; once sealed and fixed, there is no further change. On Fayan’s side, brightness and darkness interpenetrate.
The device of killing and giving life—the realm of a great person is known by Samantabhadra. Everywhere people take leaving gain and loss,
forgetting right and wrong, as supreme. Fayan runs into the sea of right and wrong and the pit of gain and loss
and makes a livelihood there. For only one beyond gain and loss can determine the gain and loss of all under heaven. Wan-
song too, speaking thus, also has gain and loss. You all, coming up like this, also
have gain and loss. Only one who deeply understands the roots of benefit and harm can weigh their loss
and gain. Sunge called this a ready-made gong’an, needing no further testing. But because he could not
cut it off upside down, Tiantong could not avoid drawing out litigation. He verses:

lg.l:
Pines are straight, thorns are crooked; cranes are long, ducks are short. In the age of Fuxi, people all forgot order andDo not touch it
disorder.The gourd ladle is chiseled out thick

In peace, the hidden dragon is in the deep; in ease, the soaringEven the Buddha eye cannot spy it
bird slips its bonds.Chopping off your head, you still cannot catch up

Then for no reason the ancestral teacher came from the West, and here gain and lossIf the upper beam is not straight
were split in two.the lower posts will be uneven

Tumbleweed turns with the wind; the empty boat cuts the current andKarmic consciousness is vast and dim, with no basis to rely on
reaches the shore.Setting sail with the current, it is hard to meet a swift favorable chance

Within this, keen patch-robed monks should look at Qing-A drunken street-curser, who dares take him head-on
liang’s methods.I have it here too, but such a person is rarely encountered

cb:div.p:
The master said: The ancients, having no choice, forcibly named the fundamental matter: pines straight, thorns crooked.
Swans white, crows black—this originally comes from the Śūraṅgama Sūtra. Tiantong transformed it into cranes long and short.
Zhuangzi says: What is long is not excessive, what is short is not insufficient. Therefore
though a duck’s legs are short, if you lengthen them there is sorrow; though a crane’s legs are long, if you cut them there is grief. A common saying
says: If you want no vexation, abide in your proper lot. Is it only the people of Fuxi’s age who forgot order
and disorder? Confucius said: In the West there is a great sage who governs not, yet there is no disorder. Order and disorder
are gain and loss. The Third Patriarch said: Put down gain and loss, right and wrong, all at once. In the Zhouyi,
the Qian hexagram says: First nine, hidden dragon, do not act. Ninth in the fourth, perhaps leaping in the deep. The Record of Qin Terraces says:
Wang Cizhong, in his youth, changed Cangjie’s ancient script into clerical script. Qin Shihuang
summoned him, but he would not rise. The emperor grew angry, caged him in a cart and sent him to the capital. On the road he transformed into a bird and escaped
his restraints, flying to the western mountains, where two feathers fell. Now in Guichuan county there is the Great Feather Mound—
that is the place. This verse praises the wind of high antiquity: appearing or withdrawing, acting or hiding, each settled in its
proper lot. Before the Buddha appeared in the world, how many sūtras, treatises, and gong’an were there? When the ancestral teacher
came from the West, there was at once gain and loss. Why not understand it before the blind was pointed to? Turning with
the wind, empty; cutting the current, reaching shore—these two lines point out the gain and loss of the two monks. Tian-
tong’s effort is great and not easy. Saying it this way: if there were no living hand, how could one
kill a man? Therefore he again says: Keen patch-robed monks within this should look at Qingliang’s
methods. Tell me, by what command may one be thus? Wait till you have taken a beating,
then I will tell you.

cb:div.head: Case Twenty-Eight: Huguo’s Three Muddleings
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: A man who hangs not an inch of thread is exactly a naked ascetic outsider. A fellow who chews not
a grain of rice surely belongs to the scorched-faced king of ghosts. Even if he is born in a holy place, he still
cannot avoid the perilous fall from the top of the pole. Is there still any place to cover one’s shame?

Raised: A monk asked Huguo, What is it like when a crane stands on a withered pine? GuoStep by step, climbing high is easy
said, Under the ground, one whole muddle. The monk said, What about when dripping water drips into ice?Hard to let go, mind after mind.
Guo said, After sunrise, one whole muddle. The monk said, During the Huichang suppression, where did the good guardian gods of the DharmaThe dharma body has no covering and cannot withstand the cold.When the snow melts, it is revealed.
A dead man comes.
go? Guo said, At the temple gate, the two of them were one whole muddle.If you point, you do not reach it.If you reach it, then
you do not point.

The master said: Huguo Jingguo Great Master of Suicheng Mountain in Suizhou, personal name Shoucheng, together with
the Second-Generation Yanhua Great Master, personal name Zhiyuan, were both at Baozi in Hunan. Baozi ascended
the seat. The master asked, What is true suchness Buddha-nature? Baozi said, Who does not have it? He withdrew from interview.
The head monk asked, Do you understand the abbot’s words just now? The master said, I do not.
The head monk said, The abbot is so compassionate—why do you not understand? True suchness Buddha-
nature, who lacks it? Even the four kinds of birth and the six paths all fully possess it. The master said, Thanks
to the head monk for explaining it for me. Jingguo, standing nearby, ground his teeth and said, This old fellow himself
has no eyes, and he further blinds others. Then he called the master and asked, What did the head monk just now
say? The master said, I did not understand then, and he explained it for me, exactly as stated above.
Jingguo said, Venerable, the Buddha-Dharma is not this kind of principle. If you do not believe it,
go ask the abbot. The master went up to the abbot and fully recounted the earlier explanation. Baozi also said, Buddha-
Dharma is not this kind of principle. The master said, I just asked the third seat, and he also
did not approve, so he told me to come ask. I only hope, out of compassion, you will decide and break it open for me. Baozi said, You
go ask the third seat. The master came down and bowed to ask. Jingguo said, Just
ask. The master then asked, What is true suchness Buddha-nature? Jingguo said, Who has it?
At these words the master awakened in accord. He bowed again in thanks and then said, Whether the head monk remains in the assembly
or goes forth to head a temple, I vow to assist him. Later they succeeded one another as abbots. This story the various houses
call Huguo’s Three Muddles, paired with Baofu’s Four Deceptions. For those who have not yet
passed the barrier, it is extremely hard to approach. Have you not seen a monk ask Chan Master Yunju Jian, When alone lodging on a solitary
peak, how is it? Yunju said, If you do not sleep in the nine-bay monks’ hall, who told
you to sleep alone on a solitary peak? Though this saying has no mystery, it is greatly opening. Raise this
one corner, and the other three can be seen. In Dongshan’s Inscription on the Mystery Within Mystery: Peaks and ridges stand lofty and distinct; the crane does not
the mechanism comes to rest. The numinous tree stands far off; the phoenix has nothing to perch on. Yet this monk asks, When a crane stands on a lone
pine, what then? This monk, having reached a stage in his dull seated effort, takes this bit of his original
native scenery and sticks it on his forehead, showing it to everyone he meets. He does not realize that at solitary peril one does not
stand; only then is the Way truly high. In Tiantai teaching this is called falling from the summit. Therefore Huguo said,
down on the ground, another bout of muddle. This monk did not make his living in the place of solitary peril,
and instead presented a place of ice-dry and snow-withered deadness. He also did not know that when all is dry and parched, there can still
arise moisture and savor; in a cold and desolate place, warmth and harmony are still required. So it is said, after the sun comes out
there is another bout of muddle. This monk was turned over twice on the spear-point; when he came up he was twice checked by Huguo
and cast down. Having no recourse, he then asked about the rise and decline of the teaching, raising this difficulty: during the Huichang suppression
of the sangha, where did the good guardian deities of the Dharma go? Emperor Wuzong of Tang loved immortality arts.
He purged 260,500 monks and nuns. In the eighth month of the fifth year of Huichang,
in the latter part of the month, he ordered them to return to lay life. The emperor took alchemical elixirs from adepts; his temperament grew more irritable and hasty, his joy
and anger were unpredictable. By early third month of the sixth year, in barely half a year, he died of elixir poisoning.
Xuanzong ascended the throne, and Buddhist temples increased again to more than triple. Judged from the standpoint of divine agency,
without Wuzong's small suppression, how could there have been Xuanzong's great revival? The expedient skillful means of the good deities
are not something ordinary people can reach. But from the patch-robed monk's point of view, the Dharma gate originally has no
rise or decline. How could the good deities come or go? Therefore it is said, at the temple gate there are two more bouts of
muddle. When Wansong talks this way, he has wrongly added footnotes for people again. And
do you not see? A monk asked, What about when mind and dharmas are both forgotten? Guo said, Not washing the face.
The monk said, What about when the moon sets in the cold pool? Guo said, Not washing the face. The monk said,
what about when both light and objects are forgotten? Guo said, Not washing the face. You must not explain it all away from the beginning
with annotations. If not annotating it, then what? Tiantong himself has a verse on the old case.

lg.l: A stalwart man, sharp and severe, with sideburns not yet autumn-white; if a man is not indignant, he will not be enfeoffed as a lord.Resenting that heaven cannot be reached.Greedy to make the journey too quickly.
Then turn and think of the man who hands down pure integrity in his house: he washes his ears by the stream and will not let the ox drink.Already too many lifetimes.At the end it goes too far.

cb:div.p:
The master said: The Third Patriarch said, The Great Way's body is broad, with neither easy nor hard; petty views are fox-like doubts.
The more hurried, the more delayed. Long ago two monks traveled together. The impatient one walked ahead and called to the one
behind, saying, Time flies swiftly, hurry up and come on. The monk behind said, The Great Way is vast and broad;
what is the rush for? Wansong once saw two verses written in the hand of National Teacher Yuantong Shan:
Time flies swiftly, hurry up and come on. If on the road you set your foot just right, the udumbara flower
blooms. The Great Way is vast and broad, what is the rush for? Let your belly go loose and at once it all gets wrapped up.
These two verses by National Teacher Yuantong Shan are like this monk asking in this way and Huguo answering in this way:
each has one eye. In Tiantong's verse, the first two lines are like sharp vigor greedily seeking
glory; the latter two lines are like retiring from office and withdrawing. In Later Han, Ban Chao was poor and often hired out
copying texts. Then he threw down his brush and said, A true man should emulate Zhang Qian and Fu Jiezi, establishing
merit in foreign lands and gain enfeoffment ten thousand li away. How can I long remain occupied with brush
and inkstone? Later he campaigned in the Western Regions and was enfeoffed as Marquis Dingyuan. This is a metaphor for this monk's three questions
poking his head out too far. In Later Han, Yang Zhen served as governor of Jingzhou; by nature he was upright and incorruptible,
accepting no private visits. His sons and grandsons ate vegetables and went on foot. Elder old friends wished to set up an estate
for the family, but Zhen would not permit it, saying, Let later generations call them descendants of a pure and honest official; by this
I bequeath to them, is that not generous enough? In the Records of the Historian, Xu You hid at Mount Ji, living by the mountain
for food and drinking from the river. Yao offered him the imperial throne; when Xu You heard this he went to the river and washed his ears. Chao
Fu was watering an ox and asked, Ordinary people wash their faces; you alone wash your ears. Xu You said, I heard Yao
asked me to be lord of the nine provinces, so I wash away right and wrong. Fu said, Timber of yuzhang grows
on high mountains, where craftsmen cannot reach it. If you wish to avoid the world, why not hide more deeply? Now you wander
among men vainly seeking fame and reputation. I was just about to let my ox drink downstream, but I fear you have fouled the ox's mouth, so I will lead it
upstream to drink. Tiantong uses Yang Zhen, Xu You, and Chao Fu, these three men, to praise the three bouts of muddle,
yet he is caught by Tong'an's saying: the muddy are muddy of themselves, the clear are clear; bodhi and affliction
are equally empty and level. This monk and Huguo have already put it all down; it has nothing to do with the rest of you.
Each of you may go as you please.

cb:div.head: Case Twenty-Nine: Fengxue's Iron Ox
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: Slow moves in chess and dull action rot away the axe handle. Eyes turn and the head gets confused, and the ladle handle is snatched away.
If you get struck into a ghost cave and grab fast to a dead snake's head, is there still any share in changing into a leopard
or not?

Cited case: Fengxue, in the government office at Yingzhou, ascended the hall and said: The Patriarch's mind-seal is shaped
like the mechanism of an iron ox: if you go, it seals you fast; if you stay, it shatters the seal. But if neither going nor staying, is sealing it right, or is not sealing it right? Then Elder Lupi came forth and asked: I have the iron ox mechanism.A needle cannot be stuck in.Drag the nostrils back.Cut off
the heel.Washing dirt in mud.
Clods.
I ask the master not to apply the seal. Fengxue said: Used to fishing for whales in the clear great deep,As if there were waves running against the current.
yet I lament a frog's gait in mud and sand. Lupi stood thinking. FengxueWaving the soul-summoning banner, jerking the breath bag.Already past the ghost gate pass.
shouted: Elder, why do you not speak? As Lupi hesitated, Fengxue struck him with a whisk and said: Do you still remember the opening words? Try raising them.Already at the cliff edge, and still given another push.After so much time, to what place
did he go and come?
Lupi was about to open his mouth; Fengxue struck him again with the whisk.For the sake of others, thoroughgoing, killing and seeing blood.Still not submitting to burning and burial.
The governor said: The Buddha-Dharma and the king's law are the same. FengxueStill short of thirty blows.If you do not know how to be an official, look at the neighboring prefecture's precedent.
said: What have you seen? The governor said: If one should cut off and does not cut off, it instead invites itsThen it is just right to give one whisk.
own disorder. Fengxue then descended from the seat.Cursing yourself and inviting it yourself.When satisfaction is strongest, that is exactly when to stop.

The master said: Layman Wujin cited this: Linji took leave of Weishan, and Yangshan attended at his side.
Wei said: How will this man's Dharma way be in later days? Yang said: In later days his Dharma way will greatly
flourish in Wu and Yue; when it meets the wind it will stop. Wei also asked, Who will succeed him? Yang said:
The time is far off and cannot yet be spoken of. Wei pressed him and said: I too wish to know.
Yang said: Does not the scripture say, With this deep mind one serves lands as numerous as dust motes; this is called
repaying the Buddha's kindness? The layman said: From this I know Fengxue was Yangshan's later body.
Fengxue first studied under Xuefeng for five years. One day he asked about a case from Linji's assembly involving two
head-seat monks who lifted their heads, looked at each other, and each gave a shout. A monk raised this and asked Linji, I do not know
whether they possessed the eye of host and guest. Linji said: Though it is so, host and guest are clearly distinct. Xuefeng
told this to Yantou and Qinshan. He set out on the road, but Linji had already passed away and he could not meet him.
Then he said: If you want to understand, ask his descendants. Fengxue raised this to Nanyuan Yong.
Nanyuan said: Xuefeng is an ancient Buddha. Later the master lived at Guanghui Chan Monastery on Fengxue Mountain in Ruzhou.
Amid the disorder of the Five Dynasties, the governor of Yingzhou invited the master to spend the summer in the government office. One
day the governor requested him to ascend the seat. He instructed the assembly, saying: The Patriarch's mind-seal is like an iron ox's
mechanism. It is not like the mysterious chanting and subtle lifting of stone men and wooden horses. Directly it is like an iron ox:
there is no place for you to draw near. The moment you go, it hooks and pulls you back; the moment you stay, it smashes you into a hundred mixed pieces.
But if neither going nor staying, is sealing right, or not sealing right? This can be called bait on the hook's tip.
Elder Lupi was also a descendant under Linji, so he turned the words around
and posed a question, truly a striking one: I have the iron ox mechanism; I ask the master
not to apply the seal. But Fengxue, standing on authority and acting, immediately said: Used to fishing for whales
in the clear great deep, yet I lament a frog's gait in mud and sand. Whales and great fish cross the seas.
In Zhuangzi, Prince Ren used fifty oxen as bait and once caught such a fish. Frog-gait
in mud and sand: one explanation says that in Emperor Wu of Han's time there was an official of Baoli who, by the Wowa waters, saw
a herd of wild horses, among them an extraordinary one coming to drink. So he made a clay man holding bridle and tether
nearby. After the horses grew accustomed to it, he replaced it with a man and captured the horse. Wanting to make the horse seem miraculous,
he claimed it came out of the water, and so it was passed down as a dragon breed. Fengxue means: the horse came from clear water
yet instead sprawls in mud and sand. This explanation is roundabout. In Fengxue's collected sayings it is plainly written as
frog-gait. Do you not see? After Xuedou had versified the rhinoceros fan story, he also said: If you want the clear
wind to rise again and horns to regrow, I ask a Chan traveler below to give a turning word. Then he said: The fan
is already broken; give me back my rhinoceros. At that time a monk came forth and said: Let the assembly go to the hall to practice.
Xuedou shouted: Casting the hook to catch leviathans, you caught a shrimp! Using these two
lines to match Fengxue's upper and lower lines, there is no doubt about the word frog. Lupi stood thinking; in the question
he had presented his mechanism, wanting to meet Fengxue face to face; but he was suddenly marked and struck, so he sought another way out,
trying a long thrust. But when he displayed his spear-technique, one roof-beam blow knocked him over. This was because he did not understand
seizing the mechanism and issuing command, the fault of not being able to exchange host and guest. The governor had long studied with Fengxue,
so it was not that he had no insight when he said, The Buddha-Dharma and the king's law are the same. Fengxue, having secured the south,
still worried about the northern frontier, and so asked the governor, What have you seen? Right here it would have been good
to redress Lupi's humiliation. But he only said, If one should cut off and does not cut off, it instead invites disorder.
That too can stand as talk. Fengxue, because he was a secular official,
swallowed his breath and voice and simply descended the seat. Two venerable adepts, one dragon and one snake, one hard and one soft, drummed out Linji's
house style. Then add Tiantong's judgment and response. The verse says:

lg.l:
The iron ox mechanism seals and shatters the seal, passing through the crown of Vairocana,Has it roared yet or not?Hook and awl in hand.
walking there.Raised up, it is not enough.

Then returning, he sits on the tongue of the transformation Buddha; Fengxue stands at the balance-beam.Matched below, it is more than enough.Worldly feeling sees coldness
and warmth.

Lupi falls defeated; beneath the staff and shout are lightning and flint-sparks.People's faces follow high and low.How could it allow discrimination and explanation?
Without waiting for it to settle.

Clearly, distinctly, like pearls on a tray; blink and raise your brows, and you have already missed it.Without being stirred, it turns by itself.
At the sound of agreement, strike at once.

cb:div.p:
The master said: The iron ox mechanism seals and shatters the seal. The Min king sent an envoy to deliver a vermilion credential.
When it arrived, Baofu ascended the hall and said: If you go, it seals you; if you stay, it shatters the seal. A monk said:
If neither going nor staying, why use the seal? Baofu struck him. The monk said: If so, then
ending up in the mountain ghost cave is entirely because of today. Baofu was silent. Wansong said: Alas,
a dragon's head and a snake's tail. If Fengxue had not transcended and realized the great gate of liberation of no-abiding and no-reliance,
he could not have passed through and walked upon Vairocana's crown. Emperor Suzong of Tang asked National Teacher Zhong:
What is the samadhi of no-contention? The National Teacher said: Patron, tread upon Vairocana's
crown and walk. This clarifies that beyond the dharma-body is not a matter of dead-tree stillness. Fengxue first
stood on this command, then came back into the gate of Buddha-work, wielding immense authority, yet he could not finish off
the case. Sweep away the calculations of ordinary and holy, sit cutting off the heads of reward and transformation Buddhas. In Linji's extensive
sayings he says: As for this mountain monk's seeing, I sit cutting off the crowns of reward and transformation Buddhas. Standing at the balance like this,
even if Lupi were a master, there would still be times he falls defeated. In India, non-Buddhist debaters set up doctrines;
when they failed, it was called defeat and downfall. They cut off head or arm to apologize for their inadequacy. This staff
and shout, this cudgel-hammer, these lightning-and-flint transformations of function, are all temporary scenes of light. Be careful
not to judge gain and loss, decide victory and defeat by them. Like pearls rolling on a tray, in a blink you miss
it. The master struck the Chan seat with the whisk and said: Finished.

cb:div.head: Case Thirty: Dasui and the Kalpa Fire
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: Cut off all oppositions, sit severing both ends, smash the mass of doubt. Then what need is there for
even one phrase? Chang'an is not a single inch away; Mount Tai weighs only three catties. Tell me then, by what
command does one dare to speak this way?

Cited case: A monk asked Dasui, When the kalpa fire blazes and the great chiliocosm is all destroyed, I do not know
whether this one is destroyed or not. Dasui said: Destroyed. The monk said: ThenA sorrowful person should not speak to a sorrowful person.Already, how could it be borne?
it goes along with it? Dasui said: It goes along with it. A monk askedIt can be tested right before your eyes.Not walking downhill, so give another push.
Longji, When the kalpa fire blazes and the great chiliocosm is all destroyed, I do not know whether this one is destroyed or
not. Longji said: Not destroyed. The monk said: Why is it not destroyed? Longji said: Because it is the same as the great chiliocosm.Those with the same illness worry for each other.Smash the tally and twist the nostrils around.Again in this
way.Cast from raw iron.

The master said: Chan Master Fazhen of Dasui in Yizhou—some say Xiyuan in Fuzhou, also
called Changqing—was a son of Da'an and a grandson in Dharma of Baizhang Dazhi. He studied with more than sixty
venerable teachers. In Weishan's assembly he worked as cook. Wei asked: You have been here
several years and cannot even bring forth a question. Dasui said: Tell me what to ask and then
I will ask it. Wei said: Why don't you ask, What is Buddha? Dasui covered Weishan's
mouth with his hand. Wei said: Later, when you have but a single roof tile over your head, you will not find even one person to sweep the ground.
Later he stayed by the roadside at Pengkou, boiling tea and receiving travelers for three years, then opened a monastery
and dwelt at Dasui. A monk asked: When the kalpa fire blazes and the great chiliocosm is all destroyed, I do not know whether this one
is destroyed or not. This question originally comes from the Humane King Sutra for Protecting the Nation. King Spot-Foot believed the heterodox
teacher Luotuo's words and took the heads of a thousand kings, making licentious sacrifices in graveyards to Mahakala, the great
Black God, hoping to prolong his dynasty. King Puming asked for a one-day reprieve and, according to the Seven Buddhas'
rite, arranged a feast for a hundred Dharma masters. The foremost Dharma master spoke a verse for the king: The kalpa fire blazes,
the great chiliocosm is all destroyed. This verse has thirty-two lines in all. As the king went to his death he in turn recited it for the other
kings. Spot-Foot, full of doubt, also heard this verse, and his mind opened in understanding and awakening. He entrusted the kingdom to his
younger brother, left home, and attained forbearance. According to the teaching, the three-thousand great-thousand world system is one
formation and destruction. Emperor Wu of Han dug Kunming Pool and found ashes, and asked Dongfang Shuo. Shuo said,
Please ask a Daoist from the Western Regions. Later, in the Later Han under Emperor Ming, Matanga and Dharmaratna came.
When asked, they said they were kalpa-ashes. Yun'an asked a lecturer, When the fire disaster arises and the world and
empty space are burned, where are all those ashes to be placed? Few could answer. In Zhiguan it says: arhats are
like charcoal; pratyekabuddhas are like ash; bodhisattvas are like a little remaining ash; a Buddha is like the kalpa-fire,
with no charcoal and no ash. This question asks whether this thing is destroyed or not destroyed. Foguo said, This monk from the start
did not know where the point of the saying landed. But tell me, what is this thing? Dasui said, Destroyed.
This saying goes against the ordinary yet accords with the Way; it is extremely hard to chew through. The monk said, If so, then let it
go along with it. This monk is laughable too; he does not know how long ago he had already dropped the whip and fallen from the stirrup.
Dasui said, Let it go with it. Wansong said, The monk followed Dasui and went; Dasui followed the monk and
went. Xuedou and Master Xiushan were contemporaries. He had not seen Master Xiushan's reply. In the later section,
the saying not destroyed means the same as the great chiliocosm. He only praised Dasui's saying, saying: In the light of the kalpa-fire
he sets up the point of questioning; patchrobed monks are still stuck at a double barrier. Many people wrongly understand this line, saying
Dasui's destroyed is one barrier, and Master Xiushan's not destroyed is a second
barrier. But if you look at the earlier case, he was only praising Dasui alone. Xuedou had not seen Master Xiushan's
recorded sayings. The very wording of the question, this thing destroyed or not destroyed, is already two barriers.
Pitiful, with one line let it go with it, he toiled alone for ten thousand li, going and returning. This praises that
monk who did not understand Dasui's words and went straight to Shuzhou to ask Touzi. Touzi said, In Xichuan
an ancient Buddha has appeared in the world; go back there quickly. The monk returned, but Dasui had already passed away. The Tang monk Jing
Zun inscribed: Clearly there is no other Dharma; only Daoyin Nan was able. With one line let it go with it,
he sent a patchrobed monk running across a thousand mountains. Xuedou used the sense of this couplet and then praised with another couplet:
Cold crickets chirp among the stepside leaves; in the still night he bows before the shrine lamp. Chanting done, by the lonely window moon, he paces
back and forth, regret beyond bearing. Tiantong, in praising the old case, further added Master Xiushan's saying not destroyed
as the same as the great chiliocosm. Some editions read: because it is not the same as the great chiliocosm. This saying is best
to examine. To say same is fine; to say not same is also fine. Following error with error, there is no yes and no.
Master Xiushan also said, Destroyed too blocks and chokes people to death; not destroyed also blocks and chokes
people to death. Wansong said, Dasui saying destroyed also has a way out; Master Xiushan
saying not destroyed also has a way out. It is not something to be taken only as no meaning whatsoever, nor can it
be understood solely through emotional consciousness. Have you not seen Chan Master Zhiche of Jiangxi ask the Sixth Patriarch about the Nirvana
Sutra's meaning of permanence and impermanence? The Patriarch said, Impermanence is Buddha-nature; permanence is
the discriminating mind regarding good and evil and all dharmas. Zhiche said, The sutra says Buddha-nature
is permanent, yet you say impermanent. Good and evil dharmas and even the bodhi-mind
are all impermanent, yet you say permanent. This is contradictory and causes students
to give rise to doubts. The Patriarch said, If Buddha-nature were permanent, why speak further of good and evil dharmas?
My saying impermanent is precisely the Buddha's teaching of true permanence. And if all
dharmas were impermanent, then each thing would have a self-nature, capable of undergoing birth and death, and
the truly permanent nature would fail to pervade everywhere. My saying permanent is exactly the Buddha's teaching of true impermanence
in meaning. Beginners nowadays, seeing Dasui say destroyed, let it go with it, cannot avoid
confusion. If they also consult the Sixth Patriarch, they can be free of doubt. Besides, Dasui is Baizhang's true grandson in the Dharma,
and Master Xiushan is Dizang's direct son. They transcend feeling and leave views behind, yet bend themselves for people of this age. Xuedou
at that time only praised half; Tiantong today finally makes it complete. The verse says:

lg.l:
Destroyed or not destroyed, let it go with it; the line of the great chiliocosmEven the Buddha's hand cannot pick it out.Turned about within the vein of speech of a measureless great man.
within it has not the slightest hook or lock mechanism.It sticks to teeth and gums, and that is no small amount.

At their very feet, many are hindered by kudzu vines.Who told
you to sprout branches and trailing vines?
Understand or not understand, the clear matter is laid out with careful insistence.Mind busy, hands hurried.This is the blind man's fault, not the sun and moon's blame.

If one who knows the mind lifts it out, do not bargain; lose to me in the trade of one in his own line.The broker spots the peddler.In the hall
selling Yangzhou goods.

cb:div.p:
The master said, Xuedou praised this monk asking whether this thing is destroyed or not, saying patchrobed monks still
get stuck at a double barrier. Tiantong, at the start of his verse, says destroyed or not destroyed, but his meaning is different. He simultaneously
raises the questioning points of both monks and simultaneously gathers the points of reply from both masters, lifting them all before your face at once.
Only let it go with it. Great chiliocosm follows Dasui's words and brings out the hooked lines of the two masters.
It points out the two monks chasing lumps. Yunju instructed the assembly, Speech is like a drill, like pincers,
like a hook, like a lock. It must be made continuous and unbroken. Now in praising the two masters' replies,
he is direct and straightforward, not involving the slightest nicety. Yet what can be done? The various places have already been by kudzu vines
tripped up and thrown down. Zhen Dianxiong once visited Chan Master Zheng at Zhangjiang Monastery in Nanchang
and sat under him. One day he held up his robe, bared his shins, and slowly walked past. Zheng, surprised, asked him. Zhen said,
The front corridor and rear racks are all kudzu vines. I fear being tripped, that's all. Zheng burst into laughter.
Tiantong says, I have praised it this clearly and set it out. Do you understand or not? If you are
a seasoned adept, it is like trade in one's own line and needs no bargaining. Wansong today
only got scared into leaving home.

cb:div.head: Case Thirty-One: Yunmen and the Pillar
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: The one mechanism above—like a crane soaring into the sky. The single road before the sun—like a hawk passing over
Silla. Even if your eyes are like shooting stars, still your mouth is unavoidably like a flat eave. Tell me then, what
is the essential principle?

Raised: Yunmen posed the saying, The ancient Buddha and the pillar meet—what stage of
mechanism is this? The assembly had no reply. On their behalf he answered, Clouds rise on the south mountain; on the northIt has fallen into bits and pieces.Instead, study together with the dew pillar.
mountain rain falls.Old Zhang drinks the wine, old Li gets drunk.

The master said, In the original record, Yunmen asked a monk, The ancient Buddha and the pillar meet—what
stage of mechanism is this? The monk had no reply. Yunmen said, Ask me and I will tell you. The monk then asked.
Yunmen said, One strip of cord, thirty cash. The monk said, What is one strip of cord, thirty
cash? Yunmen said, Give him a beating. Replacing the earlier saying, he said: Clouds rise on the south mountain; on the north mountain
rain falls. Tang-Song Kaifu Jing was skilled in songs with the jiegu drum, and had a line: Clouds rise on the south mountain,
rain falls on the north mountain. He borrows and uses it, just like Guanyin Bodhisattva bringing money to buy gruel-cakes,
but when she puts down her hand it turns out to be steamed buns. It is entirely like Muzhou's sharp device: the cup
falls to the ground and the tray becomes seven pieces. How could explanation and verbal interpretation be allowed? Tiantong can
explain where explanation cannot reach, and speak where talk cannot arrive. The verse says:

lg.l:
A single divine radiance is from the first never concealed—it transcends the conditions of seeing.Above props up heaven, below props up earth.Pure nakedness, stark and bare.
It is, yet there is no it-is.In the fierce flames, do not blink.

It is fitting, yet there is no fitting—it goes beyond emotional measure.Beyond the edge of the sword-wheel,
do not turn your head.

The pollen of cliff flowers—thus, in the honeycomb it becomes honey; the sap of wild grasses—thus,Spiritual powers are vast.
at the musk deer's navel it becomes fragrance.Transformations have no fixed mode.

According to the kind, it is three feet or one zhang six.The main mountain is high, the table mountain low. The staff
is long; the whisk is short.
Brightly, plainly—everywhere it is encountered, it stands openly revealed.Pressing through the face, there is nowhere to dodge.

cb:div.p:
The master said, Yunmen said, Everyone fully possesses luminosity. When you look, you do not see it—dark,
dim, murky. He also said, Empty space cannot contain it; the great earth cannot bear it. In the Shurangama, this
seeing and its conditions are all the wondrous, pure, luminous body of bodhi. How within it could there be right
and not-right? In Sengzhao's Prajna Has No Knowing Treatise it says: If there is no fixed fittingness, then no thing is not
fitting. If there is no fixed is, then no thing is not is. Since no thing is not is, it is and yet no is.
Since no thing is not fitting, it is fitting and yet no fitting. Therefore the sutra says: One fully sees all dharmas and yet
there is nothing seen. This verse clearly relies on one sutra and one treatise, and covertly points to one who transcends feeling and leaves
views, where person and realm interpenetrate. Since the ancient Buddha meets with the pillar, naturally on the south mountain
clouds rise and on the north mountain rain falls. Bees gather flowers and make honey; musk deer eat grass and make
fragrance. High and low mountains and streams together turn the fundamental Dharma wheel; large and small scaled and furred creatures universally manifest
the samadhi of form-body. The elder Kusila saw the three-foot body and found it inexhaustible; the Boundless
Body Bodhisattva reached the highest realm and still there was surplus. There is no time when it is not present, no place where it does not
pervade. Therefore it says: Brightly, plainly, everywhere encountered, it stands openly revealed. The master again said, Do you see?
Blind!

cb:div.head: Case Thirty-Two: Yangshan on Mind and Object
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: The sea is the dragon's world, where hidden and manifest they roam at ease. The sky is the crane's homeland,
where flying and crying are free. Why then does a trapped fish stop in a pond, and a dull bird roost in reeds? Is there still
a place for calculating profit and loss?

Raised: Yangshan asked a monk, Where are you from? The monk said, From Youzhou. Yangshan said, Do you think of that place? The monk said, I always think of it. YangshanWith the door shut, they hold a brush-up meeting.Public verification.
Clear and plain.Just when you were about to forget it—what is familiar is hard to forget.
said, The one that thinks is mind; what is thought of is object. In that place, the mountains, rivers, and greatAll along, you set up again subject and object.
earth, towers and terraces, halls and pavilions, people, animals, and other things—turn back and think of the thinking mind itself: does it also have
that many varieties? The monk said, When I get here, I altogether do not seeThe benevolent one gives rise to distinctions on their own.
any. Yangshan said, At the stage of faith, yes; at the stage of person, not yet. The monk said, Does master have some other instruction? Yangshan said,There is still this.Before the courtyard, the remaining snow melts in the sun. In the room,
who will sweep away the red dust?So it comes like this.
To say there is another or there is none misses the mark. According to the place of your seeing, you have only attained oneShoot through the twofold barrier.
mystery. Sit down, wrap your robe around you, and later look for yourself.Already there is moonlight in the boat.Add to it the wind in the sail.

The master said, Yangshan once asked a monk, Where do you come from? The monk said, From Youzhou. Yangshan
said, I happen to want news from Youzhou. What is the price of rice in Youzhou? The monk said, When I came,
for no reason I passed through the market and stepped on and broke their bridge. Yangshan then let it rest. Yangshan,
the Little Shakyamuni, received people by more than one road. This koan is precisely where learners
gain the momentum to enter the gate, the place where they get strength in applying themselves. Yangshan asked the Youzhou monk, Do you still think of
that place? If at that time he had said I do not think, then what? Yangshan would surely
have had another strength there. But the monk instead said, I always think. Honest speech deserves repentance. Yangshan said,
The one that thinks is mind; what is thought of is object. Objects differ in a thousand ways. Does the thinking mind have
many forms too? Yunmen once said, Out of compassion, Yangshan spoke in a way that stoops into the weeds.
Truly, that monk was sharp as well, saying, When I get here, I altogether do not see
any. People nowadays, if they have not reached this state, then fine; but if they do, they immediately thump their chests and carry a board,
not knowing that delight in the road never reaches home in the end. Yangshan had once walked the road below the mountain,
so he pointed out another living road. In years past, before the monks' hall, while in samadhi,
at midnight Yangshan no longer saw mountains, rivers, the great earth, monasteries, people, and things, and even his own
body—everything was wholly like empty space. Next morning he told Guishan. Gui said, When I was with Bai-
zhang I attained this state too. It is the effect of dissolving and clearing through false thoughts. If later you
teach Dharma and someone passes beyond this, that would be impossible. Wansong said, Were it not Yangshan,
he would not have realized it; were it not Guishan, he would not have recognized it. The Shurangama Sutra says: When stirring thoughts are exhausted and floating imaginings
are dispelled, then in the enlightened luminous mind it is like removing dust and grime; one whole round of birth and death, beginning and end, is perfectly
illumined. This is called the exhaustion of the aggregate of conception. Such a person can transcend the turbidity of affliction. Looking at what it comes from,
the root is the interpenetration of false thoughts. Here again one sees the wondrous accord of the father and son, Gui and Yang,
with the Buddha-mind. One day Yangshan presented his understanding, saying, If you have me look for myself, when I arrive here
there is no complete stage, nor anything to cut off. Gui said, According to the place of your seeing, there is still Dharma
present; you have also not yet left mind and object. Yang said, Since there is no complete stage, where is there still mind
and object? Gui said, Just now, was that not the understanding you made? Yang said, Yes. Gui
said, If so, that fully constitutes the dharma of mind and object. How can you say there is none? Yangshan saw
that this monk had this in him, and according to principle judged: At the stage of faith, yes; at the stage of person,
not yet. Another edition says: At the stage of faith, attained; at the stage of practice, not yet much. In the Vajra
Samadhi Sutra there are the stages of faith, contemplation, cultivation, practice, and relinquishment. Here speaking of faith-
stage and person-stage may not be exactly the same. Master Zhugan Gui said, At the place of opening and closing the mouth,
he divides this side and that side; at the place of speech and no speech, he divides faith-stage and person-stage.
This too is another family's way. Guishan asked Yangshan, Ji, speak quickly—do not fall into
the realm of the skandhas. Yang said, Huiji does not establish even faith. Gui said, You trust and yet do not
establish; not trusting and not establishing—what then? Yang said, Huiji simply has no one else to trust. Gui said,
If so, then you are a fixed-nature shravaka. Yang said, Huiji does not even see Buddha.
Chan Master Haosheng of Qingju, in the Oxherding Pictures at chapter six, says: At the stage of faith
it gradually ripens, and false states are felt as remote. Though one discerns pure and defiled, like a sword cutting mud, there still remains the nose-
rope; it is not yet something to rely on with confidence. Therefore black and white are half and half. The verse says: Though the wild herding may be said to be
long, the hand holding the rope gradually loosens. Conduct and maintenance are not dark or confused; advancing practice does not depend on following along. In the pure
field there is broad delight; the long whip is still often held. Green mountains and fine fragrant grass—one flavor day by
to fill hunger. In the twelfth chapter, the human position is originally empty; body and mind are unattached; gain and loss are utterly
exhausted. The profoundly mysterious road is far beyond discrimination. As for the upward phrase, the moment one contrives or deliberates one at once
falls. The verse says: Delusion arises and vainly toils to watch and herd; the ox is not, the person is also not. In the very midst, delusive imaginings
form images. Above, there is subtle mystery. In the great sea, a tiny speck of dust arises; in the vast furnace, a flake of snow flies. When
meeting, if you seek understanding and interpretation, do not fall into your calculating mind. Wansong says: Yangshan said faith also does not
stand. Qingju said the human position is originally empty. If, within these two masters’ words, you can pick it out, then the position of faith and
the human position will be plainly visible. This is what is called stepping back and returning to oneself, ten thousand times not losing a single one
of them. A monk said: Master, is there not some other instruction? There is a thorn in rotten mud. Yang
said: To say there is another or there is not another already misses the mark. If there is, that is adding frost on top of snow; if there is not, then one dies
beneath the words. Therefore he pointed to one mystery and told him to see for himself. Yongquan said: Empty space is the seat,
the ten thousand practices are the robe. Some take seat to mean settling in Chan and quieting thought, and robe to mean patchwork cloak
covering the head. Some take seat to mean opening the hall and ascending the seat, and robe to mean Dharma robes adorning the body. All of these
have reason in them. But tell me: later, see for yourself. See what? Ask Tiantong.
Tiantong. The verse says:

lg.l: With nothing outside, it contains all; unobstructed, it is open and expansive; gatewalls stand sheer upon sheer,Great, nothing is not encompassed.Fine, nothing is not entered.Better not stick your head out.

bolts and locks layer upon layer. The wine guest is often dead drunk and lies down; though the farmer has eaten his fill, he stillNo need to snap a finger.Wake him and strike.
slumps.Bury them in one pit.

Bursting out into empty space, the wondrous-winged bird beats the wind; trampling over and overturningPierce open the blue vault of heaven.
the blue sea, thunder escorts the roaming dragon.Jingzhe, the second-month season.

cb:div.p:
The master said: Tiantong first praises turning back to reflect on that which can think; how could there be many kinds of it? Yunmen
said: If you understand, you understand wrapped right before your eyes; if you do not understand, it is still wrapped right before your eyes. This is
the meaning of nothing outside and containing all. Mountains, rivers, towers, pavilions, people, beasts, and all things, one and many without obstruction.
Person and object interpenetrate; this is unobstructed and open. National Teacher Shao said: On the peak of Penetrating Mystery
peak, it is not the human world. Outside mind there is no dharma. Before the eyes are green mountains. The peak of Penetrating Mystery
is the object thought of; not the human world is the mind that thinks. Outside mind there is no
dharma, so altogether nothing is seen as existent. Before the eyes are green mountains. With this single phrase, this monk and
National Teacher Shao are separated by the faith position and human position, by one mystery and three mysteries, so that indeed gatewalls
stand sheer, and locks and bars are layered deep; in the end they are hard to meet. He further praises: this monk’s understanding
is like a drunk guest and a slumped farmer. What Yangshan points to is like the wondrous-winged bird and the roaming dragon. In the Lotus
Sutra it says: Suppose a man goes to a close friend’s house, gets drunk, and lies down. At that time the close friend
has official business and must leave, so he ties a priceless jewel into the man’s garment. The man lies there drunk
and knows nothing at all. Xiaozhou said: I once heard that one full meal makes one forget a hundred hungers; today this mountain
monk’s own body is just so. Zhaozhou, with one full meal, forgot a hundred hungers, fit to receive the wondrous offerings of humans and gods.
This monk, full from eating, is a slumped farmer; even a drop of water is hard for him to digest. Clear-eyed people, discern this well. In Sanskrit,
Garuda, here called the Wondrous-Winged Bird, beats the wind and parts the sea, directly seizing and swallowing dragons. Confu
cius asked Lao Dan about rites, and afterward said that seeing Laozi was like seeing a dragon. A roaming dragon appears and vanishes amid cloud and
mist, not like those hidden in hibernation. This verse is still not the human position; it only attains one
mystery. Later, see for yourself. Why tell him to move his body and change his step? If there is only a road one can
go up, then even higher people can travel it.

cb:juan.cb:jhead:
Old Man Wansong’s commentary on Tiantong Jue’s verses on ancient cases, Congrong Hermitage Record, scroll
two

Old Man Wansong’s commentary on Tiantong Jue’s verses on ancient cases
Congrong Hermitage Record, three

body.byline: Collated by the later student Xingyi

cb:div.head: Case Thirty-Three: Sansheng’s Golden Scale
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Meeting the strong, then be weak; meeting the soft, then be hard. When two hard things strike each other, there will surely be
one wounded. But tell me, how does one turn and interchange?

Raised: Sansheng asked Xuefeng, A golden scale fish that has passed through the net, I do not yet know what it takes as food.
Feng said: Wait till you come out of the net, then I will tell you.Without waiting to lower the line, it hooks itself.When meeting people, speak only three parts.
Sheng said: A good teacher of fifteen hundred people, and you do not even recognize the topic. Feng said: This old monk is busy with the affairs of the abbacy.Vulture Peak
bestowing a prophecy was not like today.Seeing cheeks behind the head.

In recent times, Master Liao of Changlu was a fellow student with Tiantong and presided over seventeen hundred monks.
Together with Zhu’an Gui, he opened the porridge and passed the summer, dividing quarters and entering the room. Xuefeng and Sansheng, though in different
ages, share the same style. Dawei Zhe said: Sansheng can indeed be called one long accustomed to being a guest at Dragon Gate’s ten-thousand-fathom height.
Xuefeng was very much like Lord Mengchang opening his gates, how could he fear lofty guests? Sansheng set
forth a question-head; it was no hindrance to pluck a glue-basin out from a thicket of brambles. Xue
feng, already thirty paces away, watched you smear and entangle yourself, saying: Wait till you come out
of the net, then I will tell you. Strange indeed. It is exactly like a master player of go seeing several
moves ahead. Sansheng saw that this passage did not divide victory and defeat, so he took another route and
said: A good teacher of fifteen hundred people, and you do not even recognize the topic. He used the claws and fangs of the Dharma cave
to seize him alive on the spot. Xuefeng, calmly and unhurriedly, only said: This old monk’s abbacy
affairs are many. Baofu said: To contend is lacking; to yield is surplus. Xuedou said: A pity
he was let off; he deserved thirty blows. Yet even one blow of this staff cannot really be judged. Truly this was
