Manjushri has nowhere to stick an awl; Vimalakirti has no awl either. Baofu Zhan said:
Manjushri too is like covering his ears while stealing a bell; his strength exhausted at Wu River. Vimalakirti's single silence still had not
gone beyond the gate of expedients. Wansong said: It is hard for people to get out of right and wrong. He also said: Big or little Vimalakirti,
once seated by Manjushri, to this day cannot rise. Wansong said: If he wants to get up, what is
so hard? Just a slap. Langya Jue said: Manjushri's praise like that is just
ladling divination and listening for empty sounds. Vimalakirti was silent. All of you people, do not drill the turtle and strike
the tile. Wansong said: There is no shortage of made-up talk. Only Xuedou, at the point where Manjushri finished asking,
did not say "silent for a long while, seated in place"; he directly said, What did Vimalakirti say? Then again he said:
Exposed and broken through! Wansong said: He does not know how to play ghost; he appears in broad daylight. Tianyi
Huai versified: Vimalakirti was not silent, nor was it a long while; sitting there discussing becomes fault and blame. To this day
in all quarters, when people present this matter, they still say, "After a long while..." A monk asked a certain
master: In the records they often say "a long while" — who is this "Long While"? He answered: "Long
While" is Liang Ba's younger brother. Those who transmitted it took it as a joke. Tianyi's last two lines
are extremely steep and sharp: he said, In the case of the hair-blowing sword, cold light is born in the box; outsiders and heavenly demons are all
beheaded. Wansong said: The divine blade passes in secret; one does not feel pain or itch. Baiyun Duan's verse
says: One, two, hundreds, thousands, ten-thousands — bending the fingers to seek the wording, you cannot finish counting. For now,
leave it before the dark window; tomorrow I will reckon it again with you. Wansong said: What
idle effort! Tiantong, in his verse, uses Mazu's "head white, sea-head black" talk, and at the end
says: Majestically seated, he cuts off the road of tongues; he should laugh at old Vimalakirti of Vaisali. Today
Vimalakirti has come — no concern for face-to-face praise. The verse says:

lg.l: Manjusri asks about illness; old Vaisali. The gate of nonduality opens — watch the adept.In the way of benevolence and righteousnessIn the patchrobed monk's portion

Coarse stone outside, pure jade within — who can appreciate it? Forget what is before, lose what is after; do not sigh.Great eloquence seems slow of speechGreat wisdom
Seems foolish

Earnestly presenting the uncut jade — in the Chu court the man is maimed; glitteringly repaying with a pearl —Present straight and get back crooked
in Sui city, the severed snake.Cast a night-shining jewel before men and few will not grasp their swords

Do not point it out and break it — cut off blemish and flaw, the worldlyLuckily still wholeLet them point and judge as they please
air is wholly absent, yet still there is a little to compare.Looking at people by appearances misses by a lot

cb:div.p:
The master said: Wenshushili and Manshushili are just different Chinese renderings of the Sanskrit sound. Here it is rendered
Wonderful Auspiciousness. Vairali is the name of the city Vast Adornment. In Sengzhao's Treatise on Nameless Nirvana:
Shakyamuni closed his chamber in Magadha; Pure Name shut his mouth in Vaisali. Subhuti
proclaimed no-speaking to reveal the Way. Indra and Brahma ceased listening and flowers rained down. In all these, principle acted as
the spirit's charioteer, so the mouth was silent. How could this be called lack of eloquence? It is eloquence speaking what cannot be spoken.
The Yan stone that is next to jade is a local riverside stone now; it is also called jade-seizing stone.
Though Vimalakirti outwardly seemed tongue-tied, the eloquence of his not speaking was refined essence within. This is called
stone concealing jade. As for forgetting what is before and losing what is after: in Yongjia's Collection, in the Shamatha Verse, the
fourth says: When we now speak of knowing, there is no need to know knowing; just know, that is all. Then the former
does not connect to cessation, and the later does not draw up arising. Before and after are cut off; the middle stands alone. In the Ending of the Unending Lamp,
in the section on unclear Dharma heirs, there is Chan Master Guangzhi of Yimen Mountain in Kaifeng Prefecture,
whose taboo name was Bensong. There are no other recorded sayings; this whole passage is quoted. Lord Wen did not know it came from
Yongjia's Collection and thought Song had originated it, so I clarify it here, that students may know. This
forgetting what is before and losing what is after is exactly the Third Patriarch's Faith in Mind: the path of words is cut off,
not past, coming, or present. Hanzi says Bian He found an uncut jade in the valleys of Jing Mountain and Kun Ridge, and presented it
to King Li of Chu. The king said, It is a stone, and had one foot cut off. When King Wu took the throne he again
presented it, and again had one foot cut off. When King Wen ascended, He embraced the jade and wept at the foot of Jing Mountain.
The king summoned and asked him. He said: I do not resent the cutting off of my feet; I resent that true jade was taken for
common stone, and loyal service was taken as insolence. The king had the stone cut open, and it was indeed true jade. King Wen
sighed: Alas, my two former lords — easy to cut off a man's feet, hard to split a stone. Now
it truly is a jade disk, a national treasure. In the Records, Sui Marquis Zhu Yuanchang, traveling to Qi,
saw a severed snake about to die, so he washed and rubbed it with water, applied divine medicine to it, and
left. Suddenly one night a light appeared in the courtyard; he thought there were thieves, so he pressed his sword and looked.
Then he saw a snake pearl on the ground, and the snake departed. He knew it was the snake repaying kindness. Vimalakirti
threw himself across for the sake of the multitude, yet could not avoid trouble coming from his own gate. How could he bear Manjushri pointing it out and exposing
the spiritual flaw and blemish? Even if Tiantong says he appeared dwelling in worldly dust yet without worldly taint, still it
is covering the nose while stealing fragrance.

cb:div.cb:mulu: 49. Dongshan Makes an Offering to the Portrait
cb:div.head: Case Forty-Nine: Dongshan Makes an Offering to the Portrait
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: It cannot be sketched, it cannot be painted. Puhua then turns a somersault. Longya
only reveals half his body. After all, what kind of person is that?

Raised: While Dongshan was making offerings to Yunyan's portrait, he then brought up the earlier story of asking about the true likeness.Who says it is false
A monk asked: Yunyan said, Just this is it — what was the meaning? Dongshan said: At that time I almost misunderstood my late teacher's meaning. The monk said:Each time it is brought up, each time it is newFor now, rejoice
Do not mistake itMeasure others by yourself
I do not know whether Yunyan knew there is this or not. Dongshan said: If he did not know there is,Bend grass to measure the sky
how could he speak thus? If he knew there is, how could he consent to speak thus?The sun rises over connected mountainsThe full moon faces the door

The master said: When Dongshan took leave of Yunyan, he asked: After the master's passing, if someone asks
whether they can portray the master's true likeness, how should I answer? Yunyan paused a long while and said: Just this is it. Dongshan
sank into thought. Yunyan said: Acarya Jie, in undertaking this great matter, you must be very careful.
Dongshan also said nothing and simply left. Later, when crossing water and seeing his reflection, only then did he fully awaken. Then
he composed a verse: Most taboo is seeking from another; far, far it grows distant from me. I now alone
go on, and everywhere I meet him. He is now precisely me; I now am not he. One must
understand it in this way to accord with Thusness. When Dongshan was in the assembly making offerings to Yunyan's portrait,
after bringing up the earlier story of the true likeness, a monk asked about Yunyan's saying "Just this is it" and its meaning.
Dongshan said: At that time I almost misunderstood my late teacher's meaning. If one grasps it at "after a long while" and "just
this is it," that is merely passing along the title and official wording. Therefore he saw the reflection and knew the form;
crossing the water, only then he awakened. The monk said: I do not know whether Yunyan knew there is this or not. If one says he always
knew, that is just an attendant at his side. Did you not hear it said: only one who knows there is can begin to know how to revere
it? If one says he completely did not know, there is danger here: there is complete not-knowing-there-is,
there is knowing-there-is and then yet not knowing-there-is, and there is going from not-knowing-there-is to becoming knowing-there-is. Dongshan
said: If he did not know there is, how could he speak thus? If he knew there is, how could he consent to speak thus?
In the Huayan school it is said: principle is complete, words are partial; once words arise, principle is lost. This is layered mystery
upon further wonder, carrying both and harmonizing through, not one-sided and dry — a bloodline without leakage. Dongshan
at the end of Tang Dazhong first dwelt at Baiji in Xinfeng, and later moved to Dongshan in Gao'an of Yuzhang,
becoming the first generation there. Because he held a memorial feast for Yunyan, a monk asked: Master,
what instruction did you receive from your former teacher? Dongshan said: Though I was among him, I did not receive his
instruction. The monk said: Then why hold a memorial feast? Dongshan said: Even so,
how dare I go against him? The monk said: You began your career under Nanquan; why then do you instead hold the feast for Yun-
yan? Dongshan said: I do not esteem my former teacher's virtue or Buddha-Dharma; I only esteem that he
did not explain it fully for me. The monk said: You inherited your former teacher — do you approve of him or not?
Dongshan said: Half approve, half do not. The monk said: Why not fully approve? Dongshan said:
If I fully approved, I would betray my former teacher. Wansong said: Yunyan spent twenty years
with Baizhang, yet inherited Yaoshan. Dongshan began under Nanquan, yet inherited Yunyan.
From one kind, different shoots turn luxuriant; the numinous root is dense and firm. With Furong, the lineage in the school
flourished; by Tiantong, literary brilliance was fully prepared. Which one is furnished with literary brilliance? The verse says:

lg.l:
How could he speak thus? At fifth watch the cock crows; the home woods dawn. How could heIn the dark, pull out the crossboneThe golden crow rises in the east
consent to speak thus?In brightness, sit on the tongue

The thousand-year crane and the cloud-pines grow old; the jeweled mirrorThe jade rabbit sinks in the west
clear and bright tests the upright and the partial.When the matter is exhausted, hit the vital point

The jade loom turns and shifts — see the gate of reaching both together.Mutual interchange, dark within the bright
The house style greatly flourishes; measured steps continue unbroken.The order from the Western Heaven is strict

Father and son adapt and transform; sound and light
vast and vast.Only when one surpasses the teacher is one fit to receive transmission

cb:div.p:
The master said: Dongshan instructed Caoshan, saying: From my former teacher Yunyan I personally received the seal of the Precious
Mirror Samadhi. The matter reaches its essential point. Now I transmit it to you; guard and uphold it well. The jeweled mirror
clear and bright tests upright and partial — is this not the cock crowing in the home woods, the crane old among cloud-pines, the verification of upright and partial?
Though the mirror is bright, it still has front and back. Only when the jade loom turns and shifts, mutually
interweaving, is there the method of mutual double brightness and double darkness, reaching both together. The Appended Phrases of the Yi says: When the way is exhausted,
then it changes; changing, it penetrates; penetrating, it endures. Dongshan and his descendants walked by rule and measured step, and to
this day the house style greatly flourishes — is this not the effect of a deep source and long current?

cb:div.head: Case Fifty: Xuefeng's What
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: The final phrase is what first reaches the impregnable barrier. Yantou, trusting himself, above did not
yield to his own teacher, below he did not defer to his Dharma brother. Was this merely forcing out complications,
or was there another mechanism?

Raised: When Xuefeng was living in a hermitage, two monks came to pay respects. Xuefeng, seeing themSeeking scent, chasing traces
come, braced the hermitage door with his hand, leaned out, and said: What is this? The monks also said: What is this? Xuefeng lowered his head and returned to the hermitage.This is still the posture of casting off the body and hiding the body
What is the posture thenSure enough, you do not recognize itDo not say silence is good
Later the monks went to Yantou. Yantou asked: Where do you come from? The monks said:Passing news and sending wordNo drilling, no boring through
From Lingnan. Yantou said: Did you visit Xuefeng? The monks said:This is north of the ridgeWhat is familiar is hard to forget
We did. Yantou said: What words were spoken? The monks raised the previous exchange. Yantou said: What did he say? The monks said: He said nothing; he loweredStill cannot hide itNo vinegar, no end to itOne word enters the public court
Eight oxen cannot drag it out of the gateBetter just lower your head and go out
his head and returned to the hermitage. Yantou said: Ah! At that time you should have told him the finalIf so, then you have never reached Xuefeng
phrase. If you had told him that, people in the world could do nothing to old Xue.And now, have you said it or not
At the end of the summer retreat, the monks again brought up the previous exchange and asked Yantou for instruction.Why not say, I am Old Xue myselfGood wine sobers a man slowly
Yantou said: Why didn't you ask earlier? The monks said: We did not dare do so lightly. YantouFond of drowsingCan weather the monastery grove well enough
said: Though Xuefeng and I are born on the same branch, we do not die on the same branch.
If you want to know the final phrase, it is just this.Those seeking something else go broke firstSteam it fresh and sell it hot

The master said: Yunyan studied alongside Daowu; Xuefeng studied alongside Yantou. A gentleman is not ashamed
to ask below. Today the ways of Yunyan and Xuefeng flourish greatly; this too is the leftover blessing of yielding oneself and letting others stand forth.
As for Yantou, his natural endowment was heroic and outstanding; he raised and spread Deshan's way.
He strode across the world; none dared meet his edge. This is because his insight was penetrating and his cultivation well matured,
and so he could be like this. Look at those two monks from Xuefeng's gate — arrowpoints facing
straight on — they too were traveling monks. Why then, all the way to the end of summer, did they still doubt the final
phrase? Only because their eyes were dull and their minds confused; right before them, they missed it. Yantou explained to them
so much reasoning: Xuefeng and I are born on the same branch, but we do not die on the same branch.
Though the one Dharma is not different, in three people there appears difference. These monks and Xuefeng, at one time,
all said, What is this? But when it came to the final phrase, even when told, they still did not know. Is this not
missing by a hair's breadth and losing by a thousand miles? Tell me, did these monks truly not understand, or did they want
to let this saying circulate? Weishan Zhe said: Big Xuefeng and Yantou were instead tested through by these
monks. Wansong said: A cold eye does not guard against it. Yantou and Xuefeng may still be
excused. Later he even criticized Deshan too as not understanding the final phrase — that is truly hard to
swallow. Therefore Tiantong brought it out in verse twice. The verse says:

lg.l:
Cutting, filing, carving, polishing; transformations mixed and mistaken — the staff that turned into a dragon at Gebei.If not for one thingOne does not gain one bit of wisdom
Already heard of crossing seas and piercing clouds

The Tao family’s hibernating shuttle; to live on the same branch has its number.Yet still seen leaning on walls and clinging to them
In worldly form they are close
To die on the same branch is not many; the final phrase is only this.In habits they are far apartFor now, believe half of it
A wind-borne boat carries the moon, floating on autumn waters.Above all avoid piling up roots

cb:div.p:
The master said: In the Mao Odes, Qiao of the Qi praises the virtue of Duke Wu. He was cultured and also able
to heed admonition, guarding himself with ritual. Therefore he could become minister under Zhou. In praise of him this poem was made.
It says: Look at that bend of the Qi, the green bamboo lush and graceful. There is a noble gentleman, as if cut,
as if filed, as if carved, as if polished. The note says: working bone is called cutting; working ivory is called filing; working jade is called
carving; working stone is called polishing. Deshan and Xuefeng, upon meeting Yantou, had the final phrase clarified. To this day
this story is widely circulated. It is the power of cutting and polishing into transformative penetration. Xuefeng is like the dragon-transforming
staff; this monk is like the hibernating shuttle. Yantou pointed it out, yet even now they do not know.
Therefore there are not many who die on the same branch. Some say Yantou is like the dragon-transforming
staff, and Xuefeng like the hibernating shuttle; please look carefully at the earlier story. Xuedou and Foguo,
using double illumination and double concealment, versified this case. Those not fully seasoned in practice do not know it. In the Eastern Han
Records of Magicians: Fei Changfang, a man of Runan, once served as a market clerk and met Hu Gong.
He cut a green bamboo staff, made it appear Changfang had hanged himself at home, and then they entered the deep mountains together.
Changfang failed to complete the Way and took leave to return. Hu Gong gave him a bamboo staff; riding it he reached home and threw it into Ge
Marsh. Changfang threw the staff into the marsh, and it changed into a dragon and departed. Also, when Jin’s Tao Kan was young,
he fished at Lei Marsh and netted a shuttle. He hung it on the wall. Later, when thunder and lightning sounded, it transformed into a dragon
and left. Xuefeng is like the staff; this monk is like the shuttle; Yantou is like a wind-boat carrying the moon.
What is Wansong like? A tea bottle from Gong County.

cb:div.cb:mulu: 51. Fayan: By Boat or By Land
cb:div.head: Case Fifty-One: Fayan, Boat or Land
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: In worldly dharmas, how many awaken; in Buddha-dharma, how many are confused.
If suddenly it is all struck into one piece, can confusion and awakening still adhere or not?

Cited: Fayan asked Senior Monk Jue, Did you come by boat or by land? Jue said,
It very much seems like there are two kinds
By boat. Fayan said, Where is the boat? Jue said, The boatDeeply discussing true reality, adept at explaining the essentials of DharmaI fear it is not solid
is in the river. After Jue withdrew, Fayan asked a nearby monk,Sure enough, there is a landing place
Tell me, did that monk just now have the eye, or not?What a pity

The master said: Huanglong Huitang asked. When Huang Luzhi was in difficulty, a man arrived.
Huitang asked, Who sent you? The man said, Scholar Ye of Dalin. He asked, Is there a letter?
The man said, Yes. He asked again, Where is the letter? The man immediately drew the back of his hand, pulled open his robe collar,
brought out the letter, and presented it to Huitang. Huitang said, To study the Way up to this field is acceptable. Huang
looked ashamed. Fayan asked Senior Monk Jue, By boat or by land? Jue said, By boat.
Fayan said, Where is the boat? Here, out of a hundred, ninety-nine
would display wit and show off. But he was a steady, solid, level man. How could he
meet you with weapons drawn? He said, The boat is in the river. Master Cizhou said, It is just
like setting an eight-legged creature on sandy ground; there is not the slightest instability
anywhere. After Jue withdrew, Fayan asked a nearby monk, Tell me, that monk just now, did he have
the eye or not? This one question greatly airs out confusion. If you say he had the eye, what
is so strange or mysterious? If you say he lacked the eye, what flaw did you see? Let
Tiantong judge it and see.

lg.l:
Water does not wash water; gold does not trade for gold. Ignoring coat color, one gets the horse.Cut off all specks, clear and pureRefined into one lump
It cannot be grasped by appearances

Dispensing with silk strings, one delights in the qin. Knotting cords and drawing trigrams bring so many affairs.It cannot be sought through soundWhen law comes forth
Craftiness arises
They utterly lose the pure simplicity of Pangu’s mind.Cleverness backfires into clumsiness

cb:div.p:
The master said: Water does not wash water; gold does not barter for gold. Buddha does not seek Buddha; Dharma does not speak
Dharma. This speaks of the divine steed while omitting black and yellow markings. One who gets the qin’s flavor forgets its strings
and tuning marks. In the Huainanzi, Duke Mu of Qin sent Bole and then Jiufang Yin to seek a horse. After three
months he returned and said, I found a horse at Shaqiu, a yellow stallion. But when the horse arrived it was a mare
and black. The duke said to Bole, The horse he sought, its coat color and sex, he did not know—
he has failed. Bole sighed deeply and said, Has he reached this point? What Yin observed was
the heavenly mechanism. He grasped the essence and forgot the coarse, saw the inner and forgot the outer.
Indeed it was a thousand-li horse. Jin’s Tao Qian, styled Yuanming, did not know how to play the qin, yet kept an unadorned qin
of one piece, without complete strings or tuning marks. He said, If one only gets the qin’s inner flavor, why toil over sounds on the strings?
The Yi says: In high antiquity they governed by knotted cords; later sages replaced this with written
tallies. It also says: In ancient times, when Baoxi ruled the world, he looked up and observed images
in heaven, looked down and observed patterns on earth, observed the markings of birds and beasts and what suited the land.
Taking what was near from the body and what was far from things, he then first drew the eight trigrams. Wansong said:
When Pangu first split heaven and earth, duality was already formed. Knotting cords and drawing trigrams further lost true
simplicity. Before Shakyamuni appeared in the world, before the patriarch came from the West, were there still ultimate truth and conventional truth,
worldly dharma and Buddha-dharma? Chan Master Qiju of Haihui in Shuzhou, after receiving the Dharma,
once went to Langya Jue. Jue asked, Reverend, where have you lately come from? Ju said,
Zhejiang. Jue said, By boat or by land? Ju said, By boat. Jue said, Where is the boat?
Ju said, In the river. Jue said, Without involving the road, in one phrase how
would you say it? Ju said, Bogus elders are as hemp and millet, and then he went down. Wan-
song said: A fine saying while walking. In the beginning, Dongshan asked a monk, Where do you come from?
The monk said, Ruzhou. Dongshan said, How far is it from here? The monk said, Seven hundred. Dongshan said,
How many straw sandals did you wear out? The monk said, Three. Dongshan said, Where did you get the money
to buy them? The monk said, Making hats. Dongshan said, Go to the hall and practice. The monk assented. Wansong
said: This is hands-and-eyes through the whole body, and still you cannot see through him. Tell me, where is this monk’s eye
located? Under the eyebrows.

cb:div.head: Case Fifty-Two: Caoshan and the Dharma Body
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: Those with wisdom understand by analogy. But when it comes to what cannot be compared
and is hard to match, how do you speak to them?

Cited: Caoshan asked Senior Monk De, The Buddha’s true dharma body is like empty space, responding to things and manifesting form like the moon in water. How would you speak of this respondingThe authorities do not
Allow even a needleYet in private carts and horses pass through
principle? De said, Like a donkey looking into a well. Caoshan said,With folded hands step forward and say yesFallen flowers, with intent, follow the flowing water
You speak well enough, but you have spoken only eighty percent. De said, Master,To exhaust the view for a thousand miles
then how about you? Caoshan said, Like the well looking at the donkey.Climb one more storyFlowing water, without intent, carries off the fallen flowers

The master said: Chan Master Benji of Caoshan in Yihuang, Fuzhou, was perhaps also named Danzhang; likely
a bestowed name or posthumous title. When he first left Dongshan, he entered Caoxi, paid homage at the patriarch’s stupa, and returned to Ji
shui in Jizhou. The assembly revered the mountain’s name and invited him to open the Dharma. The master modeled himself on Caoxi; wherever
he resided, he established Cao as the designation. In Dongshan’s lineage, it was under him that it flourished most, hence there is
the name Caodong. Caoshan asked Senior Monk De, The Buddha’s true dharma body is like empty
space, responding to things and manifesting form like the moon in water. How would you speak of the principle of response?
These four lines praising the Buddha originally come from an old version of the Golden Light Sutra. Since it is like empty
space, how does it respond to things? Juefan praised the Venerable Deva, saying: Manifesting in response to conditions,
not falling into thought. Therefore it is like dropping a needle into bowl-water. De said, Like a donkey looking
into a well. How could this be reached by emotional consciousness and calculation? Unless one has long been tempered and has a patch-robed monk’s
handle and grip, one is not permitted to reach this field. If one is of small capacity, without the upward turning-key,
I would still grant him full approval. Caoshan said, You speak broadly enough, but have spoken only eighty
percent. It is as if weighing by scales and measuring by pecks. De said, Then how about you, Master? This
one pressing question exhausted words and reached the end of principle. I dare say he could not utter that donkey-looking-in-the-well line.
Yet he calmly and gently only turned it over. This can be called extending it by analogy.
This is why he is called the source of the Caodong lineage. Tiantong loved these two turning phrases,
and in one sweep flipped them over and versified them. The verse says:

lg.l:
The donkey looks at the well, the well looks at the donkey; wisdom contains all without outside.Rising early in the fifth watchYet there are still travelers in the nightPatchrobed monks under heaven cannot leap
Out
Pure clarity contains more than enough; who can divide the seal behind the elbow?The ten thousand forms cannot escape shadow and substanceHeavenly eyes and dragon pupils cannot spy it out

In the house no books are stored; loom-thread is not hung on the shuttle-tip affair; literary colorsThe true pattern needs no vinegarThe flowers are not harmed
cross and crisscross, yet the meaning is naturally distinct.And the honey is made as well

cb:div.p:
The master said: Prajna is without knowing, yet there is nothing it does not know; therefore pure clarity overflows. In the Jin
Emperor Yuan’s first year of Yongchang, Wang Dun guarded Wuchang and raised troops to attack the court. Diao Xie
urged the emperor to execute the Wang clan completely. Wang Dao led his relatives and attendants to the palace terrace to await judgment. Zhou Yi
was about to enter court. Dao called to him, Boren, I burden you with a hundred mouths. Yi did not look back.
Once inside, he argued strongly for Dao’s loyalty and pleaded for him earnestly. When he came out, Dao was still at the gate.
Dao called again, but he did not answer. Then he said, This year I will kill the bandit slaves and take a gold seal as big as a peck,
and tie it behind my elbow. Soon after, he submitted another memorial clearly declaring Dao innocent. Dao, not knowing this, hated him deeply.
When Dun’s troops arrived, he asked Dao whether Zhou Yi could be spared. Dao gave no reply.
Dun then killed Yi. Later Dao reviewed the secretariat documents and saw Yi’s memorial saving him, and wept, saying:
In the dark beyond I have betrayed this good friend. In the Chan groves there is also the talisman behind the elbow. In the Spring and Autumn
Later Sayings, Zhao Jianzi told his sons: I have hidden a precious talisman behind the elbow on Mount Chang.
Whoever gets it first will be rewarded. The sons raced up the mountain and found nothing. Only Xiangzi’s mother returned and said,
I have already obtained the talisman. Others cannot share in it.
Jianzi asked her to explain. She said, From Mount Chang one can look down over Dai and take it.
Jianzi said, The mother is worthy, and established Xiangzi as heir. Yunyan instructed the assembly,
There is a family’s son—when questioned, there is nothing he cannot speak of. Dongshan
came forth and said, How many books are in his house? Yunyan said, Not a single character. Dong-
shan said, How then does he know so much? Yunyan said, He has never slept day or night. Dongshan again
said, Can I ask him about one matter or not? Yunyan said, If it can be said, then I do not say it. Behind the elbow,
who can divide the seal? It is the deeply hidden way of self-attainment; others cannot share it.
In the house no books are stored, yet there is such much knowing. To know by birth is highest; to know by learning
is secondary. This donkey looking at the well, the well looking at the donkey—do you permit dividing it up, grasping and surveying it in parts,
or not? Do you permit learned understanding and transmission, or not? Jiashan said, From hearing arises understanding.
Beneath the thought, painted colors; before the eyes, immediate beauty. Long stored, it becomes a sickness. Green mountains and white clouds
have never reached each other. Loom-thread is not hung on the shuttle-tip affair; literary colors cross and crisscross, while meaning
is naturally distinct. On the Jiaxiang road, the wise know it as sparse; auspicious grass without root, the worthy do not
prize it. At the end Tiantong wholly uses Jiashan’s couplet to clarify that this saying does not fall into
discursive thought, and its literary colors are complete of themselves. Tell me, possessing what samadhi does one become like this?
Just this lack of handle and grip—nothing at all can be done about it.

cb:div.head: Case Fifty-Three: Huangbo Eating Dregs
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: Facing the moment, one does not see Buddha. In great awakening, one does not keep the teacher. A sword that settles heaven and earth
has no human sentiment. In the device that seizes tigers and rhinos, holy understanding is forgotten. Tell me, what kind of person’s
maneuver is this?

Cited: Huangbo instructed the assembly, All of you are nothing but dregs-eating fellows.
Traveling about like this, where will there be a today? Do you still knowUnder Huangbo's gateIf now is not as before, later will not be as now
that in the Great Tang there are no Chan masters? Then a monk came forth and said, ButEyes high over the four seas
what about all the various places guiding students and leading assemblies? Huangbo said, It is not that there is no Chan—Huangbo himself is there too
I only say there are no teachers.At least half is saved

The master said: This case is abbreviated. If stated in full, it would say: One day, ascending the hall, he said, You
people—what is it you seek? Then he drove them off with a staff. The assembly did not disperse. The master then
said, All of you are dregs-eating fellows. In Tang times they liked to curse people as dregs-
eaters. Duke Huan of Qi was reading in the hall above, while Wheelwright Bian was hewing a wheel below the hall.
He set down his mallet and chisel and asked from below, May I ask what words my lord is reading? The duke
said, The classics of the sages. Bian said, Are the sages present? The duke said, They are dead. Bian said, Then
what my lord reads is the dregs and husks of the ancients. The duke said, I am reading, and a wheel-
wright dares to discuss it? If you can explain, all right; if not, you die. Bian said, I
look at it from my own work. If I hew slowly, it is easy but not firm; if too fast, it is hard
and does not fit. Neither slow nor fast, I get it in my hands and respond in my mind. The mouth
cannot speak it. There is a knack preserved in between. I cannot explain it to my son, and my son likewise
cannot be received from a minister. Therefore, at seventy years of age, Wheelwright Bian was still hewing wheels. What the men of old
died with, along with what could not be transmitted. Thus what my lord reads is the dregs of the ancients.
He also said: If you travel about like this, you only become a laughingstock to others. Whenever you see
a place with eight hundred or a thousand people, you go there at once. You must not merely seek bustle and excitement. When this mountain monk
was traveling, if I met some fellow down among the weeds and roots, I would give him a jab on the crown and see
how he was. If he knew pain and itch, he could be fed with a cloth sack full of rice. But if all are like
you all, taking things so easily, then where would there even be the matter of today? Since you all
call yourselves itinerant monks, you must bring forth some spirit. Do you know that in the Great Tang there are
no Chan masters? Since Huangbo, Yantou and Luoshan loved to enforce this command. In later times,
Fori and Master Yi of Qingshou who came from the north, up to their deaths found no one satisfactory. Rather than
have heirs without worthy people. Monk Jun of Xiangshan and his Dharma descendants also enforced this. Those who do not know
call it chest-pointing. Among them they want someone not indignant to come forward and take it up.
Sure enough, a monk came out and said: The venerable elders everywhere all gather communities and teach. Why then
say there are no Chan masters? Huangbo said: I do not say there is no Chan, only that there are no teachers. Guishan asked
Yangshan: How is it? Yangshan said: The king goose selects the milk; by nature it is not of the duck kind.
Gui said: This truly is hard to distinguish. Wuzu Jie cited the monk’s words: Thank you, master, you explained
the principle well. Wansong said: Raw words and dead phrases, cold lips and bland tongue. Shimen Cong
said: Huangbo’s instruction is indeed extraordinary, but as soon as he was pressed by this patched-robe monk, he lost
one eye. Wansong said: He changed out both of that monk’s eyes. Chengtian Zong said:
Wuzu Jie’s eye illumines the four quarters. Wansong said: It too is only one eye.
If you want to see Huangbo, still not yet possible. Wansong said: Indeed. If you want to uphold and establish the true
Dharma-eye treasury, you must have a master like Huangbo. Wansong said: Adding flowers atop brocade.
Cuiyan Zhen said: People everywhere discussing it then say Huangbo silenced this monk. Wan-
song said: They do not know he raised this monk up. Again they say Huangbo, when this monk came forward,
was unable to analyze it. Wansong said: Black and white become even clearer. What does this mean?
Cuiyan immediately gives rise to speculation. The mist-leopard moistens its fur and has never yet descended to feed; the courtyard bird nurses
its courage, in the end waiting to startle men. Wansong said: Watch in five more days. Huangbo also said: Venerable
monks, do you not see? Under Master Ma there were more than eighty people. Sitting in their training seats, every one of them
rolled on busily, but those who got the Master’s true eye were only two or three. Only Guizong
came somewhat close. A renunciant must know the inherited matter from above. For example, under the Fourth
Patriarch, Master Niutou Rong spoke crosswise and lengthwise, yet still did not know the upward turning
hinge. With this eye and brain, only then can one distinguish heterodox and orthodox lineages. Briefly I cite this much.
If you want to see the beginning and end of this saying, afterward there are still over a hundred more words. This is the very
first discourse he gave upon appearing in the world to instruct people, so it flourished everywhere. Xuedou versified it, and Foguo
commented on it most fully, yet the main intent of the original hall talk was still lacking. Tiantong’s verse brings it out,
bringing it to utter perfection. The verse says:

lg.l:
Branching paths and dyed silk are far too busy; leaves linked to flowers ruin the ancestral line.When you know fewer matters, you have fewer troubles
Where you know many people, there is much right and wrong

Wondrously grasping the south-pointing compass, the handle of creation; water and cloud are the implementsOnce power is in hand
in the potter’s shaping.Watch when the command is carried out

Cutting away the tangled fragments, trimming off the downy fuzz,A great elephant does not travel rabbit pathsGreat awakening is not bound by
Small details

star-balance and ornate mirror, jade ruler and golden knife, old Huangbo examines the autumnNot the slightest thing is obscuredMeasure and insight are deep and clear
hair-fine tip.You cannot fool him by even a speck
Seated, he cuts off the spring wind and does not let it rise high.Prepare for what is unforeseen

cb:div.p:
The master said: In Liezi, in the chapter Explaining Signs, Yangzi’s neighbor lost a sheep. He led along his
companions and also asked Yangzi’s servant boy to pursue it. Yangzi said: For one sheep, why are so many pursuing
it? The neighbor said: There are many branching roads. When they returned, he asked, Did you get the sheep? They said, It was lost.
He said: How was it lost? They said: Among the branching roads, there were more branches. In Mozi,
in the time of King Hui of Liang, a man of the Way went out and saw plain silk being dyed by various colors.
He lamented it, saying: People in their clarity are the same as the sage-body, but dwelling in corrupt customs, they are dyed and become
defiled. Branching paths and dyed silk are all too noisy and busy. The pronunciation note says lao-lao means clamorous bustle. It may also be written
with the character for toil. As for leaves linked and flowers joined, the First Patriarch Bodhidharma’s verse says: I originally came
to this land to speak Dharma and save deluded beings. One flower opens five leaves; the fruit naturally ripens.
One flower and five leaves is precisely branching paths and dyed silk. If in the Great Tang there were no
Chan masters, even Bodhidharma would have had no chance to emerge. The Record of the Source Mirror speaks of the south-pointing chariot, originally
to show the way to the lost. The gall-illuminating mirror is to examine the crooked. In Ancient and Modern Notes, the Yellow Emperor and
Chiyou fought at Zhuolu. Chiyou made a great fog, and all four directions were lost. The Emperor then made
a chariot that pointed south to guide them. The soldiers captured Chiyou and beheaded him. Thus it was named
the south-pointing chariot. As for pottery shaping, the Customs of Chenliu says that Shun made pottery by the riverbank; afterward
it became a clan name, now the surname Zhen, pronounced zhen. This is the dark furnace molding the myriad forms, the sea of wisdom
gathering all streams. Cutting off the tangled fragments and trimming away the down, it removes the branching-road
side paths and cuts the side-branches from the linked leaves. The Dipper is the balancing star, moving to regulate the four seasons.
The ornate mirror is the mirror of bright discernment. A scale determines light and heavy; a mirror can
distinguish beauty and ugliness. As for the jade ruler, the Records of Gathered Remnants says: When Yu toured Dragon Gate, eight spirits brought forth a jade
tablet and gave it to him, one chi and two cun long, by which he could measure heaven and earth. As for the golden knife,
in ancient times there were gold-inlaid knife coins and writings inscribed with a golden blade. This verse praises Huangbo for ranking the schools.
With scale and ruler in hand, and moreover keen hearing of ants fighting, clearly perceiving the finest autumn hair, he guards against the subtle and blocks
what gradually grows. Thus he sits cutting off the spring wind, not letting it rise high. In Great Sage Anyi’s poem on bamboo shoots,
It is best to bring axe and adze down at the root, lest more branches grow beyond the joints.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Old Man Wansong’s comments on Tiantong Jue’s Verses on Ancient Cases, Record of the Hermitage of Calm, volume three.

Old Man Wansong’s comments on Tiantong Jue’s Verses on Ancient Cases.
Record of the Hermitage of Calm, volume four.

body.byline: Collated by the later student Xingyi.

cb:div.head: Case Fifty-Four: Yunyan’s Great Compassion.
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Latticed and open on eight sides, unobstructed through all ten directions; everywhere emits light
that shakes the earth; at all times wondrous function and spiritual power. Tell me, how does it appear?

Raising the case: Yunyan asked Daowu, What does the Bodhisattva of Great Compassion do with so many hands and eyes?
Daowu said: It is like a person in the night reaching back with the hand to feel for a pillow. Yunyan said: I understand. Daowu said: How do you understand? Yunyan said: The whole body is hands and eyes. Daowu said: You have said it, butAsking like this, what are you afterOnce it reaches spiritual powers
It is not a small matterFor now, do not feign understandingSure enough
Cannot let it passThere is no gap anywhere
you only got eighty percent. Yunyan said: Elder brother, how about you?My tongue is shortWhen the principle is long, it comes together
Daowu said: The entire being is hands and eyes.There is no obstruction anywhere

The master said: Li Ao asked Ehu, What does Great Compassion use a thousand hands and eyes for? Ehu said:
What does the emperor use you for? Formerly there was a blind mountain man who sold fortunes. After rain, on muddy
roads, he wore bright white shoes into the market. People asked: Mountain man, you are blind. How is it that mud does not
stain your shoes? The mountain man raised his staff and said: At the tip of the staff there are eyes. Taking this mountain man
as proof, when feeling for a pillow at night, there are eyes in the hand. When eating, there are eyes on the tongue.
When listening to speech and recognizing a person, there are eyes in the ears. Su Zizhan, when speaking with a deaf man,
simply wrote characters. Then he laughed and said: He and I are both unusual people. I use my hand
as a mouth; he uses his eyes as ears. The Buddha spoke of the six faculties functioning interchangeably. Believe it without doubt.
Layman Wujin, in the inscription for the Great Compassion Hall at Ziyan in Luzhou, cites the Great Compassion Sutra and the Leng-
yan Sutra in greatest detail. I once saw an account saying Great Compassion was formerly Princess Miaoshan,
which a heavenly being told to Vinaya Master Xuan. Yet the thirty-two responses and the hundred million transformation
bodies also differ according to what is seen, and each account has its own basis. Tianjue said: A thousand hands
indicate the many ways of guiding the lost and receiving beings. A thousand eyes indicate the breadth of shining light into darkness.
If there were no sentient beings and no defilements, then not even one finger would remain, much less thousands and myriads
of arms. If one glance were not complete, much less thousands and myriads of eyes. Whole body, entire being, what
need of must or need-not? It seems there is shallow and deep, but in truth there is no loss or gain. Yunju addressed the assembly, saying: This old
monk, twenty years ago when living at Sanfeng Hermitage, Xinghua came and said: I will borrow a question
for the moment as a sketching line. What about it? At that time this old monk’s faculties and thought were dull, and he could not answer.
He brought forth a marvelous question-head for me; I did not dare fail him. At that time
he said: I suppose the hermitage master cannot answer this saying. Better bow and withdraw. And
now, thinking it over, back then there was no need to say, Why must it be so? Wansong said: If you let it be easy,
it is too easily gotten. Later a fundraiser monk came to Xinghua’s place. Xinghua asked: When the mountain monk lived
at Sanfeng Hermitage, this old monk once questioned him and he could not respond. Can he now
answer or not? The fundraiser repeated the former story. Xinghua said: For twenty years Yunju only
managed to say Why must it be so? Xinghua is not like that. Better to say There is no need. Wan-
song said: Contesting the length of rabbit horns. Sansheng said: Yunju in twenty years came to say it,
yet he was still half a month behind Xinghua. Wansong said: Competing over the depth of sky-flowers’
colors. A monk asked Juefan: Are the teachings of the old worthies the same or different? Fan said:
The Buddha had a dull monk recite broom. One day he greatly awakened and gained great eloquence.
From this you should see the former worthies’ heart for beings. As for Tiantong’s part, how
is it? The verse says:

lg.l:
One opening, empty and penetrating; eight-sided lattices, clear and open. Without form, without partiality, spring entersVertically exhausting the three timesHorizontally pervading the ten directions
the pitch-pipes.Meeting the time, receiving blessing

Without lingering, without obstruction, the moon moves through the sky. Pure jewel-eyes, meritoriousLetting it fall naturally into the front stream
virtue-arms.Looking ahead and behind, picking east and gathering west

How does whole-body compare to entire-being? Here before us, handsCannot sort it out clearly
and eyes reveal the complete function.The thief's loot is already exposed
Great function goes freely in all directions; what taboo could there be?Neither permissible nor not permissible

cb:div.p:
The master said: Tiantong says, from one opening being empty and penetrating, one then gains eight-sided openness.
For example, by willow ponds and flowered banks, in warm sun and gentle wind, where is spring? What
shape does it take? Yet it can respond to things and accord with the time, without lingering or obstruction, like the moon in the sky moving
by its own course. Know this: entire-being and whole-body. Who is it that reaches back to feel for the pillow?
In the puppet booth there must be one who pulls the strings. The Lengyan says there are eighty-four
thousand pure jeweled eyes, eighty-four thousand mudra arms, and eighty-four thousand
heads of Shakra. Xinghua, after falling from a horse and breaking his arm, versified: Great Compassion Bodhisattva has
a thousand hands; what real man does not have them? Tell me, which one is the hand-and-eye of the entire being?
Hand and eye. The master pried open his eye with his hand and said: Cat.

cb:div.head: Case Fifty-Five: Xuefeng the Rice-Cook.
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Ice is colder than water; indigo blue comes from indigo. Only when one surpasses the teacher can one be fit
to receive transmission. If a son does not match his father, the family declines in one generation. Tell me, the one who seizes the father’s
function, who is that person?

Raising the case: When Xuefeng was serving as rice-cook under Deshan, one day the meal was late. DeshanIf young and not diligent
carried his bowl to the Dharma hall. Xuefeng said: This old fellow, the bell has not rung and the drum has notOld yet the mind does not rest
sounded, so where are you taking your bowl? Deshan then returned to the abbot’s quarters. Xuefeng told Yantou. Yantou said: Big old Deshan does not understandTeach the child and he learns to curse his motherAll lies in
What is not spokenThe house is turned back into disorder
the final phrase. Deshan heard of it and had the attendant call Yantou, asking: YouA father shields his son, and straightness is right in it
do not approve of this old monk? Yantou then explained his meaning, and Deshan thereupon
Pour oil to put out a firePrivate whispers among men are heard in heaven like thunder
let it rest. The next day, when he ascended the hall, it was indeed unlike usual. Yan-Sure enough, you do not understandTurn the rudder with the wind
tou clapped and laughed, saying: Good! The old fellow understands the final phrase. Hereafter the people under heavenFamily shame spread outside
will have no way to do anything to him.Why is your nose in my hands

The master said: Xuefeng lowered his head and returned to the hut; Deshan immediately returned to the abbot’s quarters. This is best investigated.
Yantou secretly revealed his meaning. Now you tell me, what did he say? Deshan again let it rest.
It may be said: When they met, they did not bring it out; once the intent was raised, they knew it was there. When Deshan on the next day
ascended the hall, it truly was unlike usual. Yet this too was making use of a mistake upon a mistake. Yantou then went down to
the front of the monks’ hall, clapped his hands, and laughed loudly, saying: Good! The old fellow understands the final phrase. In the future
people under heaven will have no way with him. A grave warning: do not cover it over. Even though it is so,
he only got three years. Deshan indeed passed away after three years. Tianjue’s verse: Bell
and drum sounds sink as he returns holding the bowl; Yantou’s one pressing phrase was like thunder. Sure enough, he only got
alive after three years. Could it be he received that prediction? Wansong said: I know your underside is
exposed through and through. We once slept in the same bed. Mingzhao, speaking for Deshan, said: Tut, tut! No place to go.
No place to go. Wansong said: his nostrils are in another man's hands. Xuedou said: I once
heard tell of a one-eyed dragon; it turns out he really had only one eye. Little did they know, Deshan
was a big toothless tiger. If Yantou had not seen through him, how could there be a clear
difference between yesterday and today? People, do you want to understand the final phrase? It is only permitted that old
Hu know it, not that old Hu understand it. Half covered, half concealed, it leaks out. Yet they do not know. Da-
Weizhe said: Yantou was like a mountain rock splitting apart, so that beasts for a hundred li
hid their tracks. If not for Deshan's deep and lucid measure, how could yesterday and today
be different? Wansong said: though a toothless tiger, its claws and fangs are still there. Whenever Luzu saw
a monk come, he would immediately face the wall. When Nanquan heard of it, he said: I usually tell him, before the empty kalpa
take it up; realize it before the Buddha appeared in the world. Even then you do not get even half of one.
If he goes on like that, it will take till the year of the donkey. If you know Nanquan, then you will see Yantou, and further
walk arm in arm with Tiantong. The verse says:

lg.l:
As for the final phrase, did they understand or not? Deshan and his son were too vague.Here, whether you understand or not, your waist gets broken
Bright outside, not knowing the dark within

Among those seated there are also guests from Jiangnan; do not sing before peopleDo not say Qin has no men.
the partridge song.Stop right there.

cb:div.p:
The master said: The final phrase is like this, hard to understand. Stern and severe like Deshan, keen and brilliant like
Yantou, to this day they cannot fully distinguish and explain it. Have you not heard it said: Getting out is still easy;
shedding the body and speaking the Way is hard. In Zheng Gu's poem: Flower-and-moon towers stand near the nine streets; one clear song
overturns the golden jug. Among those seated there are also guests from Jiangnan; do not toward the spring wind sing the partridge
song. Tiantong used the last two lines, meaning there was no need to raise it again. After raising it,
what then? The master stepped down from the seat and returned to the abbot's quarters.

cb:div.cb:mulu: 56. Master Mi and the White Rabbit
cb:div.head: Case Fifty-Six: Master Mi and the White Rabbit
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Better to sink forever through kalpas than seek liberation from the various sages. Devadatta
in the Avici hell receives the bliss of the third dhyana. Udraka Ramaputra, in the Highest Heaven,
fell into the body of a flying civet. Tell me, where do benefit and harm lie?

Raised: Elder Master Mi was walking with Dongshan when they saw a white rabbit run across in front of them.
Mi said: Handsome indeed! Dongshan said: In what way? Mi said:Yet he runs off into the wild grass.No wonder you are late.
Like a commoner appointed prime minister. Dongshan said: At your age, and you say such a thing?Rising from the ground into the sky is easy.
Mi said: And you, what would you say? Dongshan said:You almost let it pass.Man has no mind to harm the tiger; the tiger has no intent to hurt man.
A noble house for generations, temporarily fallen into poverty.Coming down from the empty sky is hard.

The master raised another saying: Dongshan and Chan Master Mi of Shenshan in Tanzhou were crossing water. Dongshan asked:
What is the matter of crossing water? Mi said: The feet do not get wet. Dongshan said: At your age
and you say such words? Mi said: And you, what would you say? Dongshan said: The feet are not
wet. In the teachings there are two gates, nature and cultivation. In the Dong school this is called borrowing function to illuminate position. In general,
one awakens through cultivation, entering sagehood from the ordinary; a commoner in white is directly made chief minister. If
one awakens first and cultivates later, one enters the ordinary from sagehood; a line of nobility for generations is originally honorable. Though
scattered and fallen in ten thousand ways, the frame is still there. Therefore it is said: the parable of the poor son makes this
Way clear; the jewel-offering verse displays it openly. All of you, if you want to see where these two venerable ones
saw, look at how Tiantong receives and passes it through in one statement. The verse says:

lg.l:
Resisting frost and snow, he walks level into the clouds; Liu Xiahui left his state.In poverty, one cultivates oneself alone.In success, one aids all under heaven.
A bitter gourd is bitter to the root.
Xiangru crossed the bridge; Xiao and Cao by strategy could establish Han.A sweet melon is sweet to the stem.Sunflowers turn toward the sun.

Chao Fu and Xu You in body and mind wished to avoid Yao. Favor and disgrace startle like alarm, yet deep self-trust remains.Willow floss follows the wind.Awakening must be real awakening.
Inquiry must be real inquiry.
True feeling mingles traces, mixed among fishermen and woodcutters.Still not free of the sacred turtle dragging its tail.

cb:div.p:
The master said: Weeds crave rain and dew; pines and cypresses endure wind and frost. Only in the year's cold do we know
that the pine and cypress are the last to wither. This is the meaning of adapting to conditions without changing, and the talk of noble houses fallen low.
Only a person of great strength can bear this matter. To stride level into the clouds, in one leap directly
enter the Tathagata ground, is already too late. They do not realize that nobility for generations is originally
rich and honorable. In the Analects, Liu Xiahui was a magistrate and was dismissed three times. Someone said: Should you not
leave? He said: If I serve people by the straight Way, wherever I go would I not be dismissed three times? If by a crooked
way I serve people, why need I leave the land of my parents? Sima Xiangru, style name
Quanzi, lost his parents young. At nine he herded pigs for others. Hearing that Lin Xiangru became
a high minister, he changed his name to Xiangru. He abandoned the pigs and went to study. The pig owner beat him with a staff. The teacher asked
and recognized his worth, keeping him in a grass hut outside the gate. For ten years he had no books to read. Then he was sent away. Passing Sheng-
xian Bridge, he wrote on a pillar: If I do not ride a four-horse carriage, I will not cross this bridge again. Later he composed
the Zixu Fu. General Yang Deyi, lodging overnight in the palace, recited this fu. The emperor
said: I regret I did not live at the same time as this man. The general replied: He is now in Shu.
The emperor ordered him summoned. He rode with a four-horse carriage across the bridge and was enfeoffed as palace attendant. Xiao He and Cao
Can helped accomplish Emperor Gaozu of Han's enterprise. Chao Fu and Xu You avoided Yao, washing ears and watering oxen.
Laozi says: Favor and disgrace are like alarm; gaining them is alarming, losing them is alarming. The above
four pairs and eight matters are all one line for old Mi, one line for Dongshan. How subtle is Guifeng's
analogy: It is like an imperial clan fallen into lowliness, habit made into nature, later meeting recommendation and
promotion. Though restored to its original rank, the three virtues and six arts still require renewed training and effort
before all is complete. Even so, on Tiantong's side it still falls into stages. Have you not seen it said: Favor and disgrace
startle like alarm, yet there is deep self-trust; true feeling joins with traces, mixed among fishermen and woodcutters.

cb:div.head: Case Fifty-Seven: Yanyang's One Thing
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: To toy with the shadow wearies the form, not knowing the form is the root of shadow. To raise a voice to stop an echo,
not knowing the sound is the echo's root. If it is not riding an ox to look for the ox, then it is using a wedge to remove
a wedge. How can one avoid this fault?

Raised: Venerable Yanyang asked Zhaozhou: When one thing is not brought along, what then?
Zhou said: Put it down. Yan said: I did not bring a single thing.It is still extra.Even close-fitting clothes must be taken off.
What is there to put down? Zhou said: In that case, carry it away.A person does not know his own faults; an ox does not know its great strength.
。If called and he will not turn back, what can be done.

Venerable Yanyang of Xinxing in Wuning County, Hongzhou, first visited Zhaozhou and asked:
When one thing is not brought along, what then? This is like the monk who asked Baozi: When feeling arises, wisdom is obstructed;
as phases change, substance differs. What about before feeling arises? Ci said: Obstructed. The old bottom of Mu said:
If feeling has not yet arisen, what is obstructed? This is the same as One thing not brought along, put down
what exactly? Meng Balan, that fellow, is just the same. Zhou said: If you cannot put it down, then carry it
away. At these words he had great awakening. In Foguo's sayings it is cited, and Huanglong's verse says: One thing
not brought along, both shoulders cannot carry it. At once he suddenly knew the wrong and endless joy filled his heart. Venom and malice forgotten, he cherished snake and tigerGuo said, clear-eyed people are hard to fool.Step back and you fall into a deep
pit.Like a poor man finding treasure.When beginningless past karma is exhausted, there is purity.
as intimate friends. Through sparse hundreds and thousands of years, the clear wind has still not ceased. The masterEven those of other kinds alike understand.Who would not admire it.
where he lived always had a snake and a tiger, which he fed by hand. Therefore, like one who attained the fruit, the venerable
was so called. Zhaozhou the Ancient Buddha and this venerable one were men beyond measuring by sage or ordinary. One uttered
a word, one posed a question, and for a thousand ages thereafter they serve people as tortoise-shell and mirror. Tiantong saw that
in recent days among teachers and monks coarse minds are increasingly rampant, so he beat the grass to startle the snake. The verse says:

lg.l:
Failing to guard subtle conduct, he loses the first move; aware his mind is coarse, he is ashamed and strikes his head.Before black and white are distinguished, it is still the biased within the upright.
Head-bumping shame.Make a move in the tiger's mouth.

The game is broken; at his waist the axe-handle rots away. Washed clean of the ordinary bones,Tell me, what time is it now.
he wanders with immortals.Head light, eyes clear.

cb:div.p:
The master said: Old Wang Jiefu, holding a hidden saying on chess, said: He also dares not go first; this one
also dares not go first. Precisely because neither dares go first, therefore there is nothing to contend over. Precisely because there is no
contention, therefore one enters the unborn and undying. Chess is indeed the method of contending for first move. If you win, then
you win a first move; if you lose, you lose with a bumped head. Zhaozhou, before you had even placed a stone,
had already seen several moves ahead. Yanyang only kept leaping sideways and darting straight, needlessly
occupying a few lines, not knowing the axe-handle had already rotted. In Wang's Biographies of Immortals, during Jin Long-
an times, Wang Zhi of Xin'an County went gathering firewood to Xuanshi Slope and saw in a stone chamber
four boys playing chess. They gave Zhi something like jujubes; holding them in his mouth he did not hunger. When the game ended,
the axe-handle at his waist had rotted. His sleeves fluttered in the wind. By dusk he returned home, but already several dozen
years had passed. Zhaozhou's two turning words, Put it down and Carry it away, pull the sinews and draw the marrow.
They change bones and wash the guts; then one can walk hand in hand with Zhaozhou, lightly ascending through empty space.
Some say: Leisure and quiet are the true basis of the Way, carefree little immortals. Even so, do not take
no affair as truly no affair; often affairs arise from no affair.

cb:div.cb:mulu: 58. The Diamond Sutra on Being Despised
cb:div.head: Case Fifty-Eight: The Diamond Sutra on Being Despised
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: If you explain meaning by relying on the sutra, you wrong the Buddhas of the three times. If you depart from the sutra by one word, you turn out the same as
demon-talk. For one who neither gathers cause nor enters result, does he still receive karmic retribution or not?

Raised: The Diamond Sutra says, If a person is despised by others, this person's former-lifeI become a worm in dung.
karmic offenses should have caused a fall into evil paths; but because in this life people despise him, those former-life karmic offenses are thereby extinguished.This old monk goes in first.The monastery grove.
Donkeys and mules trample dragons and elephants.Where are you going.

The master said: Seeing and hearing are seeds; the eight hardships can surpass the stages of the ten grounds. Understanding and practice are in one's own body.
In one lifetime one may complete the fruit of vast kalpas. Guifeng classified this sutra as turning sin into Buddhahood.
Here bodhi and affliction are nondual; birth-and-death and nirvana are nondual. Prince Zhaoming of Liang
divided this sutra and called it the section on purifying karmic obstructions. Layman Fu's verse says: In former lives there were karmic obstacles;
today one upholds this sutra. Temporarily despised by others, the heavy is turned and becomes again
light. This is precisely explaining meaning by relying on the sutra. But in the later four lines there is already the breath of patch-robed monks:
It says: If one understands dependent arising, one can remove imagined feelings. Constantly relying on prajna
contemplation, why worry about not perfecting realization? The first four lines are the power of merit; the latter four are the power of contemplative practice.
The Sixth Patriarch's oral teaching says: The Buddha said, a person who upholds this sutra ought to receive from everyone
reverence and offerings; but because of heavy karmic obstacles from many lives, though in this life he upholds this
sutra, he is often despised by others and does not receive reverence and support. Because he upholds the sutra, he does not arouse
the marks of self and person. Without asking whether enemy or kin, he always acts respectfully. If offended, he does not contend. He always
cultivates prajna-paramita. Heavy sins through kalpas are all extinguished. Further, in terms of principle,
former life is just the previous thought of deluded mind; this life is just the later thought of awakened
mind. With the later thought of awakened mind despising the previous thought of deluded mind, delusion cannot abide. Therefore
it says former-life karmic offenses are thereby extinguished. Once deluded thoughts are extinguished, karmic offenses do not form.
Then one attains bodhi. These two explanations, by principle and by phenomena, both concern contemplative practice. A monk asked Yunju:
According to the teaching it says, This person's former-life karmic offenses should have fallen into evil paths, but because in this
life people despise him, those former-life karmic offenses are thereby extinguished. What is the meaning of this?
Ju said: In movement, one should fall into evil paths; in stillness, one is despised by others. Chongshou Chou
said: If there is dharma outside mind, one should fall into evil paths. If one guards and clings to oneself, one is despised by others.
Wansong said: These two old fellows have still not gotten rid of vulgarity. But tell me, on Tiantong's side
how is it? The verse says:

lg.l:
Clinging, clinging to merit and fault; sticky, sticky with cause and effect; outside the mirror madly running,Only excepting those who awaken suddenly.Even the Dharma does not comply.
Yajnadatta.Smoke rises under the feet.

A blow from the staff strikes the broken stove; the stove topples and shatters.A hundred mixed fragments.Spirit comes from
where, and from where does sagehood arrive.
They come to congratulate, yet he says: All along you have wronged me.I respectfully fear falling.Why not say so earlier.

cb:div.p:
The master said: Merit is upholding the sutra; fault is former karma. Once deluded causes are set up, they must summon deluded
effects. The Shurangama says: Have you not heard? In Sravasti city, Yajnadatta suddenly
one morning looked at his face in a mirror, loved that in the mirror his head, brows, and eyes were visible, and angrily blamed
his own head for not seeing its face and features, taking it for a demon, and ran off madly for no reason. What do you think?
Why did this man run mad without cause? Purna said: It was his mind gone mad;
there was no other reason. This is deludedly abandoning the real and clinging to the false. Prajna contemplation is the real. Merit,
fault, cause, and effect are false. When true wisdom appears before you, false karma is extinguished. Anuttara bodhi
is clearly originally complete. This is exactly the teaching's meaning. But on the side of patch-robed monks, how is it? Songyue's Stove-Breaker
monk, Pozuoduo: in a mountain hollow there was a temple, and in the hall stood a stove. Cooking and sacrificial slaughter never
ceased. Duo led his attendant into the temple, struck the stove three times with his staff and shouted: This stove
is made of mud and tiles. From where comes its sanctity? From where arises its spirit? In this way you butcher living beings,
taking life. He struck it three more times, and the stove then collapsed and broke apart. National Teacher Anguo gave him the title Stove-
Breaker. After a short while a man appeared, in blue robes and a high cap, bowed and said: I was originally the temple's
The spirit has long undergone karmic retribution. Today, receiving the master's teaching of the unborn Dharma, I am freed from rebirth here
and am born in heaven. I have come especially to give thanks. The master said, This is what you originally have; it is not something I forced
upon you by words. The spirit bowed twice again and vanished. Earth and tiles join to form it; whence does holiness come? No self and no person-
mark: this is prajna wisdom. Taking true wisdom for delusive karma, one has betrayed it all along. Today
we celebrate together. Slaughtering living beings in great numbers brings no merit at all. The old Chan master struck three times with his staff
and thereby gained rebirth in heaven. Bah. Ghosts fear evil men and cannot spread their hands; thieves rely on stolen goods
and are easily seized by the head.

cb:div.head: Case Fifty-Nine: Qinglin's Dead Snake
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: If he goes, he is kept; if he stays, he is sent away. If he neither goes nor stays, he has no
country. Where do you meet him? Everywhere, in every place. Tell me, what thing is it,
to be so marvelously strange?

Raised: A monk asked Qinglin, What about when a student goes straight on? LinThe moment you lift a step, it turns circuitous.
said, A dead snake lies across the main road; I advise you not to face it head-on. The monk said, When one faces itLong accustomed to poison.
head-on, then what? Lin said, You lose your life-root. The monk said, If one does not face itI grant you boldness.Just as expected.
head-on, then what? Lin said, There is no place to avoid it either. The monk said,
How could it depend only on you.Bumping and knocking into it.
At precisely such a time, then what? Lin said, You miss it. The monkDo not be in a hurry.Though it is a dead snake, if you know how to handle it, it lives.
said, I do not know where it has gone. Lin said, The grass is deep; there is nowhere to seekIf you do not believe it, search your robe.
it. The monk said, Reverend, you too must be on guard. LinVast above the head, vast beneath the feet.Come back.
clapped his hands and said, It is all the same poisonous vapour.I had thought besides Marquis White there was also Marquis Black.

Chan Master Shiqian of Qinglin was the third generation after Dongshan of Yunzhou. At first he came from Jiashan to study with
Wuben. Ben asked, Where have you recently come from? Lin said, Wuling. Ben said, The Way in Wuling,
how does it compare with here? Lin said, In barbarian lands, bamboo shoots sprout in winter. Ben said, Steam fragrant rice in another pot
and make an offering to this man. The master then withdrew. Ben said, This
fellow will later run the whole world ragged. While Lin was planting pines at Dongshan, there was
an old man Liu who asked the master for a verse. The master showed him this: Tapering, a little over three feet; lushly
dense, it covers the wild grass. I do not know what age's man will behold this old pine. The old man presented it to Wuben.
Ben said, Congratulations on your joy. This man is Dongshan's third generation. Lin took leave of
Wuben and went to Qingcuo Mountain in Shannan Prefecture, living in a hermitage for ten years. Suddenly he recalled Wuben's
parting words and said, One should benefit the many who are unenlightened; how could I be bound by petty details? So he went to
Suizhou. Responding to causes and conditions and the assembly's request, he stayed at Tumen Xiaoqinglin Monastery, and was therefore called Qing-
lin. He once said: All of you must directly investigate apart from mind, intention, and consciousness, beyond the paths of ordinary and holy
learning. Only then can you preserve and uphold it. If not so, you are not my descendants. A monk asked,
What about when a student goes straight on? This monk, from below the Hall of Great Compassion, wanted to go to the central capital.
He even boasted of knowing a direct and essential road. Little did he know that what is straightaway is already far too
roundabout. Lin blocked him with a dead snake across the road. The monk, heedless of danger
and death, said, What if one faces it head-on? He was already poisoned. Some say, Why did he not
use blows and shouts to enforce the command? But when did Lin ever let him off? He said, You lose your life-root. The monk
felt a little sting and ache, and sought a way out, saying, What if one does not face it head-on?
Lin said, There is no place to avoid it either. Even Qinglin himself cannot escape it. The monk
with sinews slack and strength exhausted said, At just such a time, when there is nothing to be done either way, what
is it then? Lin said, You miss it. A living man's method is seen here: able to send,
able to call, with both seizing and releasing distinguished. If it is put on your body, you cannot pluck it off; if for you
it is picked away, then there is somewhere it lands. The monk said, I do not know where it has gone. Lin
said, The grass is deep; there is nowhere to seek it. It is not that there is none; it is just not visible. This monk too
was strange, saying, Reverend, you too must be on guard. Qinglin, taking a dead snake, teased and
prodded this monk, but in the end got it wrapped round his waist and feet. Then he clapped once and said, A
single kind of poisonous vapour. Wansong said, It fumes the sky and scorches the earth. Wujindeng says, Qing-
lin's pivotal activity was swift and severe, not merely a brilliance of one time, but also a model for ages
to come. Wansong said, The spring wind's vexing tumult has not yet ceased. Look further: Tiantong blows flowers,
sways willows. The verse says:

lg.l:
The old boatmen secretly turn the rudder; the lone boat turns back at night; reed flowers on both banks,The night ravine hides the boat.At the clear source, set the oar.
like snow.Self and other mysteriously accord.

Misty waters, one whole river in autumn; wind-power supports the sail, moving on without the oar.Above and below commune in the dark.Following the current,
one attains the subtle.
Flute notes call the moon down over the blue islet.Letting it go, it falls into the stream ahead.

cb:div.p:
The master said: Chan Master Danxia Chun's verse says, The long river, limpid and clear, reflects moonlight; filling the eyes,
pure radiance is not yet home. I ask, where does the fishing boat go? Deep in the night it still lodges
among reed flowers. These two elders both praise a clear source and deep water, still poling a lone boat. Danxia uses
Xuedou's verse on Master Xuansha, which says: Originally a guest on a fishing boat, by chance he shaved off
beard and hair and put on the robe. He could not remain in the rank of patriarchs and buddhas; at night he still lodged
among reed flowers. Wansong said, One would think there was something different. Ancient and modern poetic talk says: In the river gorges they call
the stern oldman and pole-handlers the long-year old boatmen. Du's poem says: Shu salt and Wu hemp have from ancient times
flowed together; a ten-thousand-hu ship moves like wind. Amid the long songs of the old boatmen all year, in broad
daylight they spread coins upon the high waves. This matter is like a man piloting a boat: not clinging to either
bank, not staying midstream. Danxia lodges at night among reeds; Tiantong trusts the wind and plays the transverse flute.
But tell me, when turning the rudder and bringing the boat back, how is it? Deep at night he does not lodge in Reed Bay.
He strides far beyond the middle and the two ends.

cb:div.head: Case Sixty: Iron-Grindstone the Cow
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Nostrils flared high, each fully bears the mark of a true person. With heels firm and steady, who would
study old-woman Chan? Only by penetrating the mechanism with no clues or handles do you first see the hands
of a genuine master. Tell me, who is that person?

Raised: Liu Tiemo went to Weishan. Shan said, Old cow, you've come!The meeting is already done.
Mo said, Tomorrow at Mount Tai there is a great communal feast. Reverend, will you go?Stir up bees and flick scorpions.
Shan let his body drop and lay down. Mo then went out.The venomous fumes blaze like fire.Pulling away halfway down the road.One push and it turns.

The master said: Weishan called himself a water buffalo and named Tiemo a cow. Assigning names and
bestowing titles, this was masters recognizing each other. Though she was a nun, she had long studied under Weishan. Ten li from the mountain
she built a hermitage. One day she visited Zihu. Hu asked, Are you perhaps Liu Tiemo?
Mo said, I would not dare. Hu said, Turn left or turn right? Mo said, Reverend, don't topple over
yourself. Hu immediately struck her. See how with Weishan and him: when releasing, both release; when gathering, both
gather. Foguo called this a phrase across the body: the meaning connects, while the words are apart. If you want to know whether meaning and phrase
both arrive, then look further at Tiantong's full-bodied verse. The verse says:

lg.l:
After a hundred battles, merit accomplished, great peace in old age; at ease and gentle, who would still struggle bitterly for superiority?Settle the home and delight in the work.To spare others is not
foolishness.

Jade whip and golden horse, idle all day long; bright moon and clear wind, wealthy for a whole life.Though present, it is as if absent.Use and enjoyment
are inexhaustible.

cb:div.p:
The master said: Young monks talk much of Buddha; old generals do not discuss war. The wheat before the mountain is not yet sorted
into green and yellow; the rice of Luling is not known for price or profit. To speak further of Buddha-Dharma, who has ever even dreamed of it?
The biography of Chen Fan in the Eastern Han says: Fan could establish a noble reputation, challenge the corrupt customs in argument, and yet
race about amid peril and hardship, contending with mutilated men and rotten fellows. When these two met,
they did not establish reputations, did not race through dangerous straits; leisurely and plain, old and accomplished, rounded
and mature. Why can Tiantong not finish praising them? When it is attained, calculation is naturally forgotten; when it is used,
it takes no effort at all.

cb:div.head: Case Sixty-One: Qianfeng's One Stroke
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: Indirect speech is easy to understand; with one hand it can be handed over. Direct speech is hard to understand; with a crosswise stroke
it is broken open. I urge you not to use overly explicit words; when words come out too clearly, it becomes harder still. If you do not
believe it, raise it and see.

Raised: A monk asked Qianfeng, The Bhagavans of the ten directions have one road, the gate of nirvana. I do not
know where the roadhead is. Feng drew one stroke with his staff and said,A fast horse is not as good as a dull pit.
It is here. The monk raised this and asked Yunmen. Men said, The fanFor now, believe half of it.If doubtful, investigate further elsewhere.
leaps up to the Thirty-Three Heavens and strikes Indra's nostril. An East Sea carpA beggar's talk.
gets one whack, and rain pours down like an overturned basin. Do you understand? Do you understand?Explained like this, it is even harder to grasp.

The master said: Chan Master Qianfeng of Yuezhou was asked by a monk, The Bhagavans of the ten directions have one road,
the gate of nirvana; where is the roadhead? This question originally comes from the Surangama
Sutra, fascicle five: In this Abhidharma, the Bhagavans of the ten directions have one road, the gate of nirvana.
