when about to pass away said to the assembly, I obtained Tianlong's one-finger Zen; in a whole lifetime I never exhausted
it. Having said this, he passed away. Wansong said, Better to cut off the finger. Changqing, speaking in his place,
said, Fine food does not suit a full man. Wansong said, Not craving the taste of fragrant bait, this can
be called a dragon in a deep pool. Xuansha said, If I had seen it at the time, I would have snapped that finger off.
Wansong said, Not only would that redress the boy's grievance, it would also let later people vent their indignation.
Xuanjue said, Tell me, when Xuansha spoke like this, what was his meaning? Wansong
said, Sure enough, you are caught in doubt. Yunju Xi said, As for Xuansha speaking this way, did he approve
of him or not? If he approved, why speak of snapping the finger? If he did not approve, then where was Juzhi's
fault? Wansong said, The fault lies in approval and disapproval. Earlier Caoshan said,
Juzhi was crude where he took it up. He only recognized one function and one condition. One kind is
clapping hands and patting palms; that is his Nanyuan oddity. Wansong said, To separate milk from water
one must be a king goose. Xuanjue again said, Tell me, was Juzhi awakened or not? If
awakened, why say he was crude in taking it up? If not awakened, then why say he used one
finger Zen without exhausting it? Tell me, where is Caoshan's intent? Wansong
said, The tune is lofty and few can harmonize; it awaits one who knows the sound. Later Chan Master Jiashan Lai, at
Xitianing in Zhenfu, was asked, Where is Master Tieniu's stupa? Lai pointed with his hand.
On the spot he suddenly awakened, and composed a verse saying, Tieniu, Tieniu, seek no more
elsewhere. If someone asks me, I raise a finger. Wansong said, Though this is a crude taking-up,
still, at least it is not borrowed from the side. Have you not seen Mingzhao, the One-Eyed Dragon, ask Guotai
Master Shen, The ancients said Juzhi merely recited a three-line spell, and thereby gained the reputation of
surpassing everyone. How do you pick away the three-line spell? Tai raised one finger.
Zhao said, If not for today, how would I know this traveler from Guazhou? Wansong said, Even if
the symptoms differ, first one must treat the root. In the Cantongqi it says, Following words, one must understand
the source; do not set up rules by yourself. This may be called, In the deep night he recognizes the road he came by; without waiting
for dawn he leaves the pass. Foguo's verse says, How could the dynamic of question and answer be easily matched? Without
money it is hard to cut a fine figure. When there is something in the heart that cannot be spoken, one only amid the bustle
raises a finger. If you want to cut a fine figure and speak what is in the heart, then study Tiantong further,
Master Tiantong. He versified:

lg.l:
Old man Juzhi's one-finger Zen, thirty years and still not worn out.Draw back the donkey's hoofTo this day, hands are still raised helplessly
Strike wildly

Truly there is a wayfarer's art beyond the world; before his eyes no vulgar thing remains.It cannot be used here
Still complaining it is too little

What is attained is very simple, yet its application grows ever vast; the seas of worlds in the great thousandFilling and blocking heaven and earthIt does not take a single squeeze
are drunk from the tip of a hair.Leaving not a trickle or drop

Innumerable scaled dragons, into whose hands do they fall? Treasure well Master Ren'sTiantong is still here
grasp of the fishing rod.No harm in a startling method
The master again raised one finger and said, Look.Shame and alarm enough to kill a man

cb:div.p:
The master said, For ten thousand ages always empty, in one morning wind and moon; how could it be only thirty years without wearing
out? In the Great Master chapter of Zhuangzi, Confucius said, He wanders beyond the bounds.
I, Qiu, wander within the bounds. Without an art beyond the bounds, how could one
see through the root of both worldly and world-transcending entirely upon one fingertip? An old poem says, Before the eyes
there are no vulgar things; though often ill, the body is light. Tiantong takes what is near from the body, using only
one finger. The way is simple and easy, essential and not complicated. In Vimalakirti, a hair swallowing the great ocean is called
the small inconceivable scripture. In Huayan, a mote containing the Dharma realm is called the great inconceivable scripture.
In the Surangama, on the tip of a single hair one can wholly contain the lands of the ten directions. It also says, On
one hair-tip appear jeweled king lands; seated within a tiny mote one turns the great Dharma wheel. In Zhuangzi,
Prince Ren made a huge hook and giant line, using fifty oxen as bait. He squatted at Kuaiji,
cast his rod into the eastern sea, and fished day after day. For a full year he caught no fish. Then a giant
fish swallowed it, dragging the huge hook down beneath the water. It thrashed and raised its fins; white waves
rose like mountains. The sea shook and surged; the sound matched spirits and gods, blazing across a thousand li. Prince Ren
caught that fish, cut it up and dried it. From east of the Zhe River to north of Cangwu,
no one failed to be filled with that fish. As is said, When the fishing rods are chopped away, plant bamboo again; do not count
the labor and stop once you gain it. Later he received the finger-cut boy. Guotai Tao and Biefeng met;
Jiashan Lai mistakenly entered Peach Blossom Spring. Today, after Tiantong's verse, again
he raised one finger and said, Look. Master Dayin of Baishan said, Big or small, Tiantong follows
behind people's heels. Master Wuzu Yan cited: A monk asked Touzi, What is
the Tamer with ten bodies? He got down from the Chan seat and stood. If a monk asks me, I too get down
from the Chan seat and stand. Why then is it just copying the cat from the pattern? Wait till I reckon it out, then
I will tell you. Thus we know, Juzhi's finger, one time drinks water, one time gets
choked. What else can it make Wansong do in response? He threw down the whisk and said, One
may let all quarters examine and judge.

cb:div.head: Case Eighty-Five: The National Teacher's Stupa Pattern
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: There is a hammer that shatters empty space, a technique that splits Mount Hua apart,
only then does one reach the place originally without seams or cracks, the place where no flaw is seen. But who is such a
person?

Raised: Emperor Suzong asked National Teacher Zhong, After a hundred years, what will be needed? The National Teacher said, Make an unseamed stupa for this old monk. The emperor said,Right now
It is not little eitherWhere do you set your hand to it
Please, Master, the pattern for the stupa. The National Teacher was silent a long while, then said, Do you understand?Sketched, it fails; painted, it does not come outHere, whether you understand or not, do not seek elsewhere
The emperor said, I do not understand. The National Teacher said, I have a Dharma-heir disciple, Danyuan,Still somewhat short
who does understand this matter. Later the emperor summoned Danyuan and asked, What does this mean?If ancestors fail to settle it, disaster reaches children and grandchildren
Yuan said, South of Xiang, north of Tan, in the middleA true sovereign does not forget the last chargeHeaven high, earth deep, sun on the left, moon on the right
there is gold filling a whole country; beneath the shadowless tree, a shared boat; atop the lapis hall, no knowing.Filling and blocking empty spaceSo dense the golden knife cannot cut it
OpenQuietly the curtain hangs, not revealing the face

The master said, National Teacher Huizhong of Guangzhai Monastery in the western capital, having received the mind-seal, lived in the south
at Dangzi Valley on White Cliff Mountain in Nanyang. For more than forty years he did not leave the mountain gate. His conduct in the Way
became known in the imperial capital. In the second year of Shangyuan under Tang Suzong, an imperial eunuch, Sun Chaojin, was ordered
to carry the edict and summon him to the capital, where he was treated with a master's honors. At first he stayed in the west Chan cloister of Qianfu Monastery.
When Daizong came to the throne, he was again invited to reside at Guangzhai Monastery. For sixteen years he adapted to occasions
and preached the Dharma. On the ninth day of the twelfth month in the tenth year of Dali, lying on his right side he passed away. He was posthumously titled
Chan Master Great Realization. Foguo said, Many people say the National Teacher's silence itself was
the stupa pattern. If you understand it that way, Bodhidharma's whole lineage is swept clean away. Then even a mute
would understand Zen. Once two monks lived in hermitages. For ten days they did not see each other. The upper hermitage master asked,
I have not seen you for many days; where have you been? The lower hermitage master said, In the hut making an
unseamed stupa. The upper hermitage master said, I too want to make one. May I borrow your
pattern, Master? The lower hermitage master said, Why did you not say so earlier? It has just been borrowed by someone
already. Fayan said, Tell me, did he borrow his pattern or not? Wansong said,
The National Teacher was silent; why then did the lower hermitage master hedge and explain with words? Xuedou
said, Leave aside Suzong's not understanding. Did Danyuan understand or not? With only a single request for
a stupa pattern, all the patriarchs of India and this land, when pressed by this squeeze, could not
avoid turning south into north. If there is someone at the side who does not accept it, come forth, I want to ask you:
which one is the unseamed stupa? Wansong said, If not this, then speak. Jizhou Danyuan
Mountain's Chan Master Zhenying received instruction from Mazu and for a long time served as attendant to the National Teacher.
After the National Teacher passed away, the emperor summoned Yuan into the palace and raised the former story. Yuan too
was silent for a long while, then said, Does Your Majesty understand? The emperor said, I do not. Yuan recited a verse, Xiang
to the south, Tan to the north; in the middle, gold filling a whole country. Beneath the shadowless tree, shared
boat. On the lapis hall, no knowing. Some say, South of Xiang and north of Tan.
Fushan Yuan, in his record, wrote, Ox-head south, Horse-head north. If one gets the meaning, one forgets the trap.
There is nothing not allowable. Xuedou's saying they could not avoid turning south into north refers precisely to this.
A monk asked Daeling of Silla, What is purity everywhere? Ling said, Cut
jade branches and every inch is treasure; split sandalwood and every piece is fragrant. Danxia Chunhe
versified, Heaven and earth are entirely a golden land; all things fully reveal the pure, wondrous body.
Danyuan says gold fills a whole country; Danxia's land too is gold. Add one more
line and say: beneath the shadowless tree, a shared boat. In the Brief Commentary on the Zhouyi it says, Sharing one boat and
crossing over, then what fear is there that Hu and Yue will have different hearts? As for lines three and four of the Gradual hexagram, though different in form they harmonize,
and no thing can come between them. Yielding and preserving one another is like being in one boat. Above and below
are different bodies, like Hu and Yue; yet it is beneficial for resisting foes, so what fear is there of different hearts? This speaks of
one body and shared life, with benefit and harm alike. Chan Master Fazhenyi asked, This very place is
Maitreya; there is no gate and no Sudhana. Why then say on the lapis hall there is no know-
ing? Wansong said, Split open the hall and meet, then watch Tiantong bring it right to
your face and strike your nostrils. Verse:

lg.l:
Solitary, circling, round and full; where the power of sight is spent, it towers high.Not companion to the ten thousand dharmasWithout lack, without surplus
Chop your forehead and still you cannot reach it by looking

The moon sets, the pool is empty, the night colors deepen; clouds gather in, the mountains grow lean,The whole ten-direction world is like one stroke of ink
rich with autumn's look.The body is exposed in the golden wind
The eight trigrams are in proper place; the five phases' energies are in harmony.Heaven and earth join in its virtueSun and moon join in its brightness

Has the body gone ahead and already been there? The Nanyang father and son seem indeed to knowArrive and do not mark it
there is.For now believe half of it
As for the Buddhas and patriarchs of India, there is simply nothing they can do.A thousand sages have always stood downwind

cb:div.p:
The master said, Xuedou said, Layered and clear, the shadows round and round. That opening line is a fitting first phrase.
Tiantong says, Solitary, circling, round and full: a donkey-tethering stake for ten thousand kalpas. Where the
power of sight is spent, it towers high: the Buddhas of the three times uphold this as the unseen crown-protuberance.
Xuedou's verse on heaven and earth of one root and the myriad things one body, and Nansen pointing to a flower as
like a dream, also says, Seeing, hearing, awareness, knowing are not one by one; mountains and rivers are not within a mirror
to be viewed. In frosty skies the moon sets, the night is half gone; who shares the cold reflection in the clear pool? Try
comparing this verse with Tiantong's moon set, pool empty, night colors deep. The ancients truly
had deep work. Later Fojian once annotated it through, versifying: The unseamed stupa is
not a shadow. Vastly entering the realm of thusness, the Sakra eye flashes like flowing lightning.
Dim and dark, the summit cannot be seen. This too is where the power of sight is spent, towering high. Tian-
tong, in a verse on needle and thread running through, says, Towering green mountains wear autumn leanness; hair and fur wither,
the bones and frame grow old. This too is clouds gathered, mountains lean, rich autumn aspect. It can be called skin and flesh
all fallen away, only the one reality remains. Here, the eight trigrams are rightly placed, and the five
phases are also in harmony. In operation and construction there is no taboo. Why bother entering the market to ask Sun
Bin? Has the body gone ahead and seen it there? Chan Master Tianyi Huai, going in response to an invitation to Shanshan,
entered the monastery and in the hall said, For twenty years I have delighted in and admired this mountain. Today I am glad to have
come through the meeting of causes and conditions. Before this mountain monk arrived at this mountain, my body had already arrived at this mountain. When
I finally came, Shanshan was instead within this mountain monk's body. The Nanyang father and son indeed
seem to know there is. I dare not say they are so; I only say they seem like those who know there is. Tell me,
why not fully grant it? I fear slighting that National Teacher and his son. As for the Buddhas and patriarchs of India,
why are they also helpless? Right in the road a golden milestone is cast; in proper order he sets down
iron buns.

cb:div.head: Case Eighty-Six: Linji's Great Awakening
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: Copper head, iron brow; heavenly eyes, dragon pupils; eagle beak, fish gills; bear heart,
leopard gall. Beneath the vajra sword no strategy is accepted, not a single tally is gained. Why is it
like this?

Raised: Linji asked Huangbo, What is the true great meaning of the Buddha Dharma? Huangbo immediately struck him. This happened three times, and then he took leave of Huangbo and went to see Dayu.Killing people
Forgivable perhaps, but hard for feeling and reason to acceptEvery blow draws blood
Dayu asked, Where have you come from? Linji said, From Huangbo.Better heavy than lightDangerous, watch it carefullyThe staff wounds are still there
Dayu said, What words did Huangbo speak? Linji said, I asked three timesHere is a good place for revenge
about the true great meaning of the Buddha Dharma, and three times I received blows. I do not know whether I was at fault or not.
Dayu said, Huangbo is so grandmotherly, wearing himself out completely for your sake, and stillSixty blows fewer.
you come asking whether there was fault or no fault. At these words Linji had a great awakening.A second offense will not be tolerated.Only then does one know pain and itch.

The teacher said: Chan Master Huizhao of Linji Monastery in Zhenzhou was named Yixuan, from Nanhua in Caozhou.
His surname was Xing. At first he studied sutras and treatises extensively, but knowing that this was not the essential shortcut, he went to Huangbo.
He stayed with the assembly for three years and did not ask for interview at all, only keeping discipline and remaining silent. The head monk
found him different from the rest and urged him to engage in practice and questioning. In Correcting Errors in the Endless Lamp, it is privately argued that Linji
lived there three years; how could Huangbo not allow people to ask questions? Since he did allow it, with someone of Linji's
capacity and understanding, could he really not manage even a single opening question, and have to wait for the head monk to teach him? Then only afterward
be able to ask? I once saw Yang Wuwei compose a eulogy to Linji: The treasury of the true Dharma eye was extinguished at the blind donkey's side;
Huangbo the old granny, Dayu the chatterbox. I also saw Foguo compose a eulogy to Muzhou:
Pungent and sharp, gnashing and biting, he bored through north of the Ji as a great tree, and pushed
Yunmen over a perilous cliff. Words like dry firewood, principle not to be climbed by steps: this is what is called battle-array
straw sandals. In the original record, after awakening he immediately said, Originally Buddha Dharma is not much. Dayu said,
You bed-wetting ghost! Just now you asked whether there was fault or not, and now you say Buddha Dharma
is not much. How much is it then? He grabbed him and said, Speak, speak! Linji at Dayu's ribs
punched him three times. Dayu pushed him away and said, Your teacher is Huangbo; this is none of my business.
Linji returned to Huangbo. Huangbo asked, Coming and going, when will there be an end? Linji said, Only
because of your granny heart being so earnest. Then he told the previous story. Huangbo said, That old fellow Dayu, too much
loose tongue, wait till I see him and I'll give him a beating. Linji said, Why say wait till I see him? Right now I'll
strike. He then gave Huangbo a slap. Huangbo chuckled and said, This madman comes
here to stroke the tiger's whiskers. Linji then shouted. Huangbo said, Attendant, lead this madman
to the monks' hall. Guishan asked Yangshan, Did Linji get it through Dayu's power or Huangbo's power?
Yangshan said, Not only can he stroke the tiger's whiskers, he can also sit on the tiger's head. Later Linji addressed the assembly, saying:
At my former teacher's place I asked three times about the great meaning of Buddha Dharma, and three times was beaten, as if
brushed by a mugwort twig. Now I still want another beating. Who will lay on the blows? At the time
a monk came forward and said, I will do it. Linji lifted the staff and handed it over. As the monk reached to take it, Linji
immediately struck him. Xuedou said, Where Linji lets go is rather dangerous; when he gathers in, it is too quick. Tiantong
saw that this father and son were bold and uncommon. He selected the crucial point of Huangbo's functioning as transmitted by Linji
and brought it out in verse. The verse says:

lg.l: A fledgling of the ninefold phoenix, a colt for a thousand li; the true wind passes through the bellows.The wings are already grown.The divine steed is also ready.One opening is empty and unobstructed.

Subtle function releases the pivot; when it comes straight at the face, it is swift as flying lightning. When the clouds of delusion break,With one nudge it turns.No deliberation is allowed.
there the solitary sun.The old-time brilliance.

Stroking the tiger's whiskers—see! there is noneUnder Wansong, who daresto fix a hasty gaze?
but a heroic great man.But the old granny's heart is too earnest.

cb:div.p:
The teacher said: This praises Linji. As Xie Chaozong truly had phoenix feathers, Huangbo in former years
once served under Baizhang; today he met Linji's fierce hand. A true dragon gives birth to a dragon
son; a phoenix raises a phoenix chick. The Auspicious Omens says: The phoenix has nine folds. First is called Returning
to life. Second is called Heart in accord with measure, meaning heaven's measure. Third is called Ears hearing through. Fourth
is called Tongue curved and extending. Fifth is called Brilliant colored light. Sixth is called Short-crested crown, corresponding to vermilion
color. Seventh is called Sharp hook. Eighth is called Voice soaring and stirring. Ninth is called Belly door. Jiu
Fangyin judged horses for Duke Mu of Qin and indeed found a thousand-li colt. This compares Linji's
divine excellence, a thousand li in a single day. As soon as he awakened he understood the true function and great use. In various places
there is a couplet that says: At Huangbo's cheek he thundered one slap; beneath Dayu's ribs he drove three
punches. It is precisely because the true wind passed through the bellows that subtle function could release the pivot. The upper line speaks of receiving
the teacher's transmission; the lower line is the lineage-through of one's own nature. Laozi says: Between heaven and earth, is it not like a bellows?
The tuo is a bottomless sack, also a leather bellows. The yue is a three-holed flute.
Master Bajiao Che said: It is like qin, se, and konghou—though they have marvelous sounds, if there is no
marvelous touch, in the end they cannot be brought forth. This comes from the Shurangama Sutra. Coming straight at the face, like flying lightning,
swift—this speaks of his severe function and quick eloquence. Where deluded clouds break, the great sun stands alone—this speaks of his
awakened clarity. Stroking the tiger's whiskers: Zhuangzi says Confucius, after seeing Robber Zhi, withdrew and said,
This is what I call cauterizing oneself with no illness—running quickly to tease a tiger's head and braid a tiger's whiskers.
One can hardly avoid the tiger's jaws. See or not? Tiantong points this out to people, causing practitioners
to embody the complete function and great use, not yielding when duty is theirs. This is called the patch-robed monk's
nostrils. Layman Wujin, in his Continued Record of Qingliang, after saying that whatever was requested he responded to, recounts that
Chan Master Jietuo struck Manjushri, and he could not help but praise him, saying, Truly a great man. Do you also
see where Linji awakened? It shames to death the new bride of Heyang and startles awake the wooden-tower
old-woman Zen.

cb:div.head: Case Eighty-Seven: Shushan's Being and Nonbeing
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: When a gate is about to close, one shove and it opens. When a boat is about to sink, one pole and it turns.
A cart axlebox entering a valley has no road back. An arrow socket piercing heaven has one gate. Tell me,
where do you go?

Raised: When Shushan arrived at Guishan, he immediately asked, I have heard, Master, you say: With phrase, without phrase,
it is like a vine leaning on a tree. Suddenly the tree falls and the vine withers—where does the phrase return? Guishan laughed loudly. Shushan said, IHang it on the lips and teeth.Receiving the words.
The one who clings to words is stuck; the one who loses them is deluded.Where Yu’s strength cannot reach, the sound of the river flows westward.
have come four thousand li, selling my cloth robe to get here. How can you, master, toy with me like this?Have you paid back the money for the straw sandals or not?
Guishan called to the attendant, Fetch money and repay this senior monk. Then he charged him, saying,Ill-gotten wealth is to me like floating clouds.
Later there will be a one-eyed dragon who will break this open for you.And there are another four thousand li.
Later he went to Mingzhao and told the previous story. Zhao said, Guishan can be said to have the head rightOne guest troubles two hosts.
and the tail right; it is only that he did not meet one who knew the tune. Shu again asked, The treeIt is only that the rope is short; it has nothing to do with the old well being deep.
falls, the vine withers—where does the phrase return? Zhao said, It only makes Guishan laughAgain it comes like this.
all the more. At these words Shushan had an insight and then said, GuishanAnother man’s fist draws on the ground.The bedding-debt has been repaid.
all along had a knife in his laughter.Only then do you realize it has broken the skin and drawn blood.

The teacher said: Chan Master Guangren of Shushan in Fuzhou, when studying with Dongshan, asked, Concerning what has not yet been—
please, Master, give instruction. Dongshan said, No assent; no one affirms. Shu said, Is there still
any intimate point or not? Dongshan said, Right now, Reverend, can you get the intimate point? Shu said, If one cannot get it,
then there is nothing to hide. Later, in Xiangyan's assembly, a monk asked Xiangyan, Not admiring the sages
and not valuing one's own spirit—what then? Xiangyan said, The ten thousand functions rest; the thousand sages do not
carry it along. Shu, in the assembly, made a retching sound and said, What kind of words are these? Xiangyan asked, Who is that?
The assembly said, Your uncle in the Dharma. Xiangyan said, Do you not assent to this mountain monk? Shu came forth and said,
That is so. Xiangyan said, Can you say it? Shu said, I can. Xiangyan said, Try
saying it. Shu said, If you want me to say it, you must first return the rites between teacher and disciple. Xiangyan then
came down from the seat and bowed. Repeating his previous words, he asked him. Shu said, Why not say: Assent and affirmation
cannot be complete. Xiangyan said, Assent—assent to what? Affirmation—affirmation to whom?
Shu said, If assenting, one assents to the thousand sages; if affirming, one affirms to oneself. Xiangyan said,
Even so, you still must spend thirty years shitting backward. If you live on a mountain, there will be no firewood to burn.
If you live by water, there will be no water to drink. Remember this clearly. Later, when he lived on Shushan, it was indeed as Xiangyan predicted.
After twenty-seven years his illness eased, and he said to himself, Xiangyan predicted thirty years of shitting backward for me;
now I still owe three years. After every meal he used his hand to induce vomiting, to fulfill Xiangyan's prediction.
Later Shu asked Chan Master Daofu of Jingqing, Assent and affirmation cannot be complete—how do you
understand this? Fu said, Complete return is precisely assent and affirmation. Shu said, If it cannot be complete, then how is it?
Fu said, In this there is no road of assent. Shu said, Now it finally suits this sick monk's meaning. Gui-
shan Lanan was one of the Three Lazies of the Chan school, also called Chan Master Da'an of Changqing.
He instructed the assembly, saying: With phrase, without phrase, it is like a vine leaning on a tree. Shu did not consider four thousand
li too far, and sold his cloth robe to go ask. He happened upon Gui plastering a wall and asked: With phrase, without phrase,
like a vine leaning on a tree—is this not your saying, master? Gui said, Yes. Shu said, Suppose
the tree falls and the vine withers—what then? Gui set down the mud pan, laughed loudly, and returned to the abbot's room.
Shu followed after him and said, I sold my cloth robe and came four thousand li especially for this. master,
how can you toy with me? Gui called the attendant: Fetch money and repay him for his cloth robe, and send him off. Then
he charged him, Later there will be a one-eyed dragon who will break it open for you. Later he indeed met Ming-
zhao, who broke it open. Shushan sold his cloth robe and traveled four thousand li especially for this saying. People today
who wander from house to house may also keep their mind on their proper matter. But they merely establish things according to circumstances
and praise one another in turn. One must not, like Shushan, make retching sounds in the assembly. It is not that
he had no other strong points, but this breaks karmic procedure, breaks the turning of the Dharma wheel, breaks the harmony of the sangha.
It surely invites immediate retribution. Xiangyan gave a prediction; Shushan induced vomiting to answer it. Both became
examples for later people. Shushan too was someone whose level cannot be fathomed. Shu ordinarily
met people while holding a wooden snake in his hand. A monk asked, What is that thing in your hand? Shu lifted
the snake and said, A girl of the Cao family. Xuefeng entered the mountains and found a branch of wood shaped like
a snake. On its back he inscribed: Originally natural, not needing carving. He sent it to Guishan
Da'an. Gui said, A true mountain dweller, and without marks of knife or axe. Shushan,
having met Guishan because of this story, also used a wooden snake—whether personally transmitted or imitated,
cannot be known. Mingzhao also asked Shu, A tiger bears seven cubs—which one
has no tail? Shu said, The seventh has no tail. National Teacher Shao of Yunmen also
studied with Shushan. The short Dharma-uncle's name thus crowned past and present. Tiantong only took Guishan's
point of laughter, Shushan's seeing through it, and Mingzhao's raising of it, and directly brought it out in verse. The verse says:

lg.l:
Vine withered, tree fallen—he asks Guishan. A great hahahaha laugh—how could it be merelyWalk to where the waters end; sit and watch when the clouds rise.
idle?It almost became a mutual fooling.

There is a knife in the laughter—he peered and broke through it. Speech and thought have no road; the mechanism is cut off.I had thought there was something else.
The hinge-mechanism is severed.Four thousand li, and he tricked me into coming.

cb:div.p:
The teacher said: The outsider took Ayou as auspicious. At the head of the scripture, the syllable a signifies
no; the syllable you signifies yes. Therefore the outsider asked the Buddha, not asking for a statement of yes, not asking for a statement of no.
This is like a whale swallowing all the sea water dry. The World-Honored One remained silent a long time. This is like revealing
a branch of coral. The outsider bowed and praised the World-Honored One's great compassion for opening my clouds of delusion and enabling
me to enter. Xiangyan Rui said: Speech is slander, silence is deceit; above speech and silence
there is a road. Great Gui borrowed this to instruct the assembly. As soon as Shushan heard him add, vine
leaning on tree, he at once said, Tree falls, vine withers—where does the phrase return? How could this not make Gui-
shan laugh loudly? Guishan's words gave no opening, and speech did not hit the pivot. Shushan, clinging to words,
is lost; one stuck in phrases is deluded. If at that time you asked Wansong, I would only say, Blue heaven, blue
heaven. Do you not see that spring is carrying out winter's order?

cb:div.head: Case Eighty-Eight: Shurangama Not Seeing
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: With seeing and not seeing, you light a lamp at noon. With no seeing and no not-seeing,
you splash ink at midnight. If you trust that seeing and hearing are like illusory cataracts, then you will know sound and form are like empty
flowers in space. But tell me, is there in the teachings any patch-robed monk's way of speaking?

Raised, from the Shurangama Sutra: When I do not see, why do you not see the place of my not-seeing?
If you see the not-seeing, it is naturally not the mark of that not-seeing.What kind of lucky thought is that?Knowing for yourself is enough.
If you do not see the ground of my not-seeing, it is naturally not a thing. How then is it not you?
。Mind flurried, hands hurried, pushing out and crowding in.

The teacher said: Tiantong saw that Xuedou quoted the sutra in abridged form, yet still broadly raised the eye of the teachings. Though the raised passage
is brief, the verse is very detailed. In the second fascicle of the Shurangama, it first clarifies that what is not the seeing, namely objects,
are external dusts; next it clarifies that the seeing that is not a thing is true nature. Here the next section is quoted in full.
This is exactly this koan. The sutra says: If seeing were a thing, then you too could see my
seeing. If it is the same seeing, that is called seeing my seeing. When I do not see, why do you not see
the place of my not-seeing? If you see the not-seeing, it is naturally not the mark of that not-seeing.
If you do not see the ground of my not-seeing, it is naturally not a thing—how then is it not you? Chang-
shui's commentary says: The meaning of this passage, turning and turning to its conclusion, has five levels in all.
The sutra text preserves three and conceals two meanings. If discussed fully, it should say: If you do not see
the place of my not-seeing, then you also do not see the place of my seeing. Since you do not see the place of my seeing,
I see that what is naturally seen is not a thing. If what I see is not a thing, what you see is likewise not a
thing. Since what you see is not a thing, how could it not be your true seeing? Foguo cites this: A-
nanda’s idea was that the world, lanterns, and pillars all have names and forms; but the essence of seeing, luminous at root—what
thing can that be called? Let me see it. The Buddha’s meaning was: when I see the incense stand, what do you
make of it? Ananda said, I too see the incense stand; that is seeing where the Buddha sees. The Buddha
said, when I see the incense stand, that can be known. If I do not see the incense stand, then what do you
make of it? Ananda said, I also do not see the incense stand; that is seeing the Buddha’s not-seeing.
The Buddha said, when you say not seeing, that is only what you yourself know. How can you know
another person’s place of not-seeing? When the ancients got here, they could only know it themselves, not speak of it to others.
Xuedou brought out the scriptural eye and only praised seeing the Buddha. Tiantong deeply grasped the sutra’s meaning and praised
true seeing. The verse says:

lg.l:
Drain the blue sea dry, and the great void fills the patch-robed monk’s nostrilsAs before, white waves tower to the sky.Not a hair's breadth is seen.
long.For a thousand li, cold plum carries hidden fragrance.

The ancient Buddha’s tongue is short; a pearl-thread passes nine bends.A single true word cannot be fully spoken.
The effort of mind is spent in vain.

As soon as the jade loom turns once, face to face below—who recognizes him?The literary colors cross and spread, yet the meaning is distinct.
What kind of face is this?
Only then do you trust: this person is not fit for company.Demoted into the solitary hell.

cb:div.p:
The teacher said: Xuedou said, even the aeon-stone may yet be broken; but in the deep blue sea’s depths one must first
dry it up—only then is the great void filled. Zhugan Gui, heir to Foyan,
and the layman Chiyi, his elder uncle, both delighted in the Surangama Sutra. Zhu’an said: If apart from objects before you
there is a discriminating nature, that is precisely the root of birth and death. The layman was startled and said: Does the Buddha falsely speak
then? Zhu’an said: The Buddha surely does not speak falsely. But take this very moment, as I question you face to face—
where exactly is that mind? The layman sighed and said: The Buddha said, one who understands emptiness first is called a lion’s
roar. You may go now; do not linger here. Later Zhu’an ascended the hall and said: When seeing sees,
seeing is not itself seeing. Seeing is still apart from seeing; seeing cannot reach it. Fallen flowers, with intent, follow
the flowing stream; the flowing stream, without feeling, carries off the fallen flowers. Whatever can be returned is naturally not you.
That which is not returned to you—if not you, then who? We always regret spring’s return with no place to seek it;
not knowing it has turned and entered right here. He gave a shout and said: Thirty years from now, do not say the Benevolent One
taught and ruined the sons and daughters of other households. Wansong only uses the first three lines to pierce the case with commentary.
According to Xuedou, he directly praised seeing the Buddha. Foguo singled out the Buddha’s place of not-seeing as something only
self-known. Tiantong praised draining the blue sea dry and cramming the great void full. Zhu’an directly made clear that
the seeing of non-thing is also the root of birth and death. This patch-robed monk’s long nostrils all come forth
beyond the scriptural meaning, revealing another eye. The ancient Buddha’s tongue is short. Wansong said: The ancient
Buddha followed the occasion and accorded with their meaning; his words bent down for those of lesser capacity, so he spoke but half a word. The patch-robed monk
always fully upholds the true command; therefore there is the Way of a separate transmission. At Ruilu Monastery in Wenzhou,
Chan Master Yu’an of Shangfang punctuated the Surangama Sutra thus: When knowing and seeing are established, knowing
is the root of ignorance. When knowing and seeing are absent, this seeing is nirvana. He suddenly awakened. People
said, Master, you have broken the sentence wrongly. An said, This is where I awakened, and in the end he did not change it. At the time
he was called An of the Surangama. Wansong said: This too is correcting the right by means of the crooked. A pearl-thread passes nine
bends. It is said in the world that when Confucius was trapped in Chen and had to thread a nine-bend pearl, he met a girl among the mulberries
who gave him the secret: Fine indeed—think on it; think on it finely. Confucius then understood and used
a thread tied to an ant, luring it with honey to draw it through. Master Puzhao Zong of Sizhou
wrote in his preface to Tiantong’s verses on the old cases: Crooked and winding, yet interconnected, like the thread and ant threading a pearl;
circling and clinging to each other, like cloud and dragon spreading rain. In Fushan’s Nine-Belt Collection
there is the winding hanging belt; the main idea is to show that the curved serves present occasions. As soon as the jade loom
turns once, like a single shuttle of a jade loom before the pattern is formed. Tiantong cites Dongshan’s early-autumn,
late-summer saying, and then says: To go out the gate is grass, treading amid dense and tangled growth.
Leaves fall and you know autumn, dropping into dim green places. When you get here, you must realize
the point where, though the pivot has turned, the seal has not yet formed a pattern. After a pause he said: When the old clam in bright water
is pregnant, when heavy clouds gather and the blue dragon sheds its bones—meeting directly below, who recognizes
him? Only then do you trust this person is not fit for company. Jiashan said: Sit decisively as the master; do not
fall into the second view. You must know there is one person not fit for company. Wansong said: Coming alone,
you would think there was no one acquainted—then in the bustle you suddenly meet an old friend.

cb:div.head: Case Eighty-Nine: Dongshan’s No Grass
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: If you move, you bury yourself a thousand fathoms deep. If you do not move, shoots spring up where you stand. You must
let go of both ends and set down the middle. Then buy straw sandals and go traveling—only then will it do.

Raised case: Dongshan addressed the assembly, saying: Brothers, at early autumn and summer’s end, some go east, some go west.
You must go where for ten thousand miles there is not an inch of grass. Then he also said: But as forUrging a cat into a dry well.
a place where for ten thousand miles there is not an inch of grass—how do you go there? Shishuang said:Once a word is spoken, four horses cannot catch it.
As soon as you go out the gate, it is grass. Dayang said: Straight talk—if you do not go out the gate, it is alsoLook under your own feet.
grass stretching everywhere.There is no place for you to dodge.

The teacher said: Shishuang encountered the Huichang persecution and, in lay clothing, stayed at Taojiafang in Liuyang, Changsha.
At the beginning of the Dazhong era, a monk came from Dongshan after completing the summer retreat. Shishuang
asked, Where have you come from recently? The monk said, Dongshan. Shishuang said, What sayings does your master
use to instruct disciples? The monk said, Near the day of ending the summer retreat, the master ascended the hall and told the assembly:
Brothers, at early autumn and summer’s end, some go east, some go west. You must go to a place where for ten thousand miles there is not an inch
of grass. After a pause he said, But as for a place where for ten thousand miles there is not an inch of grass, then how
do you go there? Shishuang said, Step out the gate and it is grass. The monk then repeated this to Dongshan. Dongshan said:
This is the speech of a good teacher of fifteen hundred people. In the great Tang realm, how many can there
be like this? Later the awl in the bag at last showed its point; the fruit ripened and its fragrance spread. The community invited him again to wear robes.
He took up residence at Shishuang’s monastery, truly matching Wuben’s prediction. Through this case, Shishuang’s
Way spread under heaven. Later Chan Master Yan of Dayang said: Nowadays, speaking directly, if one does not
go out the gate, it is also a place of endless grass. Tell me then, where should one practice and walk?
After a pause he said: Do not keep to a cold cliff where strange grass grows green; sitting fixed in white clouds is not the school’s marvel.
National Teacher Shanguo of Yuantong said: Tell me, right now, what is the phrase under your heels?
How will you speak it? If you say, For ten thousand miles there is not an inch of grass, I permit you to have met Dongshan.
If you say, Going out the gate is grass, I permit you to have met Shishuang. If you say, not going out
the gate is also endless grass, I permit you to have met Dayang. If you cannot say any of them at all,
I permit you to have met Yansheng. Why? Only a good breeze comes to the mat;
no idle words fall among men. Wansong said: If you want to speak all three lines together,
you must further meet Tiantong. The verse says:

lg.l:
Grass everywhere—inside the gate, outside the gate, you yourself must look; in the bramble-Straight down, no bottom; to the sides, no edge.Watch out or you'll trip.
thicket, setting down your foot is easy.Picking weeds in a barren field.

Outside the night-bright curtain, turning the body is hard. Look,A pure ground still deludes people.
look.In matters, one does not disdain detail.

How many kinds are there? For now, go with the old tree in its winter leanness,Before the dead-tree cliff, the side paths are many.Just preserve today
the resolve.
then follow the spring wind into the burned scars.There will surely be a time that satisfies the heart.

cb:div.p:
The teacher said: Dayang said directly, Even not going out the gate is a place of endless grass. Tian-
tong then said, Grass everywhere, inside and outside the gate—look for yourself. It is just like speaking
without contrivance, playing within it. Going out the gate is grass: everyone easily knows this, and it is easy
to turn around there. Not going out the gate is also grass: people find it hard to know and hard to turn around. Therefore it is said:
On level ground the dead are countless; one who gets through the bramble forest is a skillful hand. Tiantong
says: In the bramble forest, setting down your foot is instead easy. On the flat clear ground, not going out the threshold,
turning around outside the night-bright curtain is extremely hard. Each must use his own eyes; no one can do it in your place.
He also says, How many kinds? Yansheng had four kinds; Wansong has five kinds. Checking them
all over, they do not go beyond the final two lines. Do you want to know Tiantong’s place behind the palm? A tiger
grows lean, but its heroic heart remains; a man may be poor, yet his resolve endures.

cb:div.head: Case Ninety: Yangshan’s Respectful Announcement
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: Qu Yuan, alone awake, was precisely dead drunk. Yangshan speaking a dream is just like
waking time. Tell me then: Wansong speaks like this, you all listen like this—tell me,
is this waking or dreaming?

Raised case: Yangshan dreamed he went to Maitreya’s place and sat in the second seat. A venerable oneTell me, who is the first seat?
announced: Today the one in the second seat should preach the Dharma. Yangshan thenThe one who comes whispering right and wrong
rose, struck the gavel, and said: The Mahayana Dharma is apart from the four propositions,Closely observe the Dharma of the Dharma King; the Dharma King's Dharma is thus.The wording on this meaning is long.
beyond the hundred negations. Respectfully announced.Speech is clear, conduct is muddy.

The teacher said: At age seven Yangshan entered samadhi and saw himself together with the Teaching Lord and Kasyapa and the twenty-
seven patriarchs assembled in a hall. The hall stood in space with no pillars or stones; the ground’s color
was like lapis. On waking he and the patriarchs all had brahman-like marks, wore golden robes, and went barefoot,
and he was in the eighth place. One venerable of very great age called out: Gautama, I now
am to conduct the Dharma affair. Yang then left his seat, went to the striker, and announced: Great assembly, pure-mind Maha-
yana Dharma is apart from the four propositions, beyond the hundred negations. Having spoken, he returned to his seat. And the monks all
discussed only the meaning of this explanation. Yang also himself said that in the dream he went to Maitreya’s place and was placed in
the third seat. Essentials of the Lineage also says third seat, the same as this record. One venerable
struck and announced: Today the one in the third seat should preach the Dharma. The mountain monk then rose and announced with the gavel:
The Mahayana Dharma is apart from the four propositions, beyond the hundred negations. Listen well, listen well. In Sanskrit,
mahayana—here it means Great Vehicle. Four propositions and hundred negations: Mazu hid the head in white,
Hai hid the head in black. The case has already been split open and judged clearly, so this will not be repeated. Dayi Xiu
said: Explaining the meaning according to the words is not impossible. But suppose in Maitreya’s assembly there were someone who
on hearing him say Mahayana Dharma immediately said, Shut those two flaps of skin. Then not only
would he cut off Yangshan’s words, he would also save later people from telling dreams in a dream. Wan-
song said: If Dayi knew how to turn the light around and reflect back, Wansong would have no place to hide.
Is there anyone clear in a dream, awake within drunkenness? The verse says:

lg.l:
In a dream, clutching robe, he consults the elders; ranks of sages stand thick, seated to his right.The familiar mirror is hard to forget.A dog-pardon decree.
The feudal lords clear the road.

When benevolence is called for, he yields to none: the striker sounds, he preaches the Dharma—fearless lion’s roar.The heart does not fail people.
The face shows no shame.

Mind at peace like the sea, courage as great as a dipper; from shark eyes tears flow.Swallowing and receiving a hundred rivers.As if no one were beside him.
Dot by dot, it is blood.

In the clam’s belly pearls are split open; who knows that babbling leaks out my secret? Pang’sScarlet heart, piece by piece.Hands and feet are all exposed.
brows should smile at exposing the family shame.Because of whom was this brought about?

Beyond four propositions, cutting off a hundred negations—the Master MaThe words are still in the ears.
father-and-son illness cannot be cured.Running with pain and ache, even spirits and ghosts can hardly make it out.

cb:div.p:
The teacher said: In the northeast there is a country always awake, without dreams. In the central region there is a country where dream and waking
are always half and half, taking what is done in dreams as unreal and what is done when awake as real. In the south-
west there is a country always dreaming, waking once in fifty days, taking waking as unreal and
dreams as real. In this, the reality and unreality of waking and dreaming are not easily known. Longya’s verse says: In a dream, how can one know
the dream is unreal? Only upon waking does one realize there was nothing in the dream. In delusion it is just like
a man in a dream; after awakening it is like a man rising from sleep. In the teachings, dreams are taken as solitary consciousness
cognizing a realm of solitary images. Yangshan had long been free of inverted dream-thoughts—how then did he make
this coming and going? The Lotus says, one constantly has such good dreams. In the Golden Light, ten-stage bodhi-
sattvas have ten kinds of dream states; some dreams are all the better. Great Master Daming Quan of Cizhou
loved joking and laughter. Master Heng of Renshan instructed the attendant always to restrain and watch
him. The great master said: Human life is one dream; a whole life of joy is a good dream. A whole life
of restraint and policing is a bad dream. I would rather have a good dream. The next day the attendant withdrew. Though Yang-
shan was in a dream, he still sported with the Mahayana Dharma; this too is the habitual energy of the holy assembly.
The striker is not only the announcing gavel; all bells and fish-drums used to alert the assembly are all called jianzhui. It is translated as
sounding voice. Lion’s roar: the Song of Enlightenment says, the lion’s roar is fearless preaching. Mind at peace
like the sea: the Lotus Sutra says, his mind is at peace like the sea; when I heard, my net of doubt was cut. In the Records of Shu,
the Minister and Great General Jiang Wei, style name Boyue, was called in common saying one with a dipper-gallbladder, Jiang Wei.
In Tiantong, not a single word lacks a source. In Ren Fang’s Record of Strange Things: In the South Sea are shark-people,
who dwell in water like fish yet do not cease weaving; when they weep, their tears become pearls. Continued Tales of Marvels says: In the Han,
Emperor Wu visited the Huzi River; a person a little over a foot tall presented a single cave pearl.
Dongfang Shuo said: at the bottom of the river there is a cavern, several hundred zhang deep. There is a red-intestined creature that produces
this pearl. It is an inch across, its brightness surpassing the world. Yangshan only knew tears issue from pained bowels, and did not
notice his tongue hanging outside his mouth. Wine molds the true nature; in a dream heaven's secret is spilled. The four propositions and hundred negations
were all spat out at once. A monk had asked Mazu and his descendants, "Apart from the four propositions and cut off from the hundred negations,"
making the old "meaning of the Patriarch's coming from the West" question his opening. Yangshan then supplied a Mahayana footnote. Yangshan
was also Mazu's great-grandson in the Dharma line. Why is the medicine one, yet the illness different? Getting out is still
easy enough; to speak in a body-free way is hard.

cb:div.head: Case Ninety-One: Nanquan's Peony
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: Yangshan took what is in the dream as real; Nanquan pointed to the waking state as empty.
If you know that waking and dreaming are originally nonexistent, then you begin to trust that emptiness and reality are beyond opposition. Tell me, what kind of eye
does this person possess?

Raised case: Once, when Minister Lu Geng said to Nanquan, "Dharma Master Zhao is truly extraordinary.
He can explain, 'Heaven and earth have one root; the myriad things are one body.'" Nanquan pointed to the courtyardIt is also the white pig of Liaodong.Raise two fingers.
peony and said, "Minister, people nowadays see this single flower as if in a dream,
just like that."Passing the complaint to the next wall.

The teacher said: Lu Geng of the Tang, style name Jingshan, was a man of Wujun. He served as Inspector of Xuanshe
and was additionally made Censor-in-Chief. At first he asked Nanquan, "I keep a goose in a bottle. It gradually
grows large and cannot get out. Now, without damaging the bottle and without harming the goose, Master,
how can it be gotten out?" Nanquan called, "Minister!" Geng answered, "Yes?" Nanquan said, "Out
it is." From this, Geng attained understanding. He then gave his mind to principle and nature, and immersed himself in Zhao's treatises. In the seventh chapter, "Marvelous Preservation," of the Nirvana No-Name Treatise,
it says: The mysterious Way lies in marvelous awakening; marvelous awakening lies
in immediacy to the real. If immediate to the real, then being and nonbeing are viewed equally; if viewed equally, then self and other are not two.
Therefore heaven and earth and I share one root; the myriad things and I are one body. If they are the same as me, then
there is no longer being or nonbeing. If they differ from me, they depart from interpenetration. Thus not outside and not inside,
and the Way abides between them. Geng cited these two lines as something extraordinary, but he did not realize
it was precisely talking in a dream. Even so, the great master Shitou once, upon reading Zhao's
treatise, when he reached the seventeenth chapter "Comprehending Antiquity" and read, "The perfected person is empty and open, without form, and yet the myriad things are none but his making.
He who can gather the myriad things as self, is that not the sage alone?" suddenly
opened and awakened to the Way. Then he said, "The sage has no self, yet there is nothing that is not self," and thereupon composed the Cantongqi.
Foguo said: Lu Geng's question is indeed very remarkable, but it still does not go beyond
doctrinal meaning. If doctrinal meaning were the ultimate, why did the World-Honored One further hold up a flower? Why did the Patriarch
still come from the West? Where Nanquan answered, he used a patchrobed monk's living grip to
point out the illness and smash his nest. So he pointed to the flower in front of the courtyard and called to the minister, saying, "People nowadays
see this single flower as if in a dream." It is like leading him to the edge of a ten-thousand-zhang cliff
and giving one shove so that the root of his life is cut off. If you merely push him along level ground,
even when Maitreya Buddha descends in the future, he still would not understand. National Teacher Shanguo of Yuantong raised his whisk and
said, "All conditioned dharmas are like dreams, illusions, bubbles, and shadows." Tiantong just within the dream
transformed and brought forth the realm of Huaxu. The verse says:

lg.l:
Illumining through the subtle and the apart, the root of creation; in endless comings and goings, its gate is seen.Walk to where the waters end.
Sit and watch when the clouds rise.

Roaming spirit beyond kalpas, ask what is there; set the eye before the body and know the marvelousOutside the mind there is no dharma.
preservation.The eyes are full of green mountains.

Tigers roar, soughing in the cliffs; it becomes dragon-chanting, drifting softly through the cave,Begging fire, one gets it along with smoke.
while the clouds grow dim.Carrying spring water, he returns with the moon.

Nanquan punctured the dream of people of the time; if you would know the statelyJust fit for sleep-talk.
Honored One of the place of succession,This very place is Maitreya.

cb:div.p:
The teacher said: In Master Zhao's Precious Treasury Treatise, in the chapter "Subtle in Departure, Wondrous in Essence," it says: In going out, subtle; in entering, apart.
Knowing entering-apart, outer dust has nowhere to rest. Knowing going-out-subtle, inner mind has nothing to do.
If inner mind has nothing to do, all views cannot move it. If outer dust has nowhere to rest, the myriad
things cannot bind it. Tiantong, in praising Nanquan, says, "Illumining through the subtle and the apart, the root of creation;"
"in endless entering and leaving, its gate is seen." You see the two gates of going-out-subtle and entering-apart, yet it is only one gate divided
into inner and outer. In truth, in the ten directions there are no walls, and on all four sides no gate either. "Roaming spirit
beyond kalpas, ask what is there" means heaven and earth share one root. "Set the eye before the body, know marvelous preservation"
means the myriad things are one body. Root and body can give rise to heaven, earth, and all things. Like dragons
chanting and mist rising, tigers roaring and wind arising, when there is response there is surely resonance. Therefore one flower before the courtyard
lets one see all spring flowers under heaven. Wansong speaks of a dream: first there is the one who does not sleep;
next there is sleeping. Because of sleep, without awareness there is dreaming. Because of dreaming, one sees objects. Because of objects, one sees
another body, distinguishing and experiencing within the scene. If you recognize the one who never sleeps,
then all these tangles are cut off with one stroke and one phrase. Do you want to know the Compassionate Honored One of the place of succession? At this
point, if you do not investigate the source, you can only wait for the future and ask Maitreya.

cb:div.head: Case Ninety-Two: Yunmen's One Treasure
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: Having attained the great samadhi of playful spiritual powers and understanding the dharani of beings' languages,
he yanks around Muzhou's Qin-era axle-borer and brings out Xuefeng's South Mountain
turtle-nosed snake. Do you still recognize this person?

Raised case: Great Master Yunmen said, "Within heaven and earth, within the cosmos,What about that which wraps heaven and earth?
there is one treasure, hidden in the mountain of form." He held up a lanternWhat about that which sets up the universe at once?If you do not believe it, search your bosom.The formed mountain is a treasure.
and said, "Bring the lantern into the Buddha Hall; bring the Three Gate onto the lantern."It is already a donkey playing with a well.How much worse when the well peers at the donkey.

The teacher said: Great Master Yunmen loved making such advances and withdrawals, in the height of noise and bustle
suddenly pulling away midway. One day, instructing the assembly, he cited Master Zhao's Precious Treasury Treatise. If quoted in full, it should read:
"Within heaven and earth, within the cosmos, there is one treasure hidden in the mountain of form.
Knowing things, it illuminates emptily; inside and outside are vacant and serene. Silent and remote, beyond seeing, its function is mysteriously
profound." Xuedou commented, saying, "Within heaven and earth, within the cosmos, there is one
treasure hanging on the wall. Bodhidharma for nine years did not dare look at it straight on. Now
if a patchrobed monk wants to see it, split his spine with a stick at once." National Teacher Yuantong said, "No wonder they are Deshan's
descendants." The original record says, "Bring the Three Gate onto the lantern." How is it? He himself answered in place:
"The meaning shifts following things." He also said, "Thunder rises and clouds surge." Foguo said: Kumarajiva
was Master Zhao's teacher in learning. Buddhabhadra of Waguan Monastery, translated as Juexian,
was his Dharma successor. In the Endless Lamp, Zhao is listed among Juexian's Dharma heirs. Juexian
succeeded the Indian Buddha Daxian. Buddha Daxian and Bodhidharma studied together under the twenty-
seventh patriarch Prajnatara. On the day Zhao faced execution, he begged a seven-day reprieve and composed the Precious Treasury
Treatise. Yunmen lifted it up to instruct the assembly, but you must not, like scriptural lecturers, merely explain
principles and meanings. He only wanted you to add a footnote. "Take the lantern into the Buddha Hall"—
ordinary thinking can measure that. "Bring the Three Gate onto the lantern"—can ordinary thinking
measure that? A true lineage master never ties a real Dharma onto people. Therefore
Xuedou said, "There is one treasure hanging on the wall." If Tiantong had not borrowed it
for use, it would almost have become stale stock. The verse says:

lg.l:
Gathering up and rolling away leftover thoughts, weary of the splendor of affairs, returning—where is life'sWater deep, waves quiet; learning broad, the voice low.
shore?Old and grown, yet still not knowing where one stands.

The rotten-axe-handle woodcutter suspects there is no road; the pot-master hangs his gourd on the tree,A place where sun and moon do not reach.
wondrously there is a home.It is another world altogether.

On night waters golden waves float cassia shadows; in autumn windIt goes through above and below.
snowlike gusts crowd the reed blossoms.Great and small are clear.

In the cold, fish stay at the bottom and do not take the bait; when the mood is spent,Casting the line is labor in vain.
a clear song turns the raft back.Again blown by the wind into another tune.

cb:div.p:
The teacher said: When waters return to the great sea, waves are quiet; when clouds reach Cangwu, the weather turns serene. Therefore
it is said: In trading insults, I yield to you; in mouth-to-mouth spitting, I yield to you; in splashing water, I yield to you. This is Yunmen
"gathering up leftover thoughts, weary of hua." The word hua is used two ways: first, cast off ornament and take the real;
second, weary of busy, flourishing affairs. "Returning, where is life's shore?" The first line is from the Precious
Treasury Treatise; the second is Yunmen's added phrase. Where will you grope for it? If you stop
your activity and stand thinking, one thought becomes ten thousand years. Even if your axe handle rots away, it is still late chess
and clumsy play. In an earlier verse on Yanyang meeting Zhaozhou there was already the original tale of the rotten-axe woodcutter.
In an earlier verse on Xuefeng's final line there was also the original tale of the tree-hung pot-master. Fei
Changfang saw Master Hu selling medicine at one fixed price. He hung his pot on a tree and would suddenly jump into
the pot. Changfang saw this from an upper story and knew he was no ordinary man. So he said he would sweep and offer food
without complaint. After a long time, Hu knew his faith was sincere and said, "Come when no one is around at dusk." He told Fang,
"Follow me and jump in." Fang obeyed his words and also jumped in, and at once entered the pot. He saw there were towers,
layered gates of five colors, and scores of attendants on both sides. The first line praises the Precious Treasury Treatise;
the next line praises Yunmen's saying. In the following two lines, the first praises brightness and the second praises clarity.
Though the words and meaning are bright and clear, how many can realize them? Yunmen penetrated the transformation and pointed out
a living road. "Cold fish stay at the bottom and do not take the bait" uses Chuanzhi's line: when night is still,
the water is cold and fish do not feed. "Golden waves, cassia shadows" means moonlight filling the boat. Golden waves and cassia
shadows are alternate names for the moon. Tiantong said, "Clear light shines in the eyes, as if one has lost the way." Zhaozhou
said, "This old monk is not in the bright and clear." Therefore when the mood is spent, with a clear song he turns the raft back.
But tell me, where does he go? Deep at night he does not lodge in the reed cove; he turns out
beyond the middle and the two ends.

cb:div.head: Case Ninety-Three: Luzu Does Not Understand
cb:div.p:
Instruction to the assembly: A Jing-jewel traded for a magpie, mouse-gold—if you do not recognize the treasure, you cannot obtain
its use. Is there anyone here who suddenly awakens to the jewel in the robe?

Raised case: Luzu asked Nanquan, "The mani jewel is not recognized by people; in the Tathagata-store
it is personally possessed. What is the store?" Nanquan said, "What Teacher WangShow off less.Before the Dharma hall, behind the Buddha hall.
and you go back and forth with is it." Luzu said, "What about what does not go back and forth?" Nanquan said, "That too is the store." Luzu said, "What is the jewel?"What mortal hurry is there?Knowing the head of the road and the tail, telling what has gone
and knowing what comes.One time alive, two times acted out.Get one and look for two.
Nanquan called, "Master Zu!" Zu answered, "Yes?" Nanquan said,This old monk is not unwilling to bring it up.The venerable is not unwilling to bring it forth.
"Go on. You do not understand my words."All my life I have poured out my liver and guts to others.

The teacher said: Chan Master Shizu of Yunjisi on Zhongnan Mountain was a Dharma heir of Nanquan. Tiantong
mistakenly took him for Luzu; let this be clarified here, and students should know it. As for Chizhou's
Chan Master Baoyun of Luzu Mountain, he was a Dharma heir of Mazu and thus Nanquan's elder Dharma brother.
Moreover, Shizu and Nanquan called each other by name. Because he awakened through this koan, he was surely
Nanquan's son in Dharma, without doubt. He first asked Nanquan, "The mani jewel is not recognized by people.
In the Tathagata-store it is personally possessed." This saying originally comes from Yongjia's Song of Enlightenment.
Monk Fantianqi's commentary says: The Sanskrit mani here means "wish-fulfilling."
It is also called "stainless light," and also "increase." The Lankavatara Sutra says: Tranquil extinction
is called One Mind. One Mind is called the Tathagata-store. It has three meanings: first, concealment—
it covers and stores the Tathagata; second, inclusion—it contains all sentient
beings and lands; third, birthing—it can produce the undefiled causes and fruits, and the human and heavenly paths
of practice. The first is in terms of delusion, the latter in terms of awakening, and the middle in terms of essence itself. Also, the Srimala text says:
there are two kinds of Tathagata-store: the empty Tathagata-store, free from all afflictions,
and the nonempty Tathagata-store, endowed with Buddha-dharmas inconceivable as sands of the Ganges. Shizu of Zhongnan Mountain
at Yunjisi, when first with Nanquan, asked about the mani jewel
not recognized by people, and then again asked about the jewel. Nanquan said, "Go on, you do not understand my words." Shizu
from this trusted and entered. National Teacher Yuantong said, "Is there anyone nowadays who can trust and enter?
If there is, then when Wangxiang arrives, the light blazes. If not, where Li Lou walks, waves surge
to heaven." Foguo said: The whole earth is the Tathagata-store—where will you place
the jewel? The whole earth is the mani jewel—what will you call the store? Xuedou separately said,
"Steep! To perform tricks atop a hundred-foot pole is not a good hand. Here, if you set
an eye in place, guest and host interchange and you can enter deep into the tiger's den. If not thus,
even if Shizu awakened, it is still a dragon's head and snake's tail. To see head and tail complete,
it must be Tiantong the monk. The verse says:

lg.l: Distinguish right and wrong, discern gain and loss; the responsive mind points to them as if on the palm.There are sinews in the eyes.In seeing, it penetrates through; in use, it is clear.

Coming and going, not coming and going, all of this is the hidden wheel.Altogether, it has nothing to do with him.Thus or not thus, both are acceptable.
The king rewards it for merit.The honest do not take; the greedy are not given.

The Yellow Emperor got it through Image-Net and the pivot turned.The mind's effort is already spent.
At the hinge and pivot, skill can play its tricks.In a hundred things, none match you.
A clear-eyed patchrobed monk is never reckless.In matters, one does not disdain detail.

cb:div.p:
The master said: Song of the Mind Pearl and Chant on Playing with the Pearl speak much of this pearl, calling it the wish-fulfilling jewel.
They speak little of the Tathagata-store; only Yongjia, the one-night awakened one, personally within the Tathagata-store
received it. Six kinds of spiritual functioning, empty and not empty; one round light, color yet not color.
What is the store? What is the pearl? What comes and goes is it; what does not come and go is also it.
Here it is for you to distinguish right and wrong, to understand gain and loss. Respond to it in the mind, obtain it in
the hand. Pointing to the palm comes originally from the Analects. Nanquan pointed to the store, pointed to the pearl as if placing it
in your palm, pointing as if giving it to you. Since coming-going and not-coming-going are the store, call—
respond; call and no response—both are the pearl. What doubt is there? But tell me: does the store come out of the pearl, or does the pearl come out of the store?
Pound them into one lump, split them into two sections. In the Lotus Sutra, the Buddha told Manjushri:
Just as a wheel-turning king, seeing among his troops those with great merit, is deeply
delighted, he takes this hard-to-believe jewel, long kept in his topknot, and does not casually give it to others—but
now gives it. The Yellow Emperor sent Xiangwang to seek the pearl; this was already clarified in the earlier verse on Shoushan's three phrases,
where it was made clear: where the mechanism-wheel turns, even the eye of wisdom can still be confused. If one cannot, within no-trickery,
work a trick, one cannot say, "Go on—you do not understand my words." Yunji awakened here.
Dongshan said: Joy is not absent; it is like on a dung heap, picking up
a bright pearl. Wansong said: I am not like Dongshan's beggar delighted at small gain;
joy is then no joy, like smashing a bright pearl within the Tathagata-store.
That is what it is like.

cb:div.head: Case Ninety-Four: Dongshan Unwell
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: The lower does not discuss the higher; the humble does not move the honored. Though one may gather oneself and follow another,
one still must not lightly trouble what is weighty. When the four elements are out of harmony, how do you attend and serve?

Raised: Dongshan was unwell. A monk asked, "Whe master is sick, is there one who is not sick?"
Shan said, "There is." The monk said, "Does the one not sick still look after master?" Shan said, "This old monk has a share in looking at him." The monk said, "When master looks at him,Let it be argued and explained as one pleases.Forcibly insist.Worldly truth.
Spread and circulate.Meeting on one’s original ground.
then how is it?" Shan said, "Then no sickness is seen."With what eyes do you meet?Simply refusing to inquire into the provisional.

The master said: The ancients, at the time of departure, played freely within the realms of old age, illness, and death. Among them Dong-
shan is especially remarkable. Since it was said he was slightly ill and the assembly came to inquire after him, a monk asked, "When master is sick,
is there still one not sick?" In this monk's words there is an echo; in the sentence a mechanism is presented. He also
wanted to see whether the sick man had eyes. Shan said, "There is." He scratched the itch; the sickness lessened by ten parts.
The monk said, "Does the one not sick still look after master?" Reversing the command like this, he wanted people to know
it is not like that. Shan said, "This old monk has a share in looking at him." If judged by worldly feeling,
the not-sick should look after the sick. But Dongshan instead says, "This old monk has a share in looking at him."
How could this be ordinary social etiquette of asking after someone? This monk wanted a meeting through to the bottom, so he asked further:
"After master looks at him, how is it?" Here it is for you, through all twelve hours, always to
attend with reverence—sleep at night and rise early, ask after well-being and daily life—only then are you one who knows gratitude and is filial.
Shan said, "If this old monk looks, then no sickness is seen." This is the place where in a lifetime of
practice, one gains strength at the moment of departure. Shan again asked the monk, "Apart from this leaking shell,
in what place will you meet me?" The monk had no reply. Shan had a verse saying: "Students,
though many, not one awakens. The fault lies in seeking along another's tongue-road. If you wish to forget form and erase
traces, strive diligently to walk in empty space." After the verse he had his head shaved, sounded the bell, sat in the hall,
bade farewell to the assembly and announced his passing. The assembly wailed. Shan opened his eyes and reproved their foolishness, then extended another seven
days, and again bade the assembly farewell and sat to pass away. In the Continuation of the Lamp of the Dading era: during the Huangtong period, in Xianping Prefecture,
Chan master Faqing of Dajue Monastery, successor to Chan master Baifoguo, once served as chief scribe.
He first lived at Puzhao in Sizhou, later moved to Songshao. When Bian was broken, he was captured and in the north herded
cattle. A lecture monk recognized him. Later he lived in the Eastern Capital. Because an attendant read the Record of Dongshan and came to the "Foolishness Feast"
passage, the attendant said, "The ancients were truly extraordinary." Jue said, "After I transform, you may call
me. If I can return, that is the power of the Way." Later he knew beforehand his time had come, and made
a verse, saying: "This year, on the fifth day of the fifth month, the four elements will leave their original master. My white bones,
let the wind scatter them, sparing the donor's land from occupation." He gave all clothes and belongings to the attendant, fed the monks,
and at the first watch bell sat and passed away. The attendant said, "Before, we agreed I should call you," and so called
three times. Jue answered, "What is it?" The attendant said, "Why master go off naked and barefoot?"
Jue said, "What was there when I came?" The attendant wanted to force clothes on him. Jue said, "Stop—leave them for those who come after."
The attendant said, "At just such a time, how is it?" Jue said, "Just like this." Again he
wrote a gatha saying: "Seventy-three years were like a flash of lightning. At departure, for you I open a single
line: The iron ox leaps across Silla, smashing empty space into seven or eight pieces." Majestically
he transformed. He lived seventy-three years. It was the fifth day of the fifth month, third year of Huangtong. Dongshan
recognized what is not sick; Dajue recognized what does not die. Therefore these two elders came and went by their own
freedom. Tiantong picked this up and said: "Able to go, able to come; able to come, able to go. I see him with a share;
he sees me not so. At just such a time, how do you fully comprehend it?" After a long pause,
he said: "The lingering mist is still deep, no summit in sight. The spring wind is always there, yet the branch does not bud." Tiantong's
full mechanism in bringing out and revealing this matter has already been explained above. In the verse we see the ancients' hard-won
strength. But how is it then? The verse says:

lg.l:
Cast off the stinking skin-bag; turn and take up the red lump of flesh right at the fore.Grass withered, the hawk's eyes are sharp.Snow gone, the horse's hooves are light.
The nostrils are straight and true.It still must be turned around to do.

Directly beneath the skull is dry; the old physician sees no long-Above all, avoid seeing ghosts.
standing ailment.The hand arrives and the illness is removed.

For the young one to look at him, drawing near is hard; the wild waters are thinHe has no land or country; where will you meet him?
when autumn floods recede.The dragon travels the old road.

Where white clouds break, the old mountain—cold, it must be cut offThis truth is hard to extinguish.
completely.A gentleman's one word
Do not be muddled. Turn it utterly with no merit, and he takes his place.Light the lamp and eat rice.Leaves fall and return to the root.
The solitary standard does not share your tray.When coming, there was no mouth.

cb:div.p:
The master said: Master Shitou said, "If you would know the deathless man in the hut, how could he be apart from
this present skin-bag?" Yet Tiantong says, "Cast off this skin-bag." Dongshan and Tiantong,
both descendants of Shitou—thus they seem to oppose each other. How will you reconcile them? Linji also
said, "On the red lump of flesh there is a true person of no rank." But Tiantong teaches to cast away the red
lump of flesh. Tell me then: where does the true person of no rank settle body and establish life? Master Fori
said, "Before this mountain monk came, the people of Yanjing had crooked noses. This mountain monk
came specially to set them straight." Wansong said: Fori's nostrils fell into the hands of the people of Yanjing.
A monk asked Xiangyan, "What is the Way?" Yan said, "A dragon's cry within a dead tree."
The monk said, "I do not understand." Yan said, "Eyes in a skull." Later a monk asked Shishuang,
"What is a dragon's cry within a dead tree?" Shuang said, "It still carries joy." The monk
said, "What are eyes in a skull?" Shuang said, "It still carries consciousness." Again there was
a monk who asked Caoshan. Shan answered with a verse: "The dragon's cry in the dead tree truly sees the Way.
When consciousness in the skull is exhausted, then the eyes first become bright. When joy and consciousness are exhausted, all tidings are exhausted. How can the very person
discern the clear within the turbid?" The monk asked again, "What is a dragon's cry within a dead tree?" Cao said,
"The bloodline is unbroken." The monk said, "What are eyes in a skull?" Cao said, "Dry—
not exhausted." In the Zuting Shiyuan it is written "dried up entirely." But according to Tiantong's verse and preface, the red
lump of flesh stands alone revealing true constancy; the skull-eyes drain dry the leaking consciousness. "Dried up entirely" is also not
bad. A worldly proverb says: "Old doctor, young diviner"—meaning medicine becomes clear only when old, while divination is efficacious when young.
Therefore Dongshan, old, could make "seeing no sickness"; while for the young one looking on, drawing near
is difficult. The moment you intend intimacy, you are estranged; the moment you intend approach, you go astray. The cold pine's diseased branch, through illness, turns
wondrous. Vimalakirti was thin but not wasted, because through sickness he daily diminished for the Way. In Xijing,
at Fengsheng, Chan master Shen, rising from illness, made a verse saying: "Breath cut off, emotions cut off; when intention is raised, there is no
road of intent. Even blinking lacks strength. For years I do not leave the door." Master Furong Kai
said: "With just this one verse, he naturally succeeds this old monk." These thin waters, receding floods, broken clouds,
and cold mountain—these too must be cut off utterly. Do not be muddled. Illness must be uprooted; medicine must
be no-medicine. Turn it all the way through with no merit, and he takes his place. The solitary standard does not share your tray. Do you still
know? In Lishui, a single spark of gold cannot be mixed with drifting sand.

cb:div.head: Case Ninety-Five: Linji's One Stroke
cb:div.p:
Addressing the assembly, he said: If Buddha comes, hit; if Mara comes, hit. With reason, thirty blows; without reason,
thirty blows. Is this mistaking and taking enemies for kin, or is it not distinguishing good and bad? Try
and speak—let us see.
